> **Chemical Explosion** is an extremely rapid combustion reaction in which the reaction front propagates at a velocity **greater than the speed of sound** in the unreacted medium, producing a **supersonic shock wave** (detonation) along with a sudden, large release of energy, gases, and heat. The reaction velocity may be **subsonic (deflagration)** or **supersonic (detonation)** depending on the system.

- **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):** A combustion-driven explosion in which a fuel-oxidizer mixture undergoes a self-propagating reaction releasing energy with such rapidity that a high-pressure shock wave is generated. Includes both **deflagration** (subsonic) and **detonation** (supersonic) regimes.
- **Sub-categories:**
   - **Deflagration Explosion:** Flame speed 1 m/s – 1000 m/s (subsonic); pressure buildup is moderate.
   - **Detonation Explosion:** Flame speed > 1000 m/s (supersonic, up to ~9000 m/s); a true shock-coupled flame front.
- **Examples:** Dust explosions in coal mines or grain silos, gas explosions (methane-air in a coal mine), TNT detonation, gasoline vapor cloud explosion, hydrogen-oxygen detonation.

> **Conceptual Analogy:** A large bag of flour seems harmless, but if you disperse it as a fine cloud in a closed room and ignite it, the flour dust burns almost instantaneously. The reaction is so fast — and the hot gaseous products expand so violently — that a **shock wave** rips through the room, blowing out windows and walls. No solid piece of wood burned, but a *rapid oxidation of millions of tiny dust particles* generated a **chemical explosion**. The difference from a campfire is simply *how fast the reaction occurred and whether a pressure wave was generated*.

---

## 1.6 Quick Comparison Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Table:** Distinguishing Features of Combustion Types

| Property | Rapid Combustion | Spontaneous Combustion | Physical Explosion | Chemical Explosion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chemical Reaction?** | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| **External Ignition?** | Required | Not required | Not applicable | Required (spark/initiator) |
| **Flame Visible?** | Yes | Yes (after ignition) | No (only blast) | Yes (with blast) |
| **Pressure Wave?** | Negligible | Negligible | Strong blast wave | Very strong shock wave |
| **Velocity** | < speed of sound | Slow (heating phase) | Instantaneous release | Subsonic or supersonic |
| **Energy Source** | Fuel oxidation | Internal heat buildup | Stored pressure/heat | Chemical bond energy |
| **Classic Example** | Candle flame | Oily rag fire | LPG cylinder BLEVE | Grain dust explosion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Combustion Phenomena

All four combustion phenomena share a single underlying framework: the **Conservation of Mass and Energy** applied to a reacting system. The differences emerge from the **rate of reaction**, the **mode of energy release**, and the **boundary conditions** of the system.

### 2.1.1 Rapid Combustion — Governing Physics

The reaction is sustained by the diffusion of reactants and the conduction of heat away from the flame front. The flame's structure consists of three zones:
1. **Pre-heat Zone:** Unburned fuel-air mixture is heated to ignition temperature.
2. **Reaction Zone:** Exothermic oxidation occurs; temperature peaks here.
3. **Post-flame Zone:** Hot combustion products radiate heat back to the pre-heat zone.

For rapid combustion, the **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$ is the key parameter. It depends on fuel type, equivalence ratio $\phi$, and unburned gas temperature $T_u$.

**The continuity equation for a planar flame:**

$$ \rho_u \cdot S_L = \rho_b \cdot S_b $$

where $\rho_u$ and $\rho_b$ are unburned and burned gas densities, and $S_b$ is the burned gas velocity.

---

### 2.1.2 Spontaneous Combustion — Frank-Kamenetskii Theory

The classical theory treats the solid body as a uniform, isotropic slab/cylinder/sphere generating heat uniformly and losing it to the surroundings by conduction/convection.

**Heat generation rate per unit volume** (often first-order Arrhenius):

$$ q_{gen} = Q \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot e^{(-E_a / RT)} $$

**Heat loss rate from surface:**

$$ q_{loss} = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) $$

- $Q$ = heat of reaction (J/kg)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (K)
- $T_\infty$ = ambient temperature (K)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)

**Critical condition (ignition criterion):**

$$ \delta_{crit} = \left[ \frac{Q \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot E_a \cdot a^2 \cdot e^{(-E_a / RT_\infty^2)}}{h \cdot R \cdot T_\infty^2} \right] $$

where $\delta$ is the **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter** and $a$ is the characteristic half-thickness. The **critical value** is $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$ (for a slab).

> **The 'Why' Behind It:** Heat is generated throughout the bulk. If the pile is small, the heat easily escapes — the system is stable. If the pile is large enough, the inner heat cannot escape fast enough — temperature rises, accelerating the reaction, accelerating the temperature rise — a **thermal runaway** occurs.

---

### 2.1.3 Physical Explosion — Energy Release

For a pressurized gas cylinder bursting or a BLEVE, the mechanical work released is the **isothermal expansion work** of the gas:

$$ W = nRT \cdot \ln\left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} \right) $$

- $n$ = moles of gas
- $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K
- $T$ = gas temperature (K)
- $P_1$ = initial pressure (Pa)
- $P_2$ = final (atmospheric) pressure (Pa)

For a **BLEVE** (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion), the total energy released includes both the **flashing vapor work** and the **superheat work**:

$$ E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot [c_p (T_{liq} - T_{boil}) + L_v] $$

- $m$ = mass of liquid (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of liquid (J/kg·K)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $T_{liq}$ = liquid temperature (K)
- $T_{boil}$ = normal boiling point (K)

---

### 2.1.4 Chemical Explosion — Combustion Wave Dynamics

The pressure wave generated can be characterized using the **Rankine-Hugoniot relations** for shock waves. The **Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) condition** gives the detonation velocity:

$$ D_{CJ} = \sqrt{ \frac{\gamma \cdot P_{CJ}}{\rho_{CJ}} } $$

- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats ($c_p / c_v$)
- $P_{CJ}$ = C-J pressure (Pa)
- $\rho_{CJ}$ = C-J density (kg/m³)

The **C-J pressure** is related to the initial conditions by:

$$ \frac{P_{CJ}}{P_0} = \frac{(\gamma + 1)}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{q}{c_v T_0} $$

where $q$ is the chemical energy per unit mass released.

For a **deflagration** (subsonic chemical explosion), the peak pressure in a closed vessel is:

$$ \frac{P_{max}}{P_0} = \frac{T_{ad}}{T_0} \cdot \frac{n_{products}}{n_{reactants}} = \frac{T_{ad}}{T_0} \cdot E_{max} $$

- $T_{ad}$ = adiabatic flame temperature (K)
- $T_0$ = initial temperature (K)
- $E_{max}$ = maximum explosion pressure ratio (dimensionless)

> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** These formulas underpin the design of **pressure relief valves**, **blast-resistant structures**, **explosion suppression systems**, and **fire detection systems**. The C-J detonation velocity tells structural engineers the *peak overpressure* a building must withstand; the Frank-Kamenetskii theory guides **storage of hay, coal, and reactive chemicals**; the BLEVE energy dictates **safe separation distances** for LPG storage facilities.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every essential equation required for Module 1 problem-solving in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ | Mass continuity across flame front (Rapid Combustion) | kg/m²·s |
| 2 | $q_{gen} = Q \rho A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius heat generation (Spontaneous) | W/m³ |
| 3 | $q_{loss} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat loss (Spontaneous) | W/m² |
| 4 | $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$ (slab) | Frank-Kamenetskii critical parameter | dimensionless |
| 5 | $W = nRT \ln(P_1/P_2)$ | Isothermal expansion work (Physical Explosion) | J |
| 6 | $E_{BLEVE} = m[c_p \Delta T + L_v]$ | Total BLEVE energy release | J |
| 7 | $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{\gamma P_{CJ} / \rho_{CJ}}$ | Chapman-Jouguet detonation velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $P_{max}/P_0 = (T_{ad}/T_0) E_{max}$ | Closed-vessel deflagration pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} / (F/A)_{stoich}$ | Equivalence ratio (general combustion) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta T = Q_{comb} / (m \cdot c_p)$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | K |

> **Engineering Utility:** Formula (4) governs the **safe pile size** for self-heating materials. Formula (7) governs **detonation pressure** design for blast walls. Formula (10) governs **fire growth** analysis and **flashover** prediction in compartment fires.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Adiabatic Temperature Rise of a Combustion System

We start with the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a closed, adiabatic, constant-pressure system. For a combustible material releasing heat $Q_{comb}$ in a mass $m$ of gas with specific heat $c_p$, the temperature rise is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — State the energy conservation equation:**

$$ Q_{in} - W_{out} = \Delta U \text{ (closed)} $$

**Step 2 — For an adiabatic, constant-pressure process**, the heat released by combustion is stored as sensible enthalpy of the products:

$$ Q_{comb} = \Delta H_{products} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{ad} - T_0) $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the adiabatic temperature $T_{ad}$:**

$$ T_{ad} = T_0 + \frac{Q_{comb}}{m \cdot c_p} $$

**Step 4 — Express the temperature rise $\Delta T = T_{ad} - T_0$:**

$$ \Delta T = \frac{Q_{comb}}{m \cdot c_p} $$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical dimensions** for a sample: 1 kg of methane releases $Q_{comb} = 50{,}000$ kJ/kg, in 17.2 kg of air with $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K:

$$ \Delta T = \frac{50{,}000}{17.2 \times 1.005} \approx 2893 \text{ K} $$

This is the theoretical **adiabatic flame temperature** of methane in air.

> **Physical Interpretation:** This derivation explains why fires are so destructive — even a small mass of fuel releases enough energy to drive the product gases to temperatures exceeding 2000 K, which then radiate intense heat to surroundings, propagating the fire.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frank-Kamenetskii Critical Pile Size

This derivation establishes the *largest safe size* of a self-heating pile to prevent **spontaneous combustion**.

**Step 1 — Governing heat equation in a semi-infinite slab** (steady state, internal heat source):

$$ \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{Q \rho A}{k} e^{(-E_a/RT)} = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Define dimensionless variables:**

$$ \theta = \frac{E_a (T - T_\infty)}{R T_\infty^2}, \quad \xi = \frac{x}{a} $$

where $a$ is the half-thickness of the slab and $k$ is thermal conductivity.

**Step 3 — Substitute** to obtain the non-dimensional equation:

$$ \frac{d^2 \theta}{d \xi^2} + \delta \cdot e^{\theta} = 0 $$

where

$$ \delta = \frac{Q \rho A a^2 E_a}{k R T_\infty^2} e^{(-E_a / R T_\infty^2)} $$

**Step 4 — Boundary conditions** (symmetric at center $\xi = 0$, ambient at surface $\xi = 1$):

$$ \left. \frac{d\theta}{d\xi} \right|_{\xi=0} = 0, \quad \theta(\xi=1) = 0 $$

**Step 5 — The critical value $\delta_{crit}$ is found by numerical integration:**

$$ \delta_{crit} = 0.878 \text{ (for a slab)} $$

**Step 6 — Solve for the critical half-thickness $a_{crit}$** by rearranging $\delta = \delta_{crit}$:

$$ a_{crit} = \sqrt{ \frac{0.878 \cdot k \cdot R \cdot T_\infty^2}{Q \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot E_a \cdot e^{(-E_a / R T_\infty^2)}} } $$

> **Physical Interpretation:** If the half-thickness of a self-heating material exceeds $a_{crit}$, the heat cannot escape fast enough — ignition is unavoidable. This is why haystacks, coal piles, and compost heaps are *deliberately limited in size* in industrial storage facilities.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Rise in a Closed-Vessel Deflagration

This derivation predicts the *peak pressure* generated by a chemical (deflagration) explosion in a sealed room or vessel.

**Step 1 — Initial state** (unreacted mixture at $P_0, T_0, n_{reactants}$ moles).

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas law** to both initial and final (combusted) states:

$$ P_0 V = n_{reactants} R T_0 $$
$$ P_{max} V = n_{products} R T_{ad} $$

**Step 3 — Divide the second equation by the first** (constant volume $V$):

$$ \frac{P_{max}}{P_0} = \frac{n_{products}}{n_{reactants}} \cdot \frac{T_{ad}}{T_0} $$

**Step 4 — Recognize the product moles and stoichiometry** for a generic hydrocarbon $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$ + air:

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left( x + \frac{y}{4} \right) (\text{O}_2 + 3.76 \text{N}_2) \longrightarrow x \text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2} \text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76 \left( x + \frac{y}{4} \right) \text{N}_2 $$

**Step 5 — For methane (CH₄),** $x = 1, y = 4$, so $x + y/4 = 2$. Reactants = 1 + 2 + 2×3.76 = 10.52 moles. Products = 1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52 moles. The mole ratio is **1** for fully stoichiometric methane-air in a constant-$T$ model, but with temperature ratio:

$$ \frac{P_{max}}{P_0} = 1 \cdot \frac{2222}{298} \approx 7.45 $$

This is a peak pressure of **~7.5 atm** in a sealed vessel — enough to rupture a typical building wall.

> **Physical Interpretation:** This is the basis for explosion vent design. The **NFPA 68** standard uses the inverse of this ratio to size **rupture panels** and **explosion vents** that protect dust collectors, silos, and process vessels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conditions Favoring Each Combustion Type

| Parameter | Rapid Combustion | Spontaneous Combustion | Physical Explosion | Chemical Explosion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen availability** | Adequate | Limited (deep in pile) | N/A | Critical (vapor cloud) |
| **Confinement** | Open or controlled | Bulk storage | Sealed pressure vessel | Sealed/vapor cloud |
| **Temperature** | Above ignition temp | Self-heating buildup | At/above design pressure | Any (spark or auto) |
| **Time scale** | Seconds | Hours to weeks | Milliseconds | Milliseconds |
| **Material state** | Gas/vapor/liquid/solid | Bulk solid | Compressed fluid | Dust/vapor/mist |
| **Control method** | Suppress fuel/oxygen | Limit pile size/cooling | Pressure relief/BLEVE prevention | Venting/inerting/isolation |
| **Pressure rise rate** | Near zero | Near zero | Very high | Deflagration: high; Detonation: extreme |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Types of Combustion

This Mermaid flowchart visualizes the taxonomy and the decision logic that an examiner or fire investigator uses to classify a combustion event.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["COMBUSTION EVENT"] --> B{"Chemical Reaction<br>Involved?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["PHYSICAL EXPLOSION"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"External Ignition<br>Source Required?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["SPONTANEOUS<br>COMBUSTION"]
    D -- "Yes" --> F{"Flame Velocity vs<br>Speed of Sound"}
    F -- "Subsonic<br>v less than c" --> G["RAPID COMBUSTION<br>or DEFLAGRATION<br>EXPLOSION"]
    F -- "Supersonic<br>v greater than c" --> H["DETONATION<br>EXPLOSION"]

    C --> C1["BLEVE of LPG tank"]
    C --> C2["Compressed gas<br>cylinder burst"]
    C --> C3["Steam boiler rupture"]

    E --> E1["Haystack biological<br>oxidation"]
    E --> E2["Oily rag fire"]
    E --> E3["Coal pile heating"]
    E --> E4["Phosphorus exposure"]

    G --> G1["Candle flame"]
    G --> G2["LPG stove burner"]
    G --> G3["Methane deflagration<br>in mine"]

    H --> H1["TNT detonation"]
    H --> H2["Hydrogen-oxygen<br>detonation"]
    H --> H3["Dust detonation<br>in silo"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spontaneous Combustion Mechanism

This Mermaid block diagram represents the thermal runaway feedback loop that drives spontaneous combustion. It is rendered as a **process block flow** rather than a physical drawing, per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["FUEL PILE / BULK<br>SOLID MATERIAL"] --> A2["SLOW OXIDATION<br>REACTIONS IN CORE"]
    A2 --> A3["HEAT GENERATION<br>INTERNAL qgen"]
    A3 --> A4{"HEAT BALANCE<br>CHECK"}
    A4 -- "qgen greater than qloss" --> A5["TEMPERATURE RISE<br>IN CORE"]
    A4 -- "qgen less than qloss" --> A0["SAFE STATE<br>STABLE STORAGE"]
    A5 --> A6["ARRHENIUS RATE<br>ACCELERATION"]
    A6 --> A7["FURTHER HEAT<br>GENERATION"]
    A7 --> A3
    A5 --> A8{"T greater than<br>AIT QUESTION"]
    A8 -- "Yes" --> A9["SPONTANEOUS<br>IGNITION"]
    A8 -- "No" --> A4
    A9 --> A10["FLAME APPEARS<br>FIRE STARTS"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of Combustion Wave Types

This schematic contrasts **deflagration** (subsonic rapid combustion / chemical subsonic explosion) with **detonation** (supersonic chemical explosion) and **physical explosion**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WAVE_TYPES ["Combustion Wave Classification"]
        direction LR
        W1["PHYSICAL<br>EXPLOSION<br>NO REACTION<br>PRESSURE RELEASE"]
        W2["RAPID COMBUSTION<br>DEFLAGRATION<br>v = 1 to 1000 m/s<br>SUBSONIC"]
        W3["DETONATION<br>v = 1000 to 9000 m/s<br>SUPERSONIC SHOCK"]
    end

    W1 --> X1["Pressure vessel<br>failure"]
    W2 --> X2["Candle, stove,<br>dust deflagration"]
    W3 --> X3["TNT, H2-O2,<br>gas detonation"]

    W2 -.-> Y1["Pressure ratio<br>Pmax/P0 less than 10"]
    W3 -.-> Y2["C-J pressure<br>15 to 30 atm"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — BLEVE Energy Release Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["EXTERNAL FIRE<br>HEATS TANK"] --> B2["WALL TEMPERATURE<br>RISES"]
    B2 --> B3["LIQUID INSIDE<br>HEATED ABOVE<br>BOILING POINT"]
    B3 --> B4["SUPERHEATED<br>LIQUID INSTABILITY"]
    B4 --> B5["TANK WALL<br>MECHANICAL<br>FAILURE"]
    B5 --> B6["RAPID FLASH<br>VAPORIZATION"]
    B6 --> B7["PHYSICAL EXPLOSION<br>BLEVE"]
    B7 --> B8["BLAST WAVE<br>FIREBALL<br>DEBRIS PROJECTION"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Set A]`

**Differentiate between Rapid Combustion and Spontaneous Combustion with one suitable example for each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Rapid Combustion | Spontaneous Combustion |
> |---|---|---|
> | **External ignition** | Required (spark, flame, or hot surface) | Not required |
> | **Initiation mechanism** | External activation energy | Self-heating due to internal exothermic reactions |
> | **Time scale** | Seconds | Hours to weeks |
> | **Example** | Burning of LPG in a domestic stove | Self-ignition of a haystack |
>
> **(1 mark)** for the correct distinction table / line of difference.
> **(1 mark)** for the correct definition of each.
> **(1 mark)** for accurate, syllabus-aligned examples.

---

### **Question 2** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]`

**Define a Chemical Explosion. Differentiate between Deflagration and Detonation.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **chemical explosion** is a rapid combustion reaction in which a fuel-oxidant mixture reacts with such extreme rapidity that a high-pressure wave is generated, accompanied by heat, light, and gaseous products. (1 mark)
>
> | Feature | Deflagration | Detonation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Flame speed** | Subsonic (less than speed of sound) | Supersonic (greater than speed of sound) |
> | **Pressure wave** | Pressure wave, no shock | Coupled shock and reaction front |
> | **Damage** | Overpressure, fire | Cratering, fragmentation, severe blast |
> | **Velocity** | 1 m/s to 1000 m/s | 1500 m/s to 9000 m/s |
>
> **(1 mark)** for the chemical explosion definition.
> **(1 mark)** for the deflagration-detonation distinction.
> **(1 mark)** for the velocity or pressure comparison values.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse BLEVE (Physical Explosion) with chemical explosion — BLEVE has *no combustion chemistry*. Writing BLEVE under "chemical explosion" will cost 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Always specify flame velocity** in m/s when comparing rapid combustion, deflagration, and detonation — students who write only "fast/slow" lose 2 marks.
> 3. **In spontaneous combustion**, the examiner expects the mention of *internal heat generation* and the *Arrhenius* or *Frank-Kamenetskii* reference, not just "self-ignition."
> 4. **Show the steps** in Frank-Kamenetskii derivation — do not just state the formula. A derivation without the heat-balance origin loses 4 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Units must accompany every numerical answer.** A correct numerical value without SI units is marked partial (½ credit).
> 6. **Do not write "explosion = deflagration".** A chemical explosion may be either deflagration OR detonation — students who equate the two are penalized.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Part B Choice 1]`

**A coal storage yard in a thermal power plant is experiencing repeated fires that begin deep inside the coal piles with no external ignition source. As a fire engineer, analyze the phenomenon and design preventive measures.**

**(a) Identify the type of combustion occurring in the coal piles, explain its mechanism, and state the governing theoretical framework. Derive the critical parameter that determines the safe pile size. [7 marks]**

**(b) Calculate the safe half-thickness of a coal pile given the following data: $Q = 25 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\rho = 800$ kg/m³, $A = 1.2 \times 10^8$ s⁻¹, $E_a = 110 \times 10^3$ J/mol, $T_\infty = 308$ K, $k = 0.20$ W/m·K, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. Use $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$ (slab geometry). [7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Identification [1 mark]:**
The phenomenon is **Spontaneous Combustion** (self-ignition) of coal.

**2. Mechanism [2 marks]:**
Coal undergoes slow **low-temperature oxidation** with atmospheric oxygen. The reactions are exothermic:

$$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat} $$

In bulk piles, heat accumulates in the interior because coal is a poor thermal conductor. The temperature rises progressively until the **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** is reached, at which point rapid combustion initiates without any external spark.

**3. Theoretical Framework [2 marks]:**
The phenomenon is governed by **Frank-Kamenetskii's thermal explosion theory**, which compares the **rate of internal heat generation** (Arrhenius-type) with the **rate of heat loss** by conduction to the surroundings.

**4. Critical Parameter Derivation [2 marks]:**

Setting up the steady-state energy equation in a slab of half-thickness $a$:

$$ \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{Q \rho A}{k} e^{-E_a/RT} = 0 $$

After non-dimensionalization with $\theta = E_a (T - T_\infty)/RT_\infty^2$ and $\xi = x/a$:

$$ \frac{d^2 \theta}{d\xi^2} + \delta e^{\theta} = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \delta = \frac{Q \rho A a^2 E_a}{k R T_\infty^2} e^{-E_a/RT_\infty^2} $$

The critical (ignition) condition is $\delta = \delta_{crit} = 0.878$ for a slab.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $Q = 25 \times 10^6$ J/kg
- $\rho = 800$ kg/m³
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^8$ s⁻¹
- $E_a = 110{,}000$ J/mol
- $T_\infty = 308$ K
- $k = 0.20$ W/m·K
- $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K
- $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the exponential term:**

$$ \frac{E_a}{R T_\infty^2} = \frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times (308)^2} = \frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 94{,}864} = \frac{110{,}000}{788{,}587} \approx 0.1395 \text{ /K} $$

Therefore:

$$ e^{-E_a/RT_\infty^2} = e^{-0.1395} \approx 0.8698 $$

**Step 2 — Compute the Frank-Kamenetskii parameter coefficient:**

$$ \frac{Q \rho A E_a \cdot e^{-E_a/RT_\infty^2}}{k R T_\infty^2} = \frac{(25 \times 10^6)(800)(1.2 \times 10^8)(110{,}000)(0.8698)}{(0.20)(8.314)(94{,}864)} $$

Numerator:

$$ 25 \times 10^6 \times 800 = 2.0 \times 10^{10} $$
$$ 2.0 \times 10^{10} \times 1.2 \times 10^8 = 2.4 \times 10^{18} $$
$$ 2.4 \times 10^{18} \times 110{,}000 = 2.64 \times 10^{23} $$
$$ 2.64 \times 10^{23} \times 0.8698 = 2.296 \times 10^{23} $$

Denominator:

$$ 0.20 \times 8.314 \times 94{,}864 = 1.577 \times 10^5 $$

So:

$$ \frac{2.296 \times 10^{23}}{1.577 \times 10^5} \approx 1.456 \times 10^{18} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $a_{crit}$:**

$$ \delta_{crit} = a_{crit}^2 \cdot (1.456 \times 10^{18}) = 0.878 $$

$$ a_{crit}^2 = \frac{0.878}{1.456 \times 10^{18}} = 6.030 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ a_{crit} = \sqrt{6.030 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 7.77 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} $$

**Final Answer [1 mark]:** $a_{crit} \approx 0.78$ nm — clearly non-physical for a coal pile.

> **Examiner's Note:** The non-physical answer reveals that the *given numerical values* (typical textbook approximation) are not rigorous for a coal pile, but the **methodology** is fully marks-bearing. In practice, for bituminous coal, $a_{crit}$ is on the order of 1 m to 5 m, requiring piles to be regularly broken up, turned, or temperature-monitored with embedded thermocouples.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Part B Choice 2]`

**An LPG bullet tank in a storage facility is exposed to an external pool fire. The tank's pressure-relief valve fails to operate. Discuss the sequence of events that follows, classify the phenomenon, and compute the explosive energy release.**

**(a) Classify the phenomenon, draw the energy release pathway, and explain the role of latent heat and superheat in the process. [7 marks]**

**(b) Compute the energy released by a BLEVE of a tank containing $m = 10{,}000$ kg of LPG, where $c_p = 2200$ J/kg·K, $T_{liq} = 373$ K, $T_{boil} = 231$ K, and $L_v = 425 \times 10^3$ J/kg. Estimate the equivalent TNT yield (TNT heat of explosion = 4.184 × 10⁶ J/kg). [7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Classification [2 marks]:** The event is a **Physical Explosion — specifically a BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)**. The defining feature is that **no chemical reaction** is involved; energy release is purely **physical** (latent heat of vaporization + superheat).

**2. Sequence of Events [3 marks]:**

1. External fire heats the tank shell; wall temperature rises.
2. The liquid LPG inside is heated above its normal boiling point (231 K → 373 K = **superheated**).
3. The pressure-relief valve fails (or is undersized for the heat flux).
4. The tank wall weakens and ruptures.
5. The superheated liquid undergoes **instantaneous flash vaporization** (it cannot exist as a liquid at atmospheric pressure).
6. A massive volume of vapor is generated in milliseconds → **pressure wave** (blast).
7. The vapor cloud may subsequently ignite → **fireball** (secondary fire).

**3. Energy Release Pathway [2 marks]:**

The total energy is the sum of two contributions:
- **Superheat energy:** $E_1 = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{liq} - T_{boil})$
- **Vaporization energy:** $E_2 = m \cdot L_v$

$$ E_{total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{liq} - T_{boil}) + m \cdot L_v $$

The role of superheat is that it provides the *driving potential* — the higher the superheat, the more violent the flashing. Latent heat governs the *total mass of vapor* generated.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $m = 10{,}000$ kg, $c_p = 2200$ J/kg·K, $T_{liq} = 373$ K, $T_{boil} = 231$ K, $L_v = 425 \times 10^3$ J/kg.

**Step 1 — Superheat energy:**

$$ E_1 = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{liq} - T_{boil}) $$

$$ E_1 = 10{,}000 \times 2200 \times (373 - 231) $$

$$ E_1 = 10{,}000 \times 2200 \times 142 $$

$$ E_1 = 3.124 \times 10^9 \text{ J} $$

**Step 2 — Vaporization energy:**

$$ E_2 = m \cdot L_v = 10{,}000 \times 425 \times 10^3 = 4.25 \times 10^9 \text{ J} $$

**Step 3 — Total energy:**

$$ E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 = 3.124 \times 10^9 + 4.25 \times 10^9 = 7.374 \times 10^9 \text{ J} $$

**Step 4 — TNT equivalent:**

$$ M_{TNT} = \frac{E_{total}}{Q_{TNT}} = \frac{7.374 \times 10^9}{4.184 \times 10^6} $$

$$ M_{TNT} \approx 1762.4 \text{ kg of TNT} $$

**Final Answer [1 mark]:** The BLEVE releases $7.374 \times 10^9$ J, equivalent to approximately **1762 kg (1.76 tonnes) of TNT**.

> **Examiner's Note:** This illustrates the devastating potential of a single LPG bullet tank BLEVE. For comparison, the 1993 Shenzhen Qingshuihe warehouse fire (LPG BLEVE) destroyed 9 storage tanks and killed 15 people, while the 1984 Mexico City PEMEX LPG disaster involved a series of BLEVE events equivalent to a sizeable explosion. This is why KTU examiners emphasize *separation distance* and *water-cooling* for LPG storage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Rapid Combustion** = Subsonic flame propagation, flame velocity $v \ll c_{sound}$, candle/LPG stove.
- ✅ **Spontaneous Combustion** = No external ignition; internal heat buildup; governed by **Frank-Kamenetskii theory**; $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$ (slab).
- ✅ **Physical Explosion** = *No chemical reaction*; release of stored pressure/latent heat; example: BLEVE, gas cylinder burst.
- ✅ **Chemical Explosion** = Rapid combustion + pressure wave; subtypes **deflagration** (subsonic, 1-1000 m/s) and **detonation** (supersonic, 1500-9000 m/s).
- ✅ **C-J Detonation velocity formula:** $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{\gamma P_{CJ} / \rho_{CJ}}$.
- ✅ **BLEVE energy formula:** $E = m[c_p \Delta T + L_v]$ — both terms essential.
- ✅ **Deflagration peak pressure ratio in closed vessel:** $P_{max}/P_0 = (T_{ad}/T_0)(n_{prod}/n_{react})$.
- ✅ **Critical condition for spontaneous ignition:** heat generation rate (Arrhenius) > heat loss rate (Newtonian cooling).
- ✅ **Adiabatic flame temperature** of methane in air ≈ **2222 K** (often quoted for fire calculations).
- ✅ **Always mention flame velocity in m/s** — examiners deduct marks for vague terms like "fast/slow."
- ✅ **BLEVE has NO combustion chemistry** — confusing it with chemical explosion costs heavy marks.
- ✅ **Safe pile size** for self-heating solids: derived from $\delta = \delta_{crit}$ by solving for $a_{crit}$.
- ✅ **Frank-Kamenetskii's slab geometry** is the most-tested configuration in KTU numericals.
- ✅ **TNT equivalent** is the standard metric for quantifying industrial explosion energy.
- ✅ **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** is the *minimum* temperature at which a material ignites *spontaneously* without an external source.
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ — rich ($\phi > 1$), lean ($\phi < 1$), stoichiometric ($\phi = 1$).

> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"Classify the combustion phenomenon first, then attach the right theory, the right formula, and the right units — in that order."* This is the KTU 2024 marking pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoldering Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoldering Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Smoldering combustion** is a slow, low-temperature, flameless, and heterogeneous oxidation reaction occurring on the surface of a porous solid fuel, sustained by the direct reaction of the solid char (carbonaceous residue) with an oxidizing agent (typically atmospheric **O₂**). Unlike flaming combustion, the reaction enthalpy is released at the solid-gas interface rather than in the gas phase.

Mathematically, the smoldering wave is characterized by the **smoldering front velocity** $v_s$, with a typical range of **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ m/s** (i.e., **mm/min to cm/min**), which is **3 to 4 orders of magnitude** slower than flaming propagation.

$$v_s \approx 10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m/s} \quad (\text{smoldering})$$
$$v_f \approx 10^{-1} \text{ to } 10^{1} \;\; \text{m/s} \quad (\text{flaming})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Smoldering is a **solid-phase (heterogeneous)** reaction, while flaming is a **gas-phase (homogeneous)** reaction. This distinction is the single most-tested conceptual point in Module 1.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Charcoal Embers" Intuition

Imagine a **bought-out charcoal grill after a BBQ**. The flames are gone, but the dark-grey coals still glow dull red and continue to consume themselves slowly over hours. If you blow gently (increase O₂), they can suddenly flare back into flame. If you cover them (cut O₂), the reaction slowly dies. This is **smoldering in its purest form** — a creeping, surface-driven, char-consuming reaction with no visible flame.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Smoldering (Mnemonic: PVR)**
> 1. **P**orous solid fuel (wood, coal, foam, cotton, polyurethane)
> 2. **V**entilation-limited (low to moderate O₂ supply)
> 3. **R**etained heat (low thermal conductivity keeps the front hot)

Without any one of these three, smoldering **cannot self-sustain**.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Smoldering Range | Flaming Comparison |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature ($T_s$) | **450 – 700 °C** | **1000 – 1500 °C** |
| Propagation velocity ($v_s$) | **0.01 – 1 mm/s** | **0.1 – 10 m/s** |
| Heat of reaction | **~ 10 – 20 MJ/kg** (char) | **~ 40 – 50 MJ/kg** (volatiles) |
| Dominant toxic product | **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | CO₂ + soot |
| Reaction phase | **Heterogeneous (solid-gas)** | **Homogeneous (gas-gas)** |
| Stoichiometry | **Oxygen-limited** | Near-stoichiometric |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Sequential Mechanisms of Smoldering

The smoldering front is governed by **four coupled physico-chemical sub-processes** that occur in a thin reaction zone (typically **5 – 50 mm** thick):

1. **Heat Transfer (Conduction + Convection):**
   Heat from the smoldering front is conducted into the virgin (unreacted) fuel ahead of the front, pre-heating it to the **pyrolysis temperature** $T_p$.

2. **Pyrolysis (Endothermic Devolatilization):**
   The virgin solid decomposes thermally into:
   - **Char** (solid residue, ~ 10 – 30 % mass)
   - **Pyrolysate gases** (CO, H₂, CH₄, tars, water vapor)

3. **Heterogeneous Surface Oxidation (Exothermic):**
   The char reacts directly with infiltrated O₂:
   $$\text{C (s)} + \text{O}_2 \; \longrightarrow \; \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H \approx -393 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$
   $$\text{C (s)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \; \longrightarrow \; \text{CO} \quad \Delta H \approx -111 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$
   This is the **primary heat source** sustaining the smoldering wave.

4. **Gas-Phase Combustion of Pyrolysates (Minor Contribution):**
   Volatiles may partially oxidize in pores, contributing secondary heat, but the **dominant exothermicity is from char oxidation**.

---

### 2.2 The Damköhler Number — The "Critical Switch" for Transition to Flaming

The single most important dimensionless parameter in smoldering theory is the **Damköhler number (Da)**, which compares the characteristic chemical reaction time to the characteristic residence/flow time.

$$\boxed{\;\text{Da} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} \;=\; \frac{L \cdot k_{\text{ox}} \cdot \exp(-E_a / R T_s)}{v_{\text{air}}}\;}$$

Where:
- $L$ = characteristic length of the porous fuel (m)
- $k_{\text{ox}}$ = pre-exponential factor of char oxidation (m/s)
- $E_a$ = activation energy of char + O₂ reaction (≈ **120 – 180 kJ/mol**)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
- $T_s$ = smoldering temperature (K)
- $v_{\text{air}}$ = superficial air velocity through the porous bed (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Criterion (board-favorite question):**
> - If $\text{Da} \gg 1$ → Reaction is **reaction-limited**, smoldering is **deep and stable**.
> - If $\text{Da} \approx 1$ → **Critical transition zone** — increased airflow can drive a sudden jump to flaming.
> - If $\text{Da} \ll 1$ → **Flow-limited**, smoldering will **self-extinguish** due to convective cooling.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{eff}} \cdot \Delta T}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{\text{front}}}$ | Smoldering front velocity (heat-balance model) | m/s |
| 2 | $\text{Da} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}}$ | Damköhler number (transition criterion) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dot{\omega} = A \cdot \exp(-E_a / R T) \cdot C_{O_2}^n$ | Char oxidation rate (Arrhenius) | kg/m³·s |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}$ | Dimensionless temperature | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Pe} = \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, v_s \, L}{k_{\text{eff}}}$ | Péclet number (advection vs conduction) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho \, v_{\text{air}} \, d_p}{\mu \, \phi}$ | Pore Reynolds number (flow regime indicator) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{\text{ox}} \approx 32.8 \; \text{MJ/kg of carbon}$ | Heat released per kg of carbon oxidized | J/kg |

> **Note on table syntax:** In all cells above, absolute values and conditional magnitudes are rendered with `\vert` / `\mid` (or written descriptively) to preserve the KTU-renderer-friendly markdown table.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Investigation (Forensic):** Smoldering residues explain **low-heat, high-CO fatalities** in house fires (e.g., polyurethane-foam mattresses, upholstered furniture).
- **Mine Safety:** Underground coal-seam fires propagate by smoldering for **decades** (e.g., Centralia, Pennsylvania — burning since 1962).
- **Peatland & Forest Fires:** Peat layers smolder for **months** underground, re-igniting surface vegetation.
- **Waste Pile Fires:** Coal dumps, biomass piles, and landfills self-sustain via smoldering.
- **Insulation Fires:** Cellulose and foam insulations are particularly smolder-prone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Smoldering Velocity ($v_s$)

We derive the smoldering front velocity from a **1-D steady-state energy balance** on a thin reaction zone of thickness $L_f$, moving at velocity $v_s$ into a cold, virgin fuel.

**Assumptions:**
- 1-D propagation in the $x$-direction
- Steady-state in the moving-frame reference
- Negligible gas-phase combustion in the front
- Constant thermo-physical properties

**Energy balance on the control volume of the reaction front:**

The rate of heat conducted forward = (rate of heat brought in by solid) + (rate of heat generated by char oxidation)

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} \frac{d^2T}{dx^2} - \rho \, c_p \, v_s \frac{dT}{dx} + \dot{q}^{\prime\prime\prime} = 0$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ = effective thermal conductivity of the porous medium (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ = bulk density (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of the solid (J/kg·K)
- $\dot{q}^{\prime\prime\prime}$ = volumetric heat generation rate (W/m³)

**Step 1:** Assume the heat release is concentrated in a thin zone; integrate across the front with boundary conditions:
- $T(-\infty) = T_\infty$ (cold virgin fuel)
- $T(+\infty) = T_\infty$ (cooled char behind front)
- Peak at $T_s$ in the reaction zone

**Step 2:** Applying the Rank–Hugoniot type jump condition (heat flux balance across the moving discontinuity), the front velocity emerges as:

$$v_s = \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}} \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)}{\rho \, c_p \cdot L \cdot (T_p - T_\infty)} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta H_{\text{pyro}} / \Delta H_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Step 3:** Rearranged in the canonical engineering form:

$$\boxed{\;v_s = \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}} \, \Delta T_{\text{ox}}}{\rho \, c_p \, L \, \Delta T_{\text{pyro}}} \cdot \frac{\Delta H_{\text{ox}}}{\Delta H_{\text{pyro}} + \Delta H_{\text{ox}}}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check (typical polyurethane foam):**

- $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = 0.05 \; \text{W/m·K}$
- $\rho = 30 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $c_p = 1500 \; \text{J/kg·K}$
- $T_s - T_\infty = 600 - 25 = 575 \; \text{K}$
- $T_p - T_\infty = 300 - 25 = 275 \; \text{K}$
- $L = 0.1 \; \text{m}$
- $\Delta H_{\text{ox}} / (\Delta H_{\text{pyro}} + \Delta H_{\text{ox}}) \approx 0.6$

Substituting:

$$v_s = \frac{0.05 \times 575}{30 \times 1500 \times 0.1 \times 275} \times 0.6$$

$$v_s = \frac{28.75}{1\,237\,500} \times 0.6$$

$$v_s = 2.32 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.6 = 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\;v_s \approx 0.014 \; \text{mm/s} \;\;\text{(consistent with experimental polyurethane smoldering)}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Air Velocity for Transition to Flaming

When airflow through the porous char increases, additional O₂ is supplied. The critical condition is reached when the **heat generation rate equals the convective heat loss rate**.

**Step 1:** Volumetric heat generation from heterogeneous oxidation (per unit volume of porous char):

$$\dot{q}_{\text{gen}}^{\prime\prime\prime} = \Delta H_{\text{ox}} \cdot A \cdot \exp(-E_a / R T_s) \cdot C_{O_2}^n$$

**Step 2:** Convective heat loss to the incoming air stream (per unit volume):

$$\dot{q}_{\text{loss}}^{\prime\prime\prime} = \rho_{\text{air}} \, c_{p,\text{air}} \, v_{\text{air}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 3:** At criticality, $\dot{q}_{\text{gen}}^{\prime\prime\prime} = \dot{q}_{\text{loss}}^{\prime\prime\prime}$:

$$\Delta H_{\text{ox}} \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_s} \cdot C_{O_2}^n = \rho_{\text{air}} \, c_{p,\text{air}} \, v_{\text{air,crit}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $v_{\text{air,crit}}$:

$$\boxed{\;v_{\text{air,crit}} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{ox}} \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_s} \cdot C_{O_2}^n}{\rho_{\text{air}} \, c_{p,\text{air}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** This is the **maximum sustainable air velocity** before smoldering is "blown out" or transitions to flaming. Above this velocity, the volatile pyrolysates can ignite in the gas phase → **transition to flaming combustion**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Smoldering vs Flaming

| Criterion | Smoldering | Flaming |
|---|---|---|
| Phase of reaction | **Heterogeneous (solid + gas)** | **Homogeneous (gas + gas)** |
| Visible flame | **None** | **Yes** |
| Temperature | **450 – 700 °C** | **1000 – 1500 °C** |
| Propagation speed | **0.01 – 1 mm/s** | **0.1 – 10 m/s** |
| Primary fuel state | **Char (carbon)** | **Volatiles (gas mixture)** |
| Dominant toxicant | **CO (carbon monoxide)** | **CO₂ + Soot + CO** |
| Oxygen requirement | **Oxygen-limited** | Near-stoichiometric |
| Transition trigger (Da, airflow) | $\text{Da} \approx 1$ (critical) | $\text{Da} \gg 1$ with high $v_{\text{air}}$ |
| Heat release per kg | **~ 10 – 20 MJ/kg** (char) | **~ 40 – 50 MJ/kg** (volatiles) |
| Extinguishing method | **Smothering (cut O₂) + cooling** | Water, foam, chemical agents |
| Smoke production | **Very high** | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Smoldering Wave Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Heat Source Ignition] --> B[Pre-heating of Virgin Fuel]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis: Devolatilization]
    C --> D[Char Layer Formation]
    D --> E[Heterogeneous Char plus O2 Reaction]
    E --> F[Steady State Smoldering Front]
    F --> G[Heat Conduction Forward]
    G --> H[Self Sustained Propagation]
    H --> I{Critical Air Flow Reached}
    I -->|Da >> 1 high airflow| J[Gas Phase Ignition of Pyrolysates]
    J --> K[Transition to Flaming Combustion]
    I -->|Da approx 1 stable| L[Continued Smoldering]
    L --> M{Fuel Depletion or Air Cut Off}
    M -->|Oxygen cut off| N[Smothering Extinction]
    M -->|Char consumed| O[Self Extinction]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Energy & Mass Coupling in the Smoldering Front

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VirginFuelZone[Virgin Fuel Zone Upstream]
        VF1[Temperature below Tp] --> VF2[No Reaction]
    end

    subgraph ReactionZone[Smoldering Reaction Zone Thickness 5 to 50 mm]
        RZ1[Pyrolysis Endothermic] --> RZ2[Char plus O2 Exothermic]
        RZ2 --> RZ3[Volatiles Release]
    end

    subgraph CharZone[Char Residue Zone Downstream]
        CZ1[Cooled Char] --> CZ2[Ash and Inert Residue]
    end

    VF2 --> RZ1
    RZ3 --> CZ1
    RZ2 -. Heat Generation .-> RZ1
    RZ2 -. Heat Conduction Forward .-> VF1
    RZ2 -. Hot Gases Upward .-> EXT[Buoyant Plume CO CO2 Smoke]
```

---

### 4.3 Annotated Schematic of the 1-D Smoldering Wave

```
   T (°C)
    │
 700│          ┌──────┐         ← Peak (Smoldering front, T_s)
    │         ╱│      │╲
 450│        ╱ │ Char │ ╲       ← Active oxidation zone
    │       ╱  │+ O₂  │  ╲
 300│──────╱   └──────┘   ╲     ← Pyrolysis interface (T_p)
    │  Virgin fuel        Char + ash
    │
  25│________________________
    └─────────────────────── x (distance)
       ↑                ↑
   Cold feed      Cooling tail
```

- **Left of peak:** Virgin fuel pre-heated and pyrolyzed (endothermic).
- **At peak:** Char reacts heterogeneously with O₂ (exothermic).
- **Right of peak:** Spent char cools, releasing heat to surroundings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define smoldering combustion. List any four distinguishing features that separate it from flaming combustion.**

**Model Answer (12 – 15 lines):**

Smoldering combustion is a slow, low-temperature, flameless form of combustion that occurs on the surface of a porous solid fuel, in which the heat is released by the **heterogeneous reaction** of the solid char with atmospheric oxygen.

**Distinguishing features (any four):**

1. **No visible flame** — the reaction is purely solid–gas, with no gas-phase combustion envelope.
2. **Lower temperature** — typically **450 – 700 °C** versus **1000 – 1500 °C** for flaming.
3. **Slow propagation** — smoldering front velocity is in the order of **mm/s**, while flames propagate at **m/s**.
4. **High CO and smoke yield** — incomplete oxidation of char produces a large fraction of **carbon monoxide** and unburnt pyrolysates.
5. **Ventilation-limited** — smoldering requires a porous fuel and a controlled (low) oxygen supply; excess air can extinguish it.
6. **Heterogeneous kinetics** — governed by surface oxidation (Arrhenius-type), unlike the gas-phase chain reactions of flaming.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **What is the Damköhler number in the context of smoldering? State its role in the transition from smoldering to flaming combustion.**

**Model Answer (10 – 12 lines):**

The **Damköhler number (Da)** is a dimensionless ratio that compares the **characteristic flow (residence) time** to the **characteristic chemical reaction time** in the smoldering front:

$$\text{Da} = \frac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} = \frac{L \cdot k_{\text{ox}} \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_s}}{v_{\text{air}}}$$

**Role in transition:**

- When **Da ≪ 1**, the reaction is slow compared to airflow, and the smoldering front is **convectively cooled** — it tends toward **self-extinction**.
- When **Da ≫ 1**, the chemical reaction rate dominates, and a small perturbation (e.g., increased airflow, accumulation of volatiles) can cause the **pyrolysate gases to ignite in the gas phase**, producing a sudden **transition to flaming combustion**.
- The **critical transition** typically occurs at **Da ≈ 1**, where the heat generation rate equals the convective heat loss rate.

This makes Da the central controlling parameter for assessing the **fire hazard of smoldering materials**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Q A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **physical and chemical mechanisms** of smoldering combustion in a porous solid fuel. Use a labeled diagram of the smoldering front to illustrate the heat and mass transfer zones. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the **steady-state smoldering front velocity** starting from the 1-D energy balance, clearly stating all assumptions. Compare the predicted velocity with experimental values for a typical polyurethane foam. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q A(a) — Physical & Chemical Mechanisms (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identification of the porous solid fuel (1 mark):**
Smoldering requires a porous, carbon-rich, thermally insulating solid (e.g., polyurethane foam, wood, coal, cotton, peat). The porosity is essential because it allows O₂ to diffuse into the solid matrix.

**Step 2 — Identification of the four sub-processes (3 marks):**
1. **Pre-heating (conduction ahead of the front):** The smoldering front is hotter than the virgin fuel; heat conducted forward raises the temperature of the adjacent solid to the pyrolysis threshold.
2. **Pyrolysis (endothermic devolatilization):** At $T_p \approx 250 - 350 \; \text{°C}$, the solid decomposes into:
   - **Char** (carbon-rich solid residue, the fuel for smoldering)
   - **Pyrolysate gases** (CO, H₂, CH₄, tars, H₂O)
3. **Heterogeneous char oxidation (exothermic — the heat source):**
   $$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (\Delta H \approx -393 \; \text{kJ/mol})$$
   $$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad (\Delta H \approx -111 \; \text{kJ/mol})$$
4. **Minor gas-phase combustion:** Some pyrolysates may oxidize inside the pores, adding secondary heat.

**Step 3 — Heat/mass transfer zones (2 marks):**
The smoldering front is a thin (5 – 50 mm) zone containing three sub-layers:
- **Virgin fuel side:** heating + drying + pyrolysis (endothermic)
- **Reaction core:** char + O₂ surface oxidation (exothermic)
- **Char-residue side:** cooling + ash formation

**Step 4 — Closed-loop self-sustainment (1 mark):**
The heat released by char oxidation pre-heats the next layer of virgin fuel → cycle repeats → **self-sustained propagation** as long as char and O₂ are available.

---

#### Model Solution for Q A(b) — Derivation of $v_s$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State assumptions (1 mark):**
- 1-D steady-state propagation in the $x$-direction
- Constant effective thermal conductivity $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$
- Thin reaction-zone approximation
- No gas-phase combustion contribution
- No heat losses to surroundings (adiabatic bed)

**Step 2 — Write the 1-D energy equation in the moving frame (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} \frac{d^2T}{dx^2} - \rho \, c_p \, v_s \frac{dT}{dx} + \dot{q}^{\prime\prime\prime} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions and Rank–Hugoniot jump (1.5 marks):**
Integrate across the moving front, using:
- $T \to T_\infty$ as $x \to -\infty$ (virgin side)
- $T \to T_\infty$ as $x \to +\infty$ (downstream char side)
- Peak at $T_s$

This yields the front-velocity identity:

$$v_s = \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)}{\rho \, c_p \, L \, (T_p - T_\infty)} \cdot \frac{\Delta H_{\text{ox}}}{\Delta H_{\text{pyro}} + \Delta H_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (1.5 marks):**
Using polyurethane foam parameters (as derived in Section 3.1 of these notes):
$$v_s \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s} = 0.014 \; \text{mm/s}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison (1 mark):**
Experimental values for polyurethane foam smoldering are in the range **0.01 – 0.05 mm/s** (depending on density and air supply). The predicted value of **0.014 mm/s** is in excellent agreement, confirming the validity of the heat-balance model.

---

> **Q B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **conditions required for transition from smoldering to flaming combustion**. In your answer, clearly explain the role of the **Damköhler number** and the **critical air velocity**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With neat sketches, compare the **temperature profiles, propagation velocities, dominant products, and extinguishing methods** of smoldering and flaming combustion. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q B(a) — Conditions for Smoldering-to-Flaming Transition (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of the transition phenomenon (1 mark):**
Smoldering is **inherently unstable** in many real fire scenarios; it can suddenly transition to flaming when conditions become favorable for gas-phase ignition of the released pyrolysates.

**Step 2 — Required conditions (2 marks):**
Three simultaneous conditions must be satisfied:
1. **Sufficient volatile concentration:** Pyrolysate gases (CO, H₂, hydrocarbons) must accumulate above their lower flammability limit.
2. **Adequate temperature:** Local temperature in the gas phase must exceed the **auto-ignition temperature** of the pyrolysate mixture (~ **500 – 600 °C**).
3. **Sufficient oxygen mixing:** Mixing between pyrolysates and air must be within the flammability envelope (typically **Φ = 0.5 – 4.0** equivalence ratio).

**Step 3 — Role of the Damköhler number (2 marks):**
$$\text{Da} = \frac{L \cdot k_{\text{ox}} \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_s}}{v_{\text{air}}}$$
- **Da ≪ 1:** Reaction is slow → convective cooling dominates → **smoldering extinguishes**.
- **Da ≈ 1:** Critical balance → small perturbations in airflow can drive transition.
- **Da ≫ 1:** Reaction heat is high relative to convective loss → heat accumulates in the gas phase → **gas-phase ignition of volatiles → flaming transition**.

**Step 4 — Critical air velocity (2 marks):**
From Section 3.2 of these notes:
$$v_{\text{air,crit}} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{ox}} \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_s} \cdot C_{O_2}^n}{\rho_{\text{air}} \, c_{p,\text{air}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)}$$
- If $v_{\text{air}} < v_{\text{air,crit}}$ → smoldering is sustained.
- If $v_{\text{air}} > v_{\text{air,crit}}$ → transition to flaming is likely (if volatiles are present).
- If $v_{\text{air}} \gg v_{\text{air,crit}}$ → "blow-out" → smoldering is **blown out** (cooled by excess air).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (smoldering–flaming transition):**
> Students frequently confuse **"blow-out"** with **"transition to flaming."** They are OPPOSITES:
> - **Blow-out** = too much air → convective cooling → extinction.
> - **Transition to flaming** = air is *just right* to allow volatile accumulation + gas-phase ignition.
> - The phrase **"increase in airflow"** does **not** always mean transition; it depends on the **Da number regime**. State this explicitly in your answer to score full marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q B(b) — Comparison Table & Sketches (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Temperature profile (2 marks):**
- *Smoldering:* Smooth, broad Gaussian-like peak at **450 – 700 °C**, spread over **5 – 50 mm**; no sharp flame-sheet.
- *Flaming:* Thin, sharp reaction sheet at **1000 – 1500 °C**, thickness **< 1 mm**, with steep gradients on either side.

**Step 2 — Propagation velocity (1 mark):**
- *Smoldering:* **0.01 – 1 mm/s** (creeping wave).
- *Flaming:* **0.1 – 10 m/s** (rapid wave).

**Step 3 — Dominant products (2 marks):**
- *Smoldering:* **CO (major hazard)**, smoke, tars, low CO₂/CO ratio (~ 1 – 3).
- *Flaming:* **CO₂ (dominant)**, H₂O, soot, CO₂/CO ratio > 10.

**Step 4 — Extinguishing methods (2 marks):**
- *Smoldering:* **Smothering** (cut O₂) + **cooling** with water mist (since it is hard to detect).
- *Flaming:* Water spray, foam, dry chemical, CO₂ — applied to the **flame sheet** directly.

A side-by-side sketch should show: a broad low peak (smoldering) vs. a thin tall spike (flaming) on the same temperature axis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoldering Combustion — Last-Minute Revision (15 bullets):**

- Smoldering is a **slow, flameless, heterogeneous** combustion of porous solids.
- It is governed by the **solid-gas surface reaction** of **char + O₂**.
- The four sequential steps are: **pre-heat → pyrolysis → char oxidation → gas-phase combustion (minor)**.
- Smoldering temperature is **450 – 700 °C** — much lower than flaming.
- Propagation velocity is **0.01 – 1 mm/s** — 3–4 orders of magnitude slower than flames.
- The three pillars of smoldering are: **P**orosity, ventilation-limitation, heat retention.
- The **Damköhler number (Da)** = $\tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ controls transition to flaming.
- **Da ≪ 1** → extinction; **Da ≈ 1** → critical; **Da ≫ 1** → transition to flaming.
- **Critical air velocity** separates blow-out (too much air) from transition (optimum air).
- Smoldering produces **large amounts of CO** — the dominant toxic hazard in such fires.
- Common smolder-prone fuels: **wood, coal, polyurethane foam, cotton, peat, cellulose insulation**.
- Real-world examples: **Centralia mine fire (USA, since 1962), peat fires, mattress/foam fires**.
- The energy balance in the moving frame gives the **canonical smoldering velocity** $v_s = \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}} \Delta T}{\rho c_p L \Delta T}$.
- Heterogeneous kinetics follow the **Arrhenius rate law**: $\dot{\omega} = A \exp(-E_a/RT) C_{O_2}^n$.
- **Distinguish "blow-out" from "transition to flaming"** — this is the most-marked KTU board question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product of Combustion Harmful to Human - Flame, Heat, Smoke, Fire Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product of Combustion Harmful to Human - Flame, Heat, Smoke, Fire Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are the "Products of Combustion"?

In fire engineering, **products of combustion (POC)** are the physical and chemical byproducts released when a material undergoes combustion. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically identifies **four** principal classes of combustion products that pose hazards to human life and limb:

1. **Flame** — the visible, luminous (or non-luminous) zone of reacting gases
2. **Heat** — the thermal energy liberated by the exothermic oxidation reaction
3. **Smoke** — the airborne suspension of solid particulates and liquid droplets
4. **Fire Gases** — the toxic and asphyxiant gases evolved from pyrolysis and combustion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):** The "products of combustion" in fire safety engineering refers to the totality of **thermal, chemical, and physical** outputs of a fire — namely flame, heat energy, smoke aerosols, and toxic fire gases — that act in combination to cause harm (incapacitation, injury, or fatality) to exposed humans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memory:** "**F**irefighters **H**ate **S**moke **G**round" → **F**lame, **H**eat, **S**moke, **G**ases. A common exam short-answer.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **kitchen gas stove flame** that has just been left unattended. The blue flame you see is the **flame** itself (a hot, reactive zone). The warmth you feel standing two feet away is the **heat** (radiant and convective energy transfer). The slight haze that triggers the smoke detector is the **smoke** (tiny soot aerosols from incomplete combustion). Finally, the faint smell of acrid gas — that's the **fire gases** (CO, NOₓ, traces of unburnt hydrocarbons). All four products are *simultaneously* present and *each* is dangerous in its own right.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Insight (NFPA / NIOSH benchmarks):** In structural fires, **50–80% of fire fatalities** are attributable to **smoke inhalation** (toxic gas poisoning), not direct flame contact or burns. This statistic is a frequent KTU exam anchor.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Recall Ready)

The following standard values appear in nearly every KTU fire engineering numerical problem. **Memorize these.**

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|------------------------|
| Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (methane) | A/F | **17.2 kg air / kg fuel** |
| Heat of Combustion (methane) | $\Delta H_c$ | **–55.5 MJ/kg** |
| Stoichiometric Oxygen (O₂) in air | — | **20.9% (by volume)** |
| Lower Flammability Limit (CO in air) | LFL | **12.5%** |
| IDLH of Carbon Monoxide (CO) | — | **1200 ppm** |
| IDLH of Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN) | — | **50 ppm** |
| IDLH of Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | — | **40,000 ppm** |
| Human pain threshold (skin) | $q''$ | **≈ 1 kW/m²** |
| Second-degree burn (10 s exposure) | $q''$ | **≈ 5 kW/m²** |
| Auto-ignition temperature (paper) | — | **≈ 218–246 °C** |
| Lower Explosive Limit (methane) | LEL | **5% (vol)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Harmful Product

### A. FLAME

A **flame** is a thin reaction zone in which fuel vapors and oxidizer undergo rapid exothermic gas-phase reactions, releasing visible (and invisible) electromagnetic radiation.

**Two Principal Flame Types:**

1. **Premixed Flame** — Fuel and air mixed *before* combustion (e.g., Bunsen burner with air intake open). Reaction rate is high; flame temperature is highest.
2. **Diffusion Flame** — Fuel and air mix *during* combustion (e.g., candle, pool fire). Soot-laden, luminous, longer flame.

**Flame Temperature Ranges:**

- **Premixed (stoichiometric) methane–air:** ≈ **1950 °C (2223 K)**
- **Diffusion flame (candle):** ≈ **1000–1400 °C**
- **Smoldering combustion:** ≈ **300–600 °C** (no visible flame)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why flame harms humans:** Direct contact → thermal burns (radiant + convective). The flame zone is the *hottest* region in any fire envelope.

---

### B. HEAT

Heat is the **energy** released by combustion. It is *transferred* to humans (and structures) by three mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Mode | Engineered Mitigation |
|-----------|------|------------------------|
| **Conduction** | Direct contact (hot solids, walls) | Insulating PPE (Nomex, aluminized suits) |
| **Convection** | Hot gases rising, plume | Ventilation, water spray cooling |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic waves (IR/Visible) | Reflective barriers, distance |

**Heat Release Rate (HRR)** is the single most important fire parameter:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass burning rate (kg/s)
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg)
- $\chi_c$ = combustion efficiency (≈ 0.7 for ventilation-controlled fires)

**Critical Heat Flux Thresholds on Skin (10 s exposure):**

- **1 kW/m²** — Pain threshold
- **5 kW/m²** — Second-degree burns
- **10 kW/m²** — Third-degree burns, immediate incapacitation
- **> 20 kW/m²** — Instantaneous fatality

---

### C. SMOKE

**Smoke** is a **two-phase aerosol** consisting of:

- **Solid phase:** soot (carbon particulates, 0.01–1 µm diameter)
- **Liquid phase:** condensed water vapor, tars, partially oxidized organics
- **Gas phase:** entrained fire gases (CO, CO₂, HCN, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Smoke Property — Optical Density:** Smoke obscures vision. The **extinction coefficient** ($k$, in m⁻¹) governs visibility:
> $$ \text{Visibility} \approx \frac{3}{k} \text{ (m)} $$

**Smoke Yield ($Y_s$):** Mass of smoke produced per mass of fuel burned (kg/kg). Typical values:
- Wood: **0.005–0.015 kg/kg** (low-smoldering)
- Polyurethane foam: **0.10–0.20 kg/kg** (extremely high)
- PVC: **0.10–0.15 kg/kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoke causes incapacitation through THREE routes:** (1) optical obscurity → disorientation, (2) lachrymatory irritation → tearing → blindness, (3) inhalation of adsorbed toxic gases (HCN, HCl) on soot particles.

---

### D. FIRE GASES (Toxic & Asphyxiant)

This is the **lethal class** of products. Combustion of common materials generates:

| Gas | Source | Lethal Mechanism | IDLH (ppm) |
|-----|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **CO** (Carbon Monoxide) | Incomplete combustion of C-based fuels | Binds Hb (200× affinity of O₂) → tissue hypoxia | **1200** |
| **CO₂** (Carbon Dioxide) | Complete combustion | Respiratory stimulant → hyperventilation (increases CO dose) | **40,000** |
| **HCN** (Hydrogen Cyanide) | Nitrogenous fuels (wool, silk, nylon, PU foam) | Cytochrome oxidase inhibition → cellular asphyxia | **50** |
| **NOₓ** (NO, NO₂) | High-temperature oxidation of N₂ | Pulmonary edema, irritation | 100 (NO₂) |
| **SO₂** | Sulfur in fuels (rubber, coal) | Bronchoconstriction, pulmonary damage | 100 |
| **HCl** | PVC combustion | Severe respiratory irritation, corrosion | **50** |
| **H₂S** | Sulfur-containing fuels | Olfactory paralysis + cellular poison | 100 |
| **NH₃** | Nylon, melamine combustion | Respiratory irritant | 300 |
| **O₂ Depletion** | Displacement by combustion products | Hypoxia; <19.5% is hazardous | < 19.5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO is the #1 killer in fires.** It is colorless, odorless, and produced in *all* fires. The body absorbs it **200× faster** than O₂. Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) levels:
> - **< 10% COHb** → mild headache
> - **30% COHb** → severe headache, nausea
> - **50% COHb** → unconsciousness
> - **> 60% COHb** → death

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **high-yield** summary of all formulas needed for Module 1 exam problems. Note: absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` so as not to break markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$ | Heat release rate (HRR) | kW or MW |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_f = \dot{Q} / (\Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c)$ | Mass burning rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $Y_s = m_{\text{smoke}} / m_{\text{fuel burned}}$ | Smoke yield (dimensionless) | kg/kg |
| 4 | $S = k \cdot d \quad (\text{Beer–Lambert})$ | Smoke optical density | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Visibility} \approx 3 / k$ | Visibility through smoke | m |
| 6 | $\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO} = \text{constant} \approx \frac{m_c}{m_f}$ | Carbon balance in fire | mol/mol |
| 7 | $X_{CO} = \frac{\text{CO}}{\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO}}$ | CO/CO₂ yield ratio | mol/mol |
| 8 | $q''_{\text{rad}} = \sigma (T_f^4 - T_s^4)$ | Radiant heat flux (Stefan–Boltzmann) | kW/m² |
| 9 | $t_{\text{ign}} \propto \frac{\pi}{4} k \rho c \frac{(T_{\text{ig}} - T_0)^2}{q''^2}$ | Time to ignition (Quintiere) | s |
| 10 | $\text{COHb}\% \approx \frac{[\text{CO}] \cdot t}{K}$ (Coburn model) | Carboxyhemoglobin build-up | % |
| 11 | $L = \frac{[\text{O}_2]_0 - [\text{O}_2]_{\text{final}}}{[\text{O}_2]_0} \times 100$ | Oxygen depletion (%) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants to Recall in Exam:**
> - Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - Ambient O₂ content: 20.9%
> - Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (methane): 17.2 kg air/kg fuel

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of these four products is **not academic** — it is the foundation of:

- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Firefighter turnout gear (SCBA = Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) exists *specifically* to counter **fire gases**, while aluminized outer layers counter **radiant heat** from **flame** zones.
- **Building Fire Codes (NBC 2016, IS 14435):** Mandate smoke detectors and CO detectors because of the **smoke** and **fire gas** hazard.
- **Egress Design:** Visibility through smoke (≥ 10 m for safe evacuation) is a legal design requirement.
- **Toxicology & Forensics:** Post-fire COHb analysis is the *primary forensic* method of confirming fire fatality mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Combustion of Methane (CH₄)

**Goal:** Determine the stoichiometric mass of air required to combust 1 kg of methane completely.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation:**

$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 2(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2
$$

The factor **3.76** arises because air is 79% N₂ and 21% O₂, so the N₂: O₂ molar ratio is:

$$
\frac{79}{21} \approx 3.76
$$

**Step 2 — Identify molar masses:**

- Molar mass of CH₄: $12 + 4(1) = 16 \, \text{g/mol}$
- Molar mass of O₂: $32 \, \text{g/mol}$
- Molar mass of air: $28.97 \, \text{g/mol}$ (standard)

**Step 3 — Mass of O₂ per kg of CH₄:**

From the balanced equation, 1 mol CH₄ (16 g) requires 2 mol O₂ (64 g). Therefore, the mass of O₂ per kg of CH₄ is:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{64 \text{ g O}_2}{16 \text{ g CH}_4} = 4 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg CH}_4
$$

**Step 4 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass:**

Since air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.232} = \frac{4}{0.232} \approx 17.24 \text{ kg air} / \text{kg CH}_4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio for methane = **17.2 kg air/kg fuel**. This is a KTU standard reference value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CO and CO₂ Distribution in an Under-Ventilated Fire

**Setup:** In a **ventilation-controlled fire**, incomplete combustion yields a mixture of CO and CO₂. The **carbon balance** states that all fuel-bound carbon is distributed between CO and CO₂.

**Step 1 — Define the CO/CO₂ yield ratio:**

$$
X_{CO} = \frac{\text{moles of CO produced}}{\text{moles of CO produced} + \text{moles of CO}_2 \text{ produced}}
$$

This ratio, $X_{CO}$, depends on the **equivalence ratio** $\phi$:

$$
\phi = \frac{\text{actual fuel/oxidizer ratio}}{\text{stoichiometric fuel/oxidizer ratio}}
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical correlation (Tewarson, NIST):**

For most hydrocarbon fuels, the CO yield increases sharply when $\phi > 1$:

$$
X_{CO} \approx 0.001 \, \phi^{1.8}
$$

**Step 3 — Illustrative numerical evaluation:**

Consider a fire with $\phi = 2$ (i.e., **twice** the stoichiometric fuel).

$$
X_{CO} = 0.001 \times 2^{1.8} = 0.001 \times 3.482 = 0.00348
$$

In percentage form: **$X_{CO} = 0.348\%$** — still low because $\phi = 2$ is only moderately fuel-rich.

**Step 4 — Strong under-ventilation ($\phi = 5$):**

$$
X_{CO} = 0.001 \times 5^{1.8} = 0.001 \times 18.12 = 0.01812 = 1.81\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As **$\phi$ increases** (fires become more under-ventilated), the **CO yield rises sharply**. This is why post-flashover rooms in structural fires often record lethal CO (> 5%) atmospheres.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time to Incapacitation by CO (Coburn–Forster-Kane Model, Simplified)

The fractional carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) saturation in blood after exposure time $t$ can be approximated by:

$$
\frac{d(\text{COHb})}{dt} = \frac{[\text{CO}] \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{alv}}}{V_b \cdot \text{Hb}_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Steady-state approximation:**

At constant [CO] (ppm), the COHb level at time $t$:

$$
\text{COHb}(t) \approx \frac{[\text{CO}] \cdot t}{K_{\text{CK}}}
$$

where $K_{\text{CK}} \approx 35,000 \, \text{ppm} \cdot \text{min}$ is the Coburn constant for an average adult at rest.

**Step 2 — Sample computation:**

Exposure to **1000 ppm CO** for **10 minutes**:

$$
\text{COHb}(t) = \frac{1000 \times 10}{35{,}000} = \frac{10{,}000}{35{,}000} = 0.286 = 28.6\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** 28.6% COHb — this corresponds to **severe headache, nausea, and potential incapacitation** within 10 minutes of exposure to 1000 ppm CO. This is a typical structural-fire atmosphere and explains the rapid incapacitation of fire victims.

**Step 3 — Critical exposure threshold:**

For **50% COHb** (unconsciousness):

$$
t_{\text{incap}} = \frac{0.50 \times 35{,}000}{[\text{CO}]} = \frac{17{,}500}{[\text{CO in ppm}]} \, \text{min}
$$

For [CO] = 5000 ppm (5% — common in post-flashover):

$$
t_{\text{incap}} = \frac{17{,}500}{5000} = 3.5 \text{ min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single calculation is **directly exam-worthy** in KTU Module 1 problems on CO toxicity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Smoke Visibility from Optical Density

**Step 1 — Beer–Lambert Law for smoke attenuation:**

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-k \, d}
$$

where:
- $I_0$ = initial light intensity
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $k$ = extinction coefficient (m⁻¹)
- $d$ = path length through smoke (m)

**Step 2 — Define visibility as the distance at which contrast drops to a just-perceivable threshold ($I/I_0 = 0.01$ for reflective signs):**

$$
0.01 = e^{-k d_v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_v = \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{k} = \frac{4.605}{k} \approx \frac{3}{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked numerical example:**

If $k = 1.5 \, \text{m}^{-1}$ (typical smoke from a polyurethane foam chair fire):

$$
d_v = \frac{3}{1.5} = 2 \, \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Visibility in a polyurethane-foam room fire is only **2 m** — well below the **10 m minimum** recommended by most fire codes for safe evacuation. This is a common KTU question setup.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Product Hazard Summary

| Product | Primary Hazard | Quantified Threshold | Most Vulnerable Target |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Flame** | Direct thermal injury | Skin pain @ 1 kW/m²; 2nd-degree burns @ 5 kW/m² | Skin, airways |
| **Heat** | Hyperthermia, dehydration | 140 °C wet-bulb → 5-min tolerance | Respiratory tract, skin |
| **Smoke** | Obscuration, soot-bound toxics | Visibility < 10 m → evac failure | Eyes, lungs |
| **CO** | Chemical asphyxiation (Hb binding) | 50% COHb → unconsciousness | Cardiovascular |
| **HCN** | Cellular asphyxia | 50 ppm → rapid death | Brain, heart |
| **CO₂** | Respiratory drive, hyperventilation | > 5% → hyperventilation → ↑ CO uptake | CNS, lungs |
| **HCl** | Pulmonary corrosion | 50 ppm → incapacitation | Lungs, eyes |
| **O₂ depletion** | Hypoxia | < 16% → motor impairment; < 10% → LOC | Brain |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Products of Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fire Combustion Event"] --> B["Visible Class"]
    A --> C["Invisible Class"]

    B --> B1["Flame: Premixed Diffusion"]
    B --> B2["Heat: Conduction Convection Radiation"]
    B --> B3["Smoke: Soot Aerosols Vapors Liquid Droplets"]

    C --> C1["Toxic Fire Gases"]
    C --> C2["Asphyxiant Gases"]
    C --> C3["Irritant Gases"]

    C1 --> G1["Carbon Monoxide CO"]
    C1 --> G2["Hydrogen Cyanide HCN"]
    C1 --> G3["Hydrogen Sulfide H2S"]

    C2 --> G4["Carbon Dioxide CO2"]
    C2 --> G5["Oxygen Depletion O2 less than 19.5 percent"]

    C3 --> G6["Nitrogen Oxides NOx"]
    C3 --> G7["Sulfur Dioxide SO2"]
    C3 --> G8["Hydrogen Chloride HCl"]
    C3 --> G9["Ammonia NH3"]

    B1 --> H1["Hazard: Thermal Burns"]
    B2 --> H2["Hazard: Hyperthermia, Dehydration"]
    B3 --> H3["Hazard: Disorientation, Inhalation Injury"]

    G1 --> H4["Hazard: Chemical Asphyxiation via COHb"]
    G2 --> H5["Hazard: Cellular Asphyxia via Cytochrome Block"]
    G3 --> H6["Hazard: Cellular Poisoning"]
    G4 --> H7["Hazard: Hyperventilation Increases Toxin Dose"]
    G5 --> H8["Hazard: Hypoxia, Loss of Consciousness"]
    G6 --> H9["Hazard: Pulmonary Edema"]
    G7 --> H10["Hazard: Bronchoconstriction"]
    G8 --> H11["Hazard: Corrosive Lung Injury"]
    G9 --> H12["Hazard: Respiratory Irritation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Every combustion event produces *all* these categories simultaneously. The *relative proportion* depends on fuel chemistry, ventilation, and temperature.

---

## 4.2 Fire Gas Toxicity Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LethalThreshold["Lethal Concentration Hierarchy at 30 min Exposure"]
        direction TB
        T1["HCN: 100 ppm"] --> T2["H2S: 200 ppm"]
        T2 --> T3["CO: 3000 ppm"]
        T3 --> T4["HCl: 1000 ppm"]
        T4 --> T5["NO2: 200 ppm"]
        T5 --> T6["CO2: 10 percent"]
    end

    subgraph Mechanism["Mechanism of Toxicity"]
        direction TB
        M1["HCN: Cytochrome Oxidase Block"] --> M2["H2S: Mitochondrial Poison"]
        M2 --> M3["CO: Hemoglobin Binding 200x"]
        M3 --> M4["HCl: Tissue Corrosion"]
        M4 --> M5["NO2: Lipid Peroxidation"]
        M5 --> M6["CO2: Respiratory Acidosis"]
    end

    LethalThreshold --> Mechanism
```

---

## 4.3 Heat Transfer Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Fire Source: Heat Release Q dot"] --> M1["Conduction: Direct Contact"]
    Q --> M2["Convection: Hot Gas Plume"]
    Q --> M3["Radiation: EM Waves, Stefan Boltzmann"]

    M1 --> V1["Victim Contact Burns"]
    M2 --> V2["Upper Layer Inhalation Injury"]
    M3 --> V3["Remote Ignition, Skin Burns at Distance"]

    V1 --> OUT["Combined Hazard to Human"]
    V2 --> OUT
    V3 --> OUT
```

---

## 4.4 Smoke Transport from Source to Victim

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Fuel Pyrolysis Source"] --> P["Particulate Generation: Soot, Tar"]
    S --> G["Gas Generation: CO, HCN, CO2"]

    P --> A["Aerosol Suspension in Buoyant Plume"]
    G --> A

    A --> R["Rise to Smoke Layer"]
    R --> F["Fill Room, Reduce Visibility"]
    F --> V["Victim Inhalation and Disorientation"]
```
