# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q:** *List and briefly explain the four principal products of combustion that are harmful to humans. State one mitigation method for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — to-the-point):**

The four harmful products of combustion are:

1. **Flame** — The visible reaction zone of combustion. *Mitigation:* Avoid direct contact; use flame-resistant PPE (e.g., Nomex).
2. **Heat** — Thermal energy transferred by conduction, convection, and radiation. *Mitigation:* Reflective aluminized suits; water-cooling sprays.
3. **Smoke** — Particulate and condensed-vapor aerosol causing obscuration. *Mitigation:* Smoke detection systems; clear egress signage with photo-luminescent markers.
4. **Fire Gases** — CO, HCN, CO₂, NOₓ, HCl, etc. *Mitigation:* Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) for firefighters; CO detectors in buildings.

**(1 mark) for enumeration; (1 mark) for brief explanation; (1 mark) for mitigation linkage.**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** *Explain the toxicological mechanism of Carbon Monoxide (CO) poisoning in fire atmospheres. State its IDLH value.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carbon Monoxide is the **leading cause of fire fatalities** in structural fires. Its toxicological mechanism is as follows:

- CO has an affinity for hemoglobin (Hb) approximately **200 times greater** than that of oxygen.
- CO binds with Hb to form **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**, which is incapable of carrying oxygen.
- The result is **tissue hypoxia** (oxygen starvation at the cellular level), particularly affecting the brain and heart.
- Symptoms progress: **< 10% COHb → headache; 30% → nausea; 50% → unconsciousness; > 60% → death**.

**IDLH of CO = 1200 ppm (per NIOSH).** 

**(1 mark) for mechanism; (1 mark) for COHb/affinity; (1 mark) for IDLH value.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **Q:** *(a)* With the help of a balanced chemical equation, derive the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio** for the complete combustion of methane. *[(7 marks)]*
>
> *(b)* A 2 m × 3 m × 2.5 m room contains a chair made of polyurethane foam. The chair burns at a rate of 0.05 kg/s, with a heat of combustion of 25 MJ/kg and a combustion efficiency of 0.85. Compute the **(i)** Heat Release Rate, **(ii)** the volume of air in the room (initially at STP), and **(iii)** the time for oxygen to fall below 16% (the threshold for motor impairment). *[(7 marks)]*

---

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation of Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for CH₄

**Step 1 — Balanced Equation:**

$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 7.52\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Moles to Mass conversion:**

- 1 kmol CH₄ = 16 kg fuel
- 2 kmol O₂ = 64 kg O₂
- Air mass per kmol fuel = 64 / 0.232 = 275.86 kg

**Step 3 — Mass of air per kg of fuel:**

$$
(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{275.86}{16} = 17.24 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} \approx 17.2 \, \text{kg air/kg CH_4}}$

**(Full marks awarded for: balanced equation = 2; mass conversion = 3; final A/F value with units = 2.)**

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**(i) Heat Release Rate (HRR):**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c
$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_f = 0.05 \,\text{kg/s}$, $\Delta H_c = 25{,}000 \,\text{kJ/kg}$, $\chi_c = 0.85$:

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.05 \times 25{,}000 \times 0.85 = 1062.5 \, \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Volume of air in the room:**

$$
V_{\text{room}} = 2 \times 3 \times 2.5 = 15 \, \text{m}^3
$$

At STP, 1 m³ of air ≈ 1.2 kg. Volume of air (in m³) is just the room volume:

$$
V_{\text{air}} = 15 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Oxygen mass in room (initial):**

$$
m_{O_2,0} = 15 \times 1.2 \times 0.232 = 4.176 \, \text{kg O}_2
$$

**Oxygen consumption rate in fire:**

If the fire is stoichiometric for the equivalent fuel, the O₂ consumption rate is:

$$
\dot{m}_{O_2} = \dot{m}_f \times 4 = 0.05 \times 4 = 0.2 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Time for O₂ to drop from 20.9% to 16%:**

Oxygen fraction to be consumed:

$$
\Delta X = 0.209 - 0.16 = 0.049 \text{ (or 4.9% in volume terms)}
$$

Corresponding oxygen mass to be consumed:

$$
\Delta m_{O_2} = 4.176 \times \frac{0.049}{0.209} = 0.979 \, \text{kg}
$$

Time required:

$$
t = \frac{\Delta m_{O_2}}{\dot{m}_{O_2}} = \frac{0.979}{0.2} = 4.9 \, \text{s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\dot{Q} = 1062.5 \, \text{kW}$; $V_{\text{air}} = 15 \, \text{m}^3$; $t \approx 4.9 \, \text{s}$ to reach 16% O₂. This shows how *rapidly* a small polyurethane-foam fire can render a room atmosphere hazardous.

**(Marks: HRR = 2; volume = 1; O₂ time = 4.)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** *(a)* Discuss the **thermal hazard** of a fire to a human. List the three modes of heat transfer and the threshold heat fluxes (kW/m²) for: (i) pain, (ii) second-degree burns, and (iii) third-degree burns (at 10 s exposure). *[(7 marks)]*
>
> *(b)* A firefighter is exposed to a 1200 ppm CO atmosphere for 12 minutes. Using the simplified Coburn–Forster–Kane equation with $K_{\text{CK}} = 35{,}000 \, \text{ppm}\cdot\text{min}$, estimate the **percentage of COHb** built up in the blood. Comment on the **physiological consequences**. *[(7 marks)]*

---

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Thermal Hazard Discussion

The thermal hazard of a fire arises from the high temperature of combustion products and the three heat-transfer mechanisms:

1. **Conduction** — Transfer of heat through direct contact (e.g., touching a hot wall or burning debris).
2. **Convection** — Transfer via hot rising gases and the ceiling jet.
3. **Radiation** — Transfer by electromagnetic waves emitted from flames and hot smoke layers.

**Threshold Heat Fluxes (at 10-s exposure):**

| Level | Heat Flux |
|-------|-----------|
| Pain threshold | **1 kW/m²** |
| Second-degree burns | **5 kW/m²** |
| Third-degree burns | **≈ 10 kW/m²** |
| Instantaneous fatality | **> 20 kW/m²** |

**Physiological Consequence:** Burns, dehydration, hyperthermia, and respiratory-tract injury (inhaled hot gases cause edema of the upper airway).

**(3 marks for modes; 3 marks for thresholds; 1 mark for consequence.)**

---

### Part (b) — COHb Calculation

**Given:** [CO] = 1200 ppm; $t$ = 12 min; $K_{\text{CK}} = 35{,}000$ ppm·min

**Coburn Equation (Simplified):**

$$
\text{COHb}(t) = \frac{[\text{CO}] \cdot t}{K_{\text{CK}}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\text{COHb}(t) = \frac{1200 \times 12}{35{,}000} = \frac{14{,}400}{35{,}000} = 0.411 = 41.1\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Approximately **41% COHb** in blood. This is a **critical level** corresponding to severe symptoms: confusion, vomiting, potential loss of consciousness, and risk of imminent collapse. The firefighter is **operationally incapacitated** and requires **immediate evacuation and 100% O₂ therapy**.

**(4 marks for calculation; 3 marks for physiological interpretation with correct threshold levels.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Losing Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing LFL with LEL:** LFL and LEL are sometimes used interchangeably, but LFL is a *property* while LEL is an *operational limit*. KTU expects **LFL** in the academic sense.
> 2. **Forgetting units on COHb:** Always express COHb as a **percentage (%)**, not a fraction.
> 3. **Using 1 mol O₂ = 32 g incorrectly for air:** Air contains only 23.2% O₂ by mass — students often forget the 0.232 factor.
> 4. **Mixing up flame types:** "Premixed" means fuel–air *mixed before* combustion. "Diffusion" means fuel–air *mix during* combustion. Reversing this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the 3.76 nitrogen factor** in stoichiometric air calculation: examiners look for the explicit term 2(3.76)N₂ in the balanced equation.
> 6. **Not linking each product to a specific mitigation measure** in definition questions. KTU often allocates 1 mark specifically for *engineering control*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month-of-Exam Rapid Revision — Bullet Form**

- [x] **Four products of combustion:** Flame, Heat, Smoke, Fire Gases.
- [x] **Flame types:** Premixed (hot, blue) vs Diffusion (sooty, yellow). Premixed CH₄–air flame T ≈ 1950 °C.
- [x] **Three heat-transfer modes:** Conduction, Convection, Radiation.
- [x] **Key heat-flux thresholds:** 1 / 5 / 10 / 20 kW/m² → pain / 2nd-degree / 3rd-degree / fatality.
- [x] **HRR equation:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_c$.
- [x] **Smoke is a 2-phase aerosol** (solid + liquid) suspended in fire gases.
- [x] **Visibility** $\approx 3/k$ where $k$ is extinction coefficient (m⁻¹).
- [x] **IDLH values to memorize:** CO = 1200 ppm; HCN = 50 ppm; CO₂ = 40,000 ppm; HCl = 50 ppm.
- [x] **CO is the #1 fire killer**; affinity for Hb is **200× that of O₂**; forms COHb.
- [x] **Coburn equation:** $\text{COHb}(t) \approx [\text{CO}] \cdot t / 35{,}000$.
- [x] **50% COHb → unconsciousness; > 60% → death.**
- [x] **HCN** is generated from nitrogenous fuels (wool, silk, nylon, PU foam) and acts as a **cellular asphyxiant** via cytochrome oxidase block.
- [x] **Stoichiometric A/F for CH₄:** 17.2 kg air / kg fuel.
- [x] **Carbon balance:** $X_{CO} = \text{CO}/(\text{CO} + \text{CO}_2)$.
- [x] **NFPA statistic:** 50–80% of fire deaths are from **smoke inhalation**, not burns.
- [x] **Visibility < 10 m** in smoke is considered **evacuation-failure** threshold.
- [x] **O₂ < 19.5%** = hazardous; **< 16%** = motor impairment; **< 10%** = loss of consciousness.
- [x] **Engineering controls** for each product: SCBA (gases), PPE/cooling (heat), smoke detectors (smoke), flame-resistant clothing (flame).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Kinds of Combustion - Flash Fire, Pool Fire, Deep Seated Fire, Spillover, Boil Over, Dust Explosion, BLEVE, VCE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Kinds of Combustion - Flash Fire, Pool Fire, Deep Seated Fire, Spillover, Boil Over, Dust Explosion, BLEVE, VCE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Special Combustion Phenomena

Standard hydrocarbon fires (diffusion flames on a wick) are well understood, but industrial fire engineering must deal with **atypical combustion regimes** that arise from specific fuel geometries, confinement conditions, and thermodynamic pathways. KTU 2024 Scheme groups these under "Special Kinds of Combustion" because they dictate **firefighting tactics, suppression system design, and process safety layouts**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to (i) classify the fire type from physical evidence, (ii) predict escalation to a more dangerous regime, and (iii) prescribe the correct suppression media (water, foam, dry chemical, inert gas, or evacuation).

---

## 1.2 The Eight Special Combustion Types — Formal Definitions

### 1.2.1 Flash Fire
A **Flash Fire** is the rapid, non-explosive combustion of a cloud of flammable vapour, mist, or dust dispersed in air, where the flame front propagates through the cloud at **subsonic velocity** (typically **< 1 m/s to ~10 m/s**) without producing damaging overpressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine striking a match inside a room where someone has spilled petrol hours ago. The vapour has mixed invisibly with air. The match ignites it and the flame races across the room, scorching eyebrows but **not blowing out windows**. The pressure rise is negligible (< 5 kPa) — this is a flash fire. It is dangerous due to thermal radiation and burns, **not** blast.

---

### 1.2.2 Pool Fire
A **Pool Fire** is a diffusion flame burning above a horizontal pool of liquid fuel. The fuel vapourises from the liquid surface and burns in a buoyant, turbulent plume whose geometry (height, lift-off, soot) depends on pool diameter, fuel type, and wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A kerosene puddle in a garage bay igniting — the flame stands vertically like a luminous pillar, fed by evaporation from the surface below. The bigger the puddle, the taller and more ferocious the flame, because evaporation scales with surface area.

---

### 1.2.3 Deep-Seated Fire
A **Deep-Seated Fire** (also called a *smouldering fire* or *deep-socket fire*) is a flameless, low-temperature (typically **300 °C – 600 °C**), surface-to-volume driven combustion that propagates slowly through porous solid fuels (coal, sawdust piles, cotton bales, polyurethane foam, jute, char) with oxygen access limited to internal pores.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A cigarette ember travelling through a mattress. It can burn for days, producing toxic CO and HCN, and re-ignite into a flaming fire days later (the *"rekindle phenomenon"*). Water sprayed on the surface often fails because the heat is hidden **deep inside** the mass.

---

### 1.2.4 Spillover Fire
A **Spillover Fire** occurs when a pool fire or tank fire becomes so large that burning fuel **overflows the bund/containment wall** and ignites the surrounding area, effectively converting a localised incident into a multi-source fire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A boiling pot of oil on a stove — the moment the froth crests the rim, the oil spills onto the burner and ignites a new flame. The original fire is now no longer contained; it has "spilled over" its engineered boundary.

---

### 1.2.5 Boilover
A **Boilover** is a violent eruption of a tank fire caused when a **water layer (or water-in-fuel emulsion) at the bottom of a hot oil storage tank** is suddenly flashed to steam, ejecting burning oil as a fountain **up to 10× the tank diameter in height** and **up to 5× the tank diameter laterally**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Dropping a wet match into hot oil. The water (denser than oil) sinks, contacts the hot bottom, and violently turns to steam, throwing burning oil onto the kitchen ceiling. In industrial tanks of crude oil (which naturally contains emulsified water), this can happen **hours into a fire** when the thermal wave reaches the water layer. Two main sub-types: **Hot Zone Boilover** (light crude, fast) and **Full Surface Boilover** (heavy crude, delayed).

---

### 1.2.6 Dust Explosion
A **Dust Explosion** is the rapid combustion of finely divided solid particles (typically **< 500 µm**) suspended in air within a confined or semi-confined space, producing damaging overpressure due to the extremely high surface-area-to-mass ratio of the dust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Powdered custard in a flour mill. Normally harmless, but when the dust cloud fills a room and a spark is introduced, every particle becomes a tiny fuel droplet — the entire cloud ignites in milliseconds. Grain silos, sugar refineries, coal handling plants, and 3D-printing workshops are all at risk. The first, smaller explosion often **disturbs settled dust** on beams and ledges, creating a larger **secondary explosion** that is typically the destructive one.

---

### 1.2.7 BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion)
A **BLEVE** is a catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a liquefied gas (e.g., LPG, propane, butane, chlorine, ammonia) at a temperature **above its atmospheric boiling point**, triggered by external fire impingement that weakens the shell. The vessel ruptures, the superheated liquid instantly flashes to vapour, and a **fireball** is produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A pressure cooker on a gas stove. If the gas burner is left on and the cooker boils dry, the metal softens, the lid blows off, and superheated steam erupts. An LPG BLEVE is identical in principle but on an industrial scale — the fireball can be **50 m – 300 m in diameter** and last **5 s – 30 s**, with thermal radiation lethal at hundreds of metres. **Note:** A BLEVE of a *non-flammable* liquefied gas (e.g., chlorine) produces no fireball but a toxic gas cloud.

---

### 1.2.8 Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)
A **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is a deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT) or high-speed deflagration occurring in a large, partially confined or congested cloud of flammable vapour released accidentally. It produces destructive blast overpressure (often **> 20 kPa**, capable of demolishing buildings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine opening a valve on a propane pipe and letting the gas drift for 10 minutes through a refinery pipe-rack. The gas has filled every gap between pipes, columns, and vessels. A single spark — and the entire congested cloud detonates. The **Flixborough disaster (1974, UK)** and **Skikda disaster (2004, Algeria)** are textbook VCE events. The key accelerants are **confinement** (walls, decks) and **congestion** (pipes, vessels creating turbulence).

---

## 1.3 Quick Classification Matrix

| Phenomenon | Phase of Fuel | Driving Mechanism | Pressure Rise | Classic Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Fire | Vapour/mist/dust cloud | Subsonic flame propagation | < 5 kPa | Ignition source control, ventilation |
| Pool Fire | Liquid pool | Diffusion flame on surface | Atmospheric | Foam blanket, cooling |
| Deep-Seated | Porous solid | Surface oxidation / smoulder | Atmospheric | Penetration + soaking (water/foam) |
| Spillover | Liquid overflow | Mechanical breach of bund | Atmospheric | Larger bund, drainage |
| Boil Over | Liquid + water layer | Steam explosion in oil | Transient burst | Avoid water contamination, early extinguishment |
| Dust Explosion | Suspended solid | Rapid surface combustion | **5 – 9 bar** | Inerting, venting, suppression |
| BLEVE | Pressurised liquefied gas | Vessel failure + flash vaporisation | **5 – 20 bar** | Water cooling, relief valves, spacing |
| VCE | Released flammable gas | Deflagration / DDT in congestion | **> 20 kPa (up to 1 MPa)** | Vapour detection, congestion control |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** The KTU board often frames questions around the *transition* between regimes — e.g., *pool fire → boilover*, *vapour leak → flash fire → VCE*, *small dust deflagration → secondary dust explosion*. Memorise these escalation chains.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pool Fire — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Mass Burning Rate (Heskestad / Babrauskas)

The mass burning rate of a liquid pool depends on pool diameter **D** and fuel type. The dimensionless form is:

$$ \dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k \beta D}\right) $$

Where:
- $\dot{m}''$ = mass burning rate per unit area (kg/m²·s)
- $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ = asymptotic (large-pool) mass burning rate (kg/m²·s)
- $k\beta$ = dimensionless optical extinction coefficient × geometric factor (typically **1.1 – 2.1 m⁻¹**)
- $D$ = pool diameter (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** For **D > 0.3 m** (most industrial pools), $\dot{m}'' \approx \dot{m}''_{\infty}$, and the **total burning rate scales linearly with pool area** (i.e., $A = \pi D^2 / 4$). This is why a small increase in spill size produces a disproportionately large increase in heat release.

### 2.1.2 Heat Release Rate (HRR)

$$ \text{HRR} = Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta H_c $$

Where $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (J/kg).

### 2.1.3 Flame Height Correlation (Heskestad)

$$ \frac{L_f}{D} = -1.02 + 15.6 \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{Q}^{*\,2/5}}{D}\right) $$

Where $\dot{Q}^* = \dot{Q} / (\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{gD} D^2)$ is the dimensionless HRR. Flame height governs **thermal radiation footprint**.

---

## 2.2 Boilover — Time-to-Boilover (Broido Theory)

For a crude oil tank, the **thermal wave** descends through the fuel at velocity **v** (m/s). When it reaches the water layer, boilover occurs:

$$ t_{bo} = \frac{H_{fuel} - h_{water}}{v_{bo}} $$

Where:
- $t_{bo}$ = time to boilover (s)
- $H_{fuel}$ = depth of fuel layer (m)
- $h_{water}$ = depth of water layer (m)
- $v_{bo}$ = thermal wave descent velocity, typically **0.0001 – 0.0006 m/s** (≈ **10 – 50 cm/hour**) for crude oil

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For light crude, expect boilover in **6 – 12 hours**; for heavy crude, it may be **20 – 50 hours**. Firefighters must be withdrawn from the tank farm well before this.

---

## 2.3 Dust Explosion — Kst and Pmax

The hazard of a dust is characterised by two standardised tests (Siwek 20-L sphere, ASTM E1226):

$$ K_{St} = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} $$

Where:
- $(dP/dt)_{max}$ = maximum rate of pressure rise (bar/s)
- $V$ = vessel volume (m³)
- $K_{St}$ = deflagration index (bar·m/s)

**Dust Hazard Classes:**

| Class | $K_{St}$ (bar·m/s) | Typical Dusts |
|---|---|---|
| St 1 | 0 – 200 | Charcoal, sugar, wood flour |
| St 2 | 201 – 300 | Cellulose, sulphur, hard rubber |
| St 3 | > 300 | Aluminium, magnesium, TNT dust |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** $K_{St}$ is for the **cloud**, not the layer. A low-$K_{St}$ dust can still cause catastrophic **secondary explosions** if it accumulates on surfaces and is lofted by the primary event.

The **explosion pressure** $P_{max}$ ranges from **5 bar (St 1) to 9–12 bar (St 3)** at optimum concentration.

---

## 2.4 BLEVE — Conditions and Fireball Geometry

A BLEVE requires three simultaneous conditions:

1. The vessel contains a **liquefied gas** (e.g., propane, butane, chlorine).
2. The liquid temperature is **above** its atmospheric boiling point (i.e., superheated).
3. The vessel wall is **mechanically weakened** — typically by an external flame impingement that softens the steel above the liquid level (where there is no internal liquid cooling).

**Fireball diameter (Roberts correlation):**

$$ D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot M^{0.325} $$

**Fireball duration:**

$$ t_{fb} = 0.825 \cdot M^{0.26} $$

Where $M$ is the mass of fuel released (kg), $D_{fb}$ is in metres, and $t_{fb}$ is in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (1000 kg propane release):** $D_{fb} \approx 6.48 \times 1000^{0.325} \approx 67$ m, lasting $\approx 8.3$ s. The thermal radiation can ignite secondary fuels at distances exceeding **150 m**.

---

## 2.5 Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) — TNT Equivalence

The most common engineering prediction method is the **TNT Equivalence Method**:

$$ W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot \frac{M_f \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$

Where:
- $W_{TNT}$ = equivalent mass of TNT (kg)
- $M_f$ = mass of flammable vapour in cloud (kg)
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion of fuel (J/kg)
- $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg = **4.68 × 10⁶ J/kg** (energy release of TNT)
- $\eta$ = empirical yield factor, typically **0.01 – 0.10 (1 % – 10 %)** depending on congestion

The overpressure at distance **R** is then estimated using standard TNT blast curves (Kingery-Bulmash).

**Alternative — TNO Multi-Energy Method (van den Berg):**
The blast strength depends on the **congestion level** (energy release rate categories 1–10), with realistic industrial VCEs typically assigned to **Category 7–9** (strong blast).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pool fire burning rate | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ | $\dot{m}''$ (kg/m²·s), $D$ (m) |
| 2 | Pool fire HRR | $Q = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot (\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot \Delta H_c$ | $Q$ (W), $\Delta H_c$ (J/kg) |
| 3 | Flame height | $L_f/D = -1.02 + 15.6 \dot{Q}^{*\,2/5}/D$ | $L_f, D$ (m) |
| 4 | Boilover time | $t_{bo} = (H_{fuel} - h_{water})/v_{bo}$ | $t$ (s), length (m), $v$ (m/s) |
| 5 | Dust deflagration index | $K_{St} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | $K_{St}$ (bar·m/s), $V$ (m³) |
| 6 | BLEVE fireball diameter | $D_{fb} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$ | $D_{fb}$ (m), $M$ (kg) |
| 7 | BLEVE fireball duration | $t_{fb} = 0.825 M^{0.26}$ | $t_{fb}$ (s), $M$ (kg) |
| 8 | VCE TNT equivalence | $W_{TNT} = \eta M_f \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ | $W$ (kg), $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg |
| 9 | Ideal gas (BLEVE flash) | $PV = nRT$ | $P$ (Pa), $V$ (m³), $T$ (K) |

> [!TIP]
> All $\Delta H_c$ values for hydrocarbons cluster around **40 – 50 MJ/kg** (methane ≈ 50, propane ≈ 46, gasoline ≈ 44, hydrogen ≈ 142). Memorise $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg — it appears in every VCE numerical.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pool Fire Heat Release Rate Scaling

**Problem:** A circular pool of gasoline (diameter **D = 3 m**) catches fire. Given $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.055$ kg/m²·s and $\Delta H_c = 43.7$ MJ/kg, find the **total heat release rate (HRR)**.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

We start with the relation between mass burning rate, pool area, and heat release rate:

$$ Q = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot A_{pool} \cdot \Delta H_c $$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the pool area.**

For a circular pool of diameter D = 3 m:

$$ A_{pool} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 3^2}{4} = \frac{9\pi}{4} \approx 7.069 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the total mass burning rate.**

The total mass burning rate is the flux times the area:

$$ \dot{m} = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot A_{pool} = 0.055 \times 7.069 \approx 0.3888 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 4 — Convert $\Delta H_c$ to compatible units.**

$$ \Delta H_c = 43.7 \text{ MJ/kg} = 4.37 \times 10^7 \text{ J/kg} $$

**Step 5 — Calculate the HRR.**

$$ Q = 0.3888 \times 4.37 \times 10^7 $$

$$ Q \approx 1.699 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \approx 17 \text{ MW} $$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

A 3 m gasoline pool releases **~17 MW** of heat. This is equivalent to the continuous heat output of **~17 average domestic boilers** and would cause second-degree burns at a distance of approximately **15 m** within **10 seconds** (using the classical point-source radiation model).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-to-Boilover in a Crude Oil Tank

**Problem:** A vertical cylindrical crude oil storage tank has the following data:
- Total fuel depth: $H_{fuel} = 8$ m
- Water layer depth at bottom: $h_{water} = 0.5$ m
- Thermal wave descent velocity for this crude: $v_{bo} = 0.0002$ m/s

Find the **time to boilover** from ignition.

**Step 1 — Write the boilover time equation.**

$$ t_{bo} = \frac{H_{fuel} - h_{water}}{v_{bo}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known depths.**

The thermal wave must traverse only the **fuel layer** (it is blocked at the fuel–water interface). The fuel thickness through which the wave travels is:

$$ \Delta H = H_{fuel} - h_{water} = 8 - 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the time.**

$$ t_{bo} = \frac{7.5}{0.0002} $$

$$ t_{bo} = 37{,}500 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours.**

$$ t_{bo} = \frac{37{,}500}{3600} \approx 10.42 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 5 — Engineering recommendation.**

The fire brigade must initiate **defensive withdrawal** of personnel from the bund area by **hour 8** (≈ 28,800 s) to account for the **±20 % uncertainty** in $v_{bo}$ due to fuel composition variability. Boilover is imminent by hour 10.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BLEVE Fireball Sizing

**Problem:** An LPG road tanker (capacity **18 tonnes**) is engulfed in fire. The pressure relief valve fails. Estimate the **fireball diameter** and **duration** if the entire contents release and ignite.

**Step 1 — State the Roberts correlations.**

$$ D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot M^{0.325} $$

$$ t_{fb} = 0.825 \cdot M^{0.26} $$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to kg.**

$$ M = 18 \text{ tonnes} = 18{,}000 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the fireball diameter.**

$$ D_{fb} = 6.48 \times (18{,}000)^{0.325} $$

First, evaluate the exponent term:

$$ (18{,}000)^{0.325} = e^{0.325 \cdot \ln(18{,}000)} = e^{0.325 \times 9.798} = e^{3.184} \approx 24.16 $$

Therefore:

$$ D_{fb} = 6.48 \times 24.16 \approx 156.6 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the fireball duration.**

$$ t_{fb} = 0.825 \times (18{,}000)^{0.26} $$

$$ (18{,}000)^{0.26} = e^{0.26 \times 9.798} = e^{2.547} \approx 12.77 $$

$$ t_{fb} = 0.825 \times 12.77 \approx 10.5 \text{ s} $$

**Step 5 — Hazard distance estimation.**

Using the pi-method of fireball thermal radiation, the distance for **2nd-degree burn** is approximately:

$$ R_{burn} = 2.5 \cdot D_{fb} \approx 2.5 \times 156.6 \approx 392 \text{ m} $$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

A 156 m diameter, 10-second fireball is **lethal by thermal radiation out to ~400 m**. All residential and process structures within this radius must be **evacuated** before the BLEVE, and emergency responders must withdraw to **at least 1 km**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — VCE TNT Equivalence for a Propane Leak

**Problem:** A ruptured propane pipe releases **5000 kg** of propane that forms a vapour cloud. The cloud partially fills a congested pipe-rack region. Given:
- $\Delta H_c$ (propane) = **46 MJ/kg**
- Yield factor $\eta = 0.05$ (5 %, typical for moderately congested cloud)
- $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg

Find the **equivalent TNT mass** and the **peak overpressure at R = 200 m**.

**Step 1 — Total chemical energy released in the cloud.**

$$ E_{cloud} = M_f \cdot \Delta H_c = 5000 \times 46 \times 10^6 = 2.30 \times 10^{11} \text{ J} $$

**Step 2 — Convert to TNT equivalence.**

$$ W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot \frac{E_{cloud}}{E_{TNT}} $$

$$ W_{TNT} = 0.05 \times \frac{2.30 \times 10^{11}}{4.68 \times 10^6} $$

$$ W_{TNT} = 0.05 \times 49{,}145 \approx 2457 \text{ kg of TNT} \approx 2.46 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the scaled distance for TNT blast prediction.**

$$ Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{(2457)^{1/3}} $$

$$ (2457)^{1/3} \approx 13.51 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$

$$ Z = \frac{200}{13.51} \approx 14.8 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$

**Step 4 — Read the overpressure from the Kingery-Bulmash curve.**

At $Z \approx 14.8$ m/kg^(1/3), the peak side-on overpressure is approximately:

$$ \Delta P \approx 7 \text{ kPa} $$

**Step 5 — Damage assessment.**

7 kPa corresponds to:
- **Windows shattered** at 200 m
- **Partial damage to house structures** at this range
- **Eardrum rupture threshold** (16 kPa) reached at ~80 m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the conversion factor so small (η = 0.05)?** A real vapour cloud does not release all its chemical energy as blast — much is lost to incomplete combustion, heat radiation, and "puffing" (unburnt gas expansion). A value of 1 – 5 % is realistic for an open cloud; up to 10 % only for very heavily confined/congested cases.

## 4.1 Master Escalation Flow — How Special Combustions Evolve

The diagram below maps the **escalation pathways** between the special combustion types, which is a frequent KTU 2024 Scheme question topic.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a **Flash Fire** and a **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** with respect to flame propagation velocity, overpressure generation, and confinement requirement.

**Model Answer:**

A **flash fire** is the subsonic combustion (flame speed typically < 10 m/s) of a dispersed flammable vapour cloud in the open, producing negligible overpressure (ΔP < 5 kPa). It is a non-damaging thermal hazard causing burns by radiation and direct contact; confinement is **not required** and the cloud burns as it sits.

A **VCE** is a deflagration (or DDT) of a vapour cloud in a **congested and partially confined** geometry (pipe racks, building modules). Flame speeds reach 100 – 1000 m/s, generating destructive overpressure (typically > 20 kPa, up to 1 MPa) capable of structural collapse. A VCE is a **blast hazard**, not merely a thermal one. The two critical requirements absent in flash fire but essential for VCE are: **(i) confinement** (walls, decks, partial enclosures) and **(ii) congestion** (equipment creating turbulent flow fields that accelerate the flame).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Explain the phenomenon of **boilover** in a crude oil tank fire. How does the **boilover time** depend on the thermal wave velocity?

**Model Answer:**

A **boilover** is a violent steam explosion that occurs when a water layer underlying burning crude oil is suddenly flashed to steam, ejecting burning oil as a fountain. The progression is: (i) crude oil is heated at the surface by the pool fire, (ii) a thermal wave descends through the fuel at velocity $v_{bo}$ (typically 0.0001 – 0.0006 m/s for crude), (iii) when the wave reaches the water layer, nucleate boiling occurs, (iv) the steam expands rapidly, displacing the overlying burning oil as a froth that ignites on ejection.

The **boilover time** is given by $t_{bo} = (H_{fuel} - h_{water})/v_{bo}$, where $H_{fuel}$ is the fuel depth and $h_{water}$ is the water layer depth. It is therefore **inversely proportional** to $v_{bo}$: faster thermal descent → earlier boilover. The value of $v_{bo}$ depends on fuel composition (light crude has higher $v_{bo}$ than heavy crude), so light crudes exhibit boilover in **6 – 12 hours** while heavy crudes may take **20 – 50 hours**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 contributes one ESE question of 14 marks with **internal choice** between Q-A and Q-B. Each is structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**A (a)** *(7 marks)* — Classify the **eight special kinds of combustion** studied in Module 1 based on **(i) physical state of the fuel, (ii) presence/absence of confinement, and (iii) overpressure generation**. Present the classification as a **matrix** with clear justifications.

**Model Answer:**

| Phenomenon | Fuel State | Confinement | Overpressure | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Fire | Vapour/mist/dust | None required | < 5 kPa | Subsonic open burn |
| Pool Fire | Liquid pool | None | Atmospheric | Buoyant diffusion flame |
| Deep-Seated | Solid porous | Internal pores only | Atmospheric | Smouldering, no shock wave |
| Spillover | Liquid | Open but spreading | Atmospheric | Geometric overflow |
| Boil Over | Liquid + water | Vessel | Transient burst | Steam expansion in tank |
| Dust Explosion | Suspended solid dust | Confined vessel/room | 5 – 9 bar | Rapid surface combustion |
| BLEVE | Liquefied gas under pressure | Vessel (then ruptures) | 5 – 20 bar | Vessel failure + flash vaporisation |
| VCE | Released vapour | Congested pipe rack | up to 1 MPa | Deflagration/DDT in congestion |

**Logical grouping (for 7 marks):** The matrix above already earns ~4 marks. Additional 3 marks come from a textual **synthesis** explaining that:

- The **non-damaging** group (Flash, Pool, Deep-seated, Spillover) is dominated by **thermal hazards** and can be fought with conventional water/foam.
- The **damaging** group (Dust Explosion, BLEVE, VCE) requires **blast-resistant** design, special suppression (inerting, relief venting), and large **separation distances**.
- **Boil-over** is a transition between thermal and blast regimes because the steam expansion is brief but violent.

---

**A (b)** *(7 marks)* — A 5 m diameter pool of methanol (CH₃OH) catches fire. Given $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.017$ kg/m²·s and $\Delta H_c = 20$ MJ/kg, calculate the **(i) total mass burning rate, (ii) total HRR, and (iii) comment on the suppression strategy**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pool area.**

$$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 5^2}{4} = \frac{25\pi}{4} \approx 19.63 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Total mass burning rate.**

$$ \dot{m} = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \times A = 0.017 \times 19.63 \approx 0.334 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 3 — Total HRR.**

$$ Q = \dot{m} \times \Delta H_c = 0.334 \times 20 \times 10^6 = 6.68 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 6.7 \text{ MW} $$

**Step 4 — Suppression commentary.**

Methanol is **polar** and **water-miscible**, so the **best suppression is alcohol-resistant AFFF foam** at an application rate of **6.5 L/min·m²**. Standard AFFF foam collapses because methanol strips water from the foam blanket. Water spray alone can be used as a cooling stream but will not extinguish the fire because methanol mixes with water and re-ignites on the surface. The fire-water run-off must be contained — methanol-contaminated water is toxic and flammable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**B (a)** *(7 marks)* — With neat sketches, explain the **mechanism of BLEVE**. Using the **Roberts correlation**, determine the fireball diameter and duration for a BLEVE involving **5000 kg of propane**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mechanism (4 marks).**

A BLEVE requires a **pressurised liquefied gas** at a temperature above its atmospheric boiling point and a vessel that has been **structurally weakened** by external fire. The mechanism in five stages:

1. External flame impinges on the shell, particularly the **vapour space** above the liquid level.
2. The dry metal (no internal liquid cooling) heats rapidly, loses tensile strength around **550 – 600 °C**.
3. The pressure relief valve may not be sized to relieve the rising vapour pressure from external heating.
4. The shell **ruptures catastrophically**, releasing the superheated liquid.
5. The liquid **flashes instantaneously to vapour** (latent heat drawn from the liquid itself, cooling the rest), and a **fireball** rises buoyantly as a turbulent diffusion flame.

**Step 2 — Fireball diameter.**

$$ D_{fb} = 6.48 \times M^{0.325} $$

$$ M^{0.325} = 5000^{0.325} = e^{0.325 \times 8.517} = e^{2.768} \approx 15.94 $$

$$ D_{fb} = 6.48 \times 15.94 \approx 103.3 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Fireball duration.**

$$ t_{fb} = 0.825 \times M^{0.26} $$

$$ M^{0.26} = 5000^{0.26} = e^{0.26 \times 8.517} = e^{2.214} \approx 9.15 $$

$$ t_{fb} = 0.825 \times 9.15 \approx 7.55 \text{ s} $$

**Step 4 — Hazard distance.**

The thermal radiation fatality zone (2nd-degree burn) is approximately $2.5 \times D_{fb} \approx 258$ m. A 103 m fireball lasting 7.5 s is a major emergency that mandates **1 km evacuation**.

---

**B (b)** *(7 marks)* — A natural gas pipeline ruptures and releases **10 000 kg of methane** that forms a vapour cloud. The cloud is moderately congested (yield factor $\eta = 0.04$). Estimate the **TNT equivalent mass** and the **peak overpressure at 300 m from the cloud centre**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Methane energy content.**

$$ \Delta H_c (\text{CH}_4) = 50 \text{ MJ/kg} = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ J/kg} $$

**Step 2 — Total chemical energy.**

$$ E_{cloud} = M_f \times \Delta H_c = 10{,}000 \times 5.0 \times 10^7 = 5.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ J} $$

**Step 3 — TNT equivalence.**

$$ W_{TNT} = \eta \times \frac{E_{cloud}}{E_{TNT}} = 0.04 \times \frac{5.0 \times 10^{11}}{4.68 \times 10^6} $$

$$ W_{TNT} = 0.04 \times 1.068 \times 10^5 = 4273.5 \text{ kg} \approx 4.27 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 4 — Scaled distance at 300 m.**

$$ W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (4273.5)^{1/3} \approx 16.24 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$

$$ Z = \frac{300}{16.24} \approx 18.47 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$

**Step 5 — Overpressure from Kingery-Bulmash curve.**

At $Z \approx 18.5$ m/kg^(1/3), the peak side-on overpressure is:

$$ \Delta P \approx 4.5 \text{ kPa} $$

This corresponds to **window breakage** at 300 m. The 16 kPa (eardrum rupture) zone would be reached at approximately:

$$ Z_{16kPa} \approx 7 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} \Rightarrow R_{16kPa} = 7 \times 16.24 \approx 114 \text{ m} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing kg/s with m³/s, or kPa with bar. **1 bar = 100 kPa**. The board explicitly deducts for wrong units in HRR calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponent 1/3 in TNT scaling:** A frequent error is using $W_{TNT}$ directly as the scaled distance variable. Always cube-root.
> 3. **Confusing $K_{St}$ with $P_{max}$:** $K_{St}$ is the **rate** of pressure rise, not the maximum pressure. They are different dust-hazard parameters.
> 4. **Misclassifying Boil-Over vs Spillover:** Spillover is **mechanical overflow of fuel** beyond a bund; boilover is **internal steam explosion** in a tank. They are not the same.
> 5. **Writing "BLEVE" without expansion:** Always write **B**oiling **L**iquid **E**xpanding **V**apour **E**xplosion at first mention.
> 6. **Forgetting that a BLEVE of a non-flammable gas (e.g., chlorine) has NO fireball** — only a toxic release. The board tests this nuance in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

- **Flash Fire** → subsonic, low overpressure, thermal hazard only, **no confinement needed**.
- **Pool Fire** → diffusion flame above liquid, HRR scales with $D^2$, suppress with **foam blanket**.
- **Deep-Seated Fire** → smoulder, low temperature, **rekindle risk**, requires **penetration + soaking** (water/foam through the mass).
- **Spillover** → mechanical overflow of fuel beyond containment; mitigate with **larger bunds and drainage**.
- **Boil Over** → steam explosion in oil tank at $t_{bo} = (H_{fuel} - h_{water})/v_{bo}$; **light crude 6 – 12 h, heavy crude 20 – 50 h**.
- **Dust Explosion** → characterised by $K_{St}$ (St 1, St 2, St 3) and $P_{max}$ (5 – 12 bar). **Primary** explosion disturbs dust on surfaces → **secondary** explosion is the killer. Mitigate with **inerting, venting, suppression, housekeeping**.
- **BLEVE** → vessel failure of pressurised liquefied gas above its boiling point. Fireball: $D_{fb} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$, $t_{fb} = 0.825 M^{0.26}$. Mitigation: **water cooling of vessel, relief valves, separation distance**.
- **VCE** → deflagration/DDT in congested vapour cloud. Predicted via **TNT equivalence** with $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg, $\eta = 0.01 - 0.10$. Key inputs: **mass of fuel, $\Delta H_c$, yield factor, distance**.
- **Key constants to memorise:** $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg, atmospheric pressure 101.325 kPa, $g = 9.81$ m/s², typical $\Delta H_c$ of hydrocarbons = 40 – 50 MJ/kg.
- **Escalation Chains (most-tested):**
  - Liquid spill → Pool Fire → Boilover / Spillover
  - Gas leak → Flash Fire / VCE (depending on confinement & congestion)
  - Dust accumulation → Primary Explosion → Secondary Explosion
  - Pressurised LPG + fire → BLEVE
- **Suppression Selection at a Glance:**
  - Water → cooling (good for A, B class)
  - Foam → pool fires, Boilover prevention
  - Dry chemical → flash back, small flammable liquid
  - CO₂ / Inert gas → electrical, dust explosion suppression
  - Evacuation → BLEVE / VCE / Boilover (when fire has outgrown suppression capacity)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Fire Based on Material. Development of Enclosure Fire in Terms of Temperature - Incipient, Smoldering, Flame and Heat Stages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classification of Fire Based on Material. Development of Enclosure Fire in Terms of Temperature - Incipient, Smoldering, Flame and Heat Stages # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Classification of Fire Based on Material ### Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Fire Classification (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** Fire is systematically categorized into distinct classes based on the **physical state**, **chemical nature**, and **combustion behaviour** of the burning material. This classification governs the selection of the appropriate fire extinguishing agent, suppression technique, and risk-mitigation strategy. The universally accepted classification (per **NFPA 10**, **IS 15683:2018**, and **EN 2:1992/A1:2004**) divides fire into the following classes: | Class | Fuel Type | Common Examples | |-------|-----------|-----------------| | **Class A** | Ordinary solid combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics, trash | | **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, diesel, LPG, solvents, oils | | **Class C** | Flammable gases | Methane, propane, hydrogen, acetylene | | **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, titanium, zirconium | | **Class E (K)** | Electrical / Kitchen | Energised equipment / Cooking oils & fats | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Wrong Medicine" Analogy:** Imagine a doctor prescribing water-based medicine for an oil-based illness — it would be useless, or worse, harmful. Similarly, **using water on a Class D metal fire (magnesium) produces hydrogen gas and may cause an explosion!** This is why fire classification is the first step a fire engineer learns — *it tells you exactly which "medicine" (extinguisher) to use*. --- ## 1.2 Development of Enclosure Fire ### Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Enclosure Fire Development:** The progressive sequence of thermal, chemical, and physical events occurring inside a confined compartment (room, building, vessel) from ignition through decay. It is characterized by a **temperature-time curve** governed by fuel load, ventilation, and compartment geometry. ### The Four Canonical Stages 1. **Incipient Stage** — Ignition and invisible pre-combustion. 2. **Smoldering Stage** — Surface oxidation without visible flame; high CO yield. 3. **Flame Stage (Growth)** — Sustained flaming combustion; rapid temperature rise. 4. **Heat Stage (Fully Developed)** — Flashover completed; compartment fully engulfed. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Campfire Ignition" Analogy:** Think of lighting a campfire in a closed tent (the *enclosure*). > 1. **Incipient** = striking the match (energy input, no visible damage yet). > 2. **Smoldering** = the charcoal glowing red, smoking, but no flame (slow oxidation). > 3. **Flame** = the fire catches and flames leap up (rapid energy release). > 4. **Heat** = the whole tent is now a furnace — every combustible inside ignites spontaneously (**flashover**). --- ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Fire Exposure Temperature:** $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + T_0$ (ASTM E119 / ISO 834) - **Standard Reference Temperature ($T_0$):** **20 °C** (293 K) - **Flashover Threshold Temperature:** Approximately **500 °C – 600 °C** at the ceiling layer. - **Auto-ignition Temperature (typical solid):** **300 °C – 450 °C**. - **Heat Release Rate (HRR):** measured in **kW** or **MW** (ISO 9705 standard). # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Classification of Fire — Detailed Theoretical Framework ### Class A: Ordinary Combustibles - **Combustion Mode:** Smoldering + flaming with **ash residue**. - **Extinguishing Mechanism:** **Cooling** with water (high specific heat) and **smothering**. - **Standard Extinguisher:** Water, foam, dry chemical (ABC powder). ### Class B: Flammable Liquids & Gases - **Combustion Mode:** Vapour-phase combustion above the liquid surface. - **Extinguishing Mechanism:** **Oxygen exclusion** (smothering) — never use water jet (splashing spreads fire). - **Standard Extinguisher:** Foam, CO₂, dry chemical, clean agents. ### Class C: Flammable Gases (often clubbed with Class B in Indian standards) - **Combustion Mode:** Jet diffusion flame or vapour cloud explosion. - **Extinguishing Mechanism:** **Shut-off at source** is primary; then smother. - **Standard Extinguisher:** Dry chemical powder (ABC), CO₂. ### Class D: Combustible Metals - **Combustion Mode:** High-temperature surface oxidation releasing metal oxides. - **Extinguishing Mechanism:** **Heat absorption** with **special dry powder** (NaCl, graphite, copper powder). **NEVER WATER** — produces hydrogen + explosion. - **Standard Extinguisher:** Class D specific dry powder. ### Class E (Electrical) / Class K (Kitchen Oils) - **Class E:** Energised electrical equipment — non-conductive agent must be used. - **Class K:** High-temperature cooking oils — saponification reaction needed (wet chemical). --- ## 2.2 Development of Enclosure Fire — Analytical Breakdown ### Stage 1: Incipient Stage - **Duration:** Minutes to hours (very slow). - **Temperature:** Near ambient ($20$ °C to $50$ °C). - **Products:** Trace aerosols, possibly odorants. - **Visibility:** Unchanged. - **Detection:** Ionization or photoelectric smoke detectors (early warning). ### Stage 2: Smoldering Stage - **Temperature:** $50$ °C to $300$ °C. - **Combustion Mode:** Heterogeneous surface oxidation, sub-stoichiometric. - **Toxicity:** **HIGH CO yield** (carbon monoxide > 1000 ppm). - **Detector Response:** Smoke detectors activated; heat detectors may not trigger yet. ### Stage 3: Flame (Growth) Stage - **Temperature:** $300$ °C to $700$ °C. - **HRR Behaviour:** Follows the famous **t²-fire growth curve**: $$Q(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$ where: - $Q(t)$ = Heat Release Rate (kW) - $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient (kW/s²) - $t$ = time elapsed after effective ignition (s) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Growth Coefficients (per ISO/EN):** > - **Slow growth:** $\alpha = 0.00293$ kW/s² > - **Medium growth:** $\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s² > - **Fast growth:** $\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s² > - **Ultra-fast growth:** $\alpha = 0.1876$ kW/s² ### Stage 4: Heat (Fully Developed) Stage - **Temperature:** $700$ °C to $1100$ °C+. - **Phenomenon:** **Flashover** — simultaneous ignition of all combustible surfaces. - **Heat Flux to Floor:** $> 20$ kW/m². - **Structural Threat:** Steel loses $50\%$ strength at $550$ °C. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All key formulas for the KTU 2024 Scheme Fire Engineering examination are summarized below. **Memorize these — they carry 60% of the numerical marks in Module 1.** | Formula | Description | Units | Applicability | |---------|-------------|-------|---------------| | $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | Standard fire curve (ASTM E119) | °C, min | Structural fire design | | $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$ | t-squared fire growth | kW | HRR estimation | | $\dot{Q} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Convective heat release | kW | Energy balance | | $E_{total} = \int_0^{t_{total}} \alpha t^2 \, dt = \frac{\alpha t^3}{3}$ | Total energy released | kJ | Fuel load calculation | | $\rho_{smoke} = \frac{D_{m}}{D_{CO_2}}$ | Smoke optical density | dimensionless | Detector activation | | $t_{flashover} = \sqrt{\frac{Q_{fo}}{\alpha}}$ | Time to flashover | s | Egress design | | $T_{ign} = f(\text{Fuel type})$ | Auto-ignition temperature | °C | Risk assessment | | $v = K \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{H}$ | Ventilation factor (Birch formula) | m^(5/2) | Compartment fire | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** In the formula $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$, examiners often ask: *"If the HRR at 60 s is 21.6 kW, identify the fire growth class."* Compute $\alpha = Q/t^2$ and compare to the standard table above. **Memorize the four $\alpha$ values — they are asked every year.** --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Structural Design:** Civil engineers use the standard fire curve to design fire-resistant concrete and steel ratings (e.g., 2-hour fire-rated slabs). - **Egress Modelling:** Architects and safety engineers use the t²-growth model to size escape routes and design smoke management systems. - **Insurance & Risk:** Industrial fire hazard assessments use Class B/C fire loadings to set premiums and storage limits. - **Fire Suppression:** Sprinkler systems are activated based on ceiling temperature thresholds defined in the incipient-to-flame stages. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: The Standard Fire Temperature-Time Curve The **ASTM E119 / ISO 834 standard fire curve** gives the average gas temperature in a furnace as a function of elapsed time. It is derived empirically to simulate a fully developed cellulosic fire. ### Given The standard expression: $$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ where: - $T(t)$ = average furnace temperature at time $t$ (in °C) - $T_0$ = ambient temperature (taken as **20 °C**) - $t$ = time (in **minutes**) - $345$ = empirical constant calibrated for hydrocarbon/cellulosic fires (°C) - $8$ = empirical time coefficient (per minute) ### Derivation Logic (Conceptual Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > This curve is empirical (not derived from first principles). However, it can be motivated from the **lumped heat balance** of a fully involved enclosure fire. We present the standard solution framework: **Step 1 — Heat Balance for the Enclosure:** $$\frac{dE_g}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{fire} - \dot{Q}_{loss}$$ For a fully developed fire, the energy stored in the gas layer is small compared to the throughput, so the gas temperature reaches a quasi-steady state dictated by the fire size and ventilation. **Step 2 — Empirical Calibration:** Researchers at ASTM and ISO plotted experimental data from fully involved compartment fires involving cellulosic fuels (wood, paper). They found that the **logarithmic form** fits the data better than linear or quadratic forms because heat transfer in a hot compartment is dominated by radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann, $\propto T^4$). **Step 3 — Construction of the Standard Curve:** The logarithmic function $\log_{10}(8t+1)$ was chosen because: - At $t = 0$: $T(0) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(1) = T_0 + 0 = T_0$ ✓ - At $t = 30$ min: $T(30) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241) \approx 20 + 345(2.382) \approx 842$ °C (matches real furnace tests). - At $t = 60$ min: $T(60) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) \approx 20 + 345(2.682) \approx 945$ °C. - At $t = 240$ min: $T(240) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(1921) \approx 20 + 345(3.284) \approx 1153$ °C. **Step 4 — Validation:** The curve matches the **ASTM E119 furnace test** within ±10%, which is the acceptable engineering tolerance for fire-resistance certification. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern) ### Problem A **medium-growth t²-fire** ignites inside a $50$ m² office. Compute the HRR at $t = 90$ s, the total energy released in the first $90$ s, and identify the fire class. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Given:** - $\alpha_{medium} = 0.01172$ kW/s² - $t = 90$ s **Step 1 — Compute HRR at t = 90 s:** $$Q(90) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$ $$Q(90) = 0.01172 \times (90)^2$$ $$Q(90) = 0.01172 \times 8100$$ $$Q(90) = 94.93 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Total energy released (integrate Q from 0 to 90):** $$E_{total} = \int_0^{90} \alpha t^2 \, dt$$ $$E_{total} = \alpha \cdot \frac{t^3}{3} \Big|_0^{90}$$ $$E_{total} = \frac{\alpha \cdot t^3}{3} = \frac{0.01172 \times (90)^3}{3}$$ $$E_{total} = \frac{0.01172 \times 729000}{3}$$ $$E_{total} = \frac{8543.88}{3} = 2847.96 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to MJ:** $$E_{total} = 2.848 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 4 — Fire Class Identification:** Fuels involved: office furniture (paper, plastics, wood) → **Class A fire**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stage vs. Property | Property | Incipient | Smoldering | Flame (Growth) | Heat (Fully Dev.) | |----------|-----------|------------|----------------|-------------------| | **Temp (°C)** | $20$–$50$ | $50$–$300$ | $300$–$700$ | $700$–$1100$+ | | **HRR** | Negligible | Very low | Rising (t²) | Peak (steady / declining) | | **Visible Flame** | None | None | Yes | Yes (full room) | | **Smoke** | Trace aerosols | Heavy white/grey | Black, sooty | Less (efficient comb.) | | **CO (ppm)** | $< 50$ | $> 1000$ | $500$–$2000$ | $< 500$ | | **Detectors Active** | Aspirating only | Smoke | Smoke + Heat | All | | **Egress Safe?** | Yes | Caution | No | No — FLASH | | **Key Phenomena** | Pyrolysis begins | Surface oxidation | Flaming combustion | **Flashover** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Golden Rule:** *The smoke and CO during the smoldering stage are often MORE dangerous than the flame stage itself*. This is why CO detectors are mandatory in residential codes — most fire deaths occur in the smoldering phase before people wake up. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Enclosure Fire Development Stages (Temperature vs Time) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STAGE 1
INCIPIENT
20 to 50 C"]:::stage1 B["STAGE 2
SMOLDERING
50 to 300 C"]:::stage2 C["STAGE 3
FLAME GROWTH
300 to 700 C"]:::stage3 D["STAGE 4
FULLY DEVELOPED
700 to 1100 C"]:::stage4 E["STAGE 5
DECAY
Cooling Phase"]:::stage5 F["FLASHOVER
500 to 600 C ceiling"]:::flash A --> B B --> C C --> F F --> D D --> E classDef stage1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stage2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stage3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stage4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef stage5 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef flash fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Fire Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["FIRE INCIDENT
Identify Fuel Type"]:::start Q1{"Is fuel a
solid combustible?"}:::q Q2{"Is fuel a
liquid or gas?"}:::q Q3{"Is fuel a
combustible metal?"}:::q Q4{"Is the source
energised electrical?"}:::q Q5{"Is fuel a
cooking oil or fat?"}:::q A["CLASS A
Wood, Paper, Cloth
Use WATER or FOAM"]:::classA B["CLASS B
Petrol, Oil, LPG
Use FOAM or CO2 or DCP"]:::classB C["CLASS C
Methane, Propane
Use DCP SHUT SOURCE"]:::classC D["CLASS D
Magnesium, Sodium
Use SPECIAL DRY POWDER"]:::classD E["CLASS E
Electrical Equipment
Use CO2 or CLEAN AGENT"]:::classE F["CLASS K
Cooking Oils
Use WET CHEMICAL"]:::classK Start --> Q1 Start --> Q2 Start --> Q3 Start --> Q4 Start --> Q5 Q1 -- YES --> A Q2 -- YES --> B Q3 -- YES --> D Q4 -- YES --> E Q5 -- YES --> F B -.->|Also covers gases| C classDef start fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef q fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classB fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classC fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classD fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classE fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classK fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: HRR t-squared Growth Curve (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR F["FUEL PACKAGE
alpha value selected"]:::fuel T["TIME ELAPSED t"]:::time CALC["COMPUTE
Q of t equals alpha times t squared"]:::calc CURVE["PLOT HRR CURVE
Slow Medium Fast UltraFast"]:::curve FO["FLASHOVER
Threshold approx 1000 kW"]:::fo TEN["TENABILITY LIMIT
Smoke CO Heat"]:::ten F --> CALC T --> CALC CALC --> CURVE CURVE --> FO CURVE --> TEN classDef fuel fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef time fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef curve fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fo fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef ten fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Enclosure Fire Process ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["INPUTS TO COMPARTMENT"] I1["Fuel Load MJ per m2"] I2["Ventilation Openings"] I3["Ignition Source"] end subgraph S2["FIRE DYNAMICS CORE"] P1["Pyrolysis Rate"] P2["Combustion Efficiency"] P3["Heat Feedback to Fuel"] end subgraph S3["OUTPUTS"] O1["Temperature Field T xyz t"] O2["Smoke and CO Layer"] O3["HRR Q of t"] end subgraph S4["DESIGN RESPONSE"] R1["Detection Activation"] R2["Suppression Discharge"] R3["Evacuation Trigger"] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P2 I3 --> P1 P1 --> P3 P3 --> P1 P2 --> P1 P1 --> O3 P1 --> O1 P1 --> O2 O1 --> R1 O2 --> R1 O3 --> R2 O2 --> R3 O1 --> R2 classDef inputs fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef core fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef outputs fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef response fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Class B and Class D fires. Mention two suitable extinguishing media for each. **Model Answer:** > **Class B Fires:** Fires involving flammable liquids (e.g., petrol, diesel, oils) and liquefiable solids. Extinguishing media: **Foam (AFFF)** and **Dry Chemical Powder (ABC)**. > > **Class D Fires:** Fires involving combustible metals such as magnesium, sodium, potassium, and titanium. Extinguishing media: **Special Class D Dry Powder (e.g., NaCl-based or graphite powder)** and **Dry Sand** (for small fires). > > ⚠ *Note: Water MUST NOT be used on Class D fires as it reacts violently producing hydrogen gas, leading to explosion.* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** List the four stages of enclosure fire development with their typical temperature ranges. **Model Answer:** | Stage | Temperature Range | |-------|-------------------| | Incipient | $20$ °C – $50$ °C | | Smoldering | $50$ °C – $300$ °C | | Flame (Growth) | $300$ °C – $700$ °C | | Heat (Fully Developed) | $700$ °C – $1100$ °C+ | > The transition from flame stage to fully developed stage is marked by **flashover**, occurring at ceiling temperatures of approximately $500$ °C – $600$ °C. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Classify fire based on the type of fuel involved. Discuss the extinguishing media recommended for each class with reasoning. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Describe in detail the four stages of enclosure fire development with a neat sketch of the temperature–time curve. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Fire Classification (NFPA / IS 15683:2018):** **1. Class A – Ordinary Combustibles:** - *Examples:* Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics. - *Extinguishing Media:* **Water** (cooling — water has high specific heat of 4.18 kJ/kg·K and high latent heat of vaporization 2260 kJ/kg). Foam also used for depth. - *Mechanism:* Cooling below ignition temperature + quenching. **2. Class B – Flammable Liquids & Gases:** - *Examples:* Petrol, diesel, kerosene, solvents, LPG. - *Extinguishing Media:* **Foam (AFFF)**, **CO₂**, **Dry Chemical Powder (ABC)**. - *Mechanism:* Smothering — excludes oxygen from the fuel surface. - *Forbidden:* Water jet (causes splashing and fire spread). **3. Class C – Flammable Gases:** - *Examples:* Methane, propane, hydrogen, acetylene. - *Extinguishing Media:* **Dry Chemical Powder**. - *Mechanism:* Inhibition of chain reaction + shut-off of gas supply at source (primary action). **4. Class D – Combustible Metals:** - *Examples:* Magnesium, sodium, titanium, zirconium. - *Extinguishing Media:* **Special Class D Dry Powder** (e.g., sodium chloride, graphite, copper powder). - *Mechanism:* Heat absorption + oxygen exclusion by forming a crust. - *Forbidden:* Water (reacts to release H₂ → explosion), CO₂ (reacts with magnesium). **5. Class E (Electrical) / Class K (Kitchen):** - *Class E:* Energised electrical equipment → **CO₂, clean agents (Novec 1230), ABC powder** (must be non-conductive). - *Class K:* Cooking oils & fats → **Wet chemical (potassium acetate)** — forms soap-like layer via saponification. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Four Stages of Enclosure Fire Development:** **Stage 1 — Incipient Stage:** - Begins at ignition; products of combustion (aerosols, invisible gases) are released. - Temperature: $20$ °C – $50$ °C; no visible flame. - Detection: Aspirating smoke detectors can detect. **Stage 2 — Smoldering Stage:** - Slow heterogeneous combustion on solid surface; pyrolysis dominant. - Temperature: $50$ °C – $300$ °C; heavy white/grey smoke. - **Toxic hazard:** CO concentrations may exceed 1000 ppm — lethal in minutes. - Detection: Ionization / photoelectric smoke detectors trigger. **Stage 3 — Flame (Growth) Stage:** - Visible flaming combustion; HRR rises per **$Q(t) = \alpha t^2$**. - Temperature: $300$ °C – $700$ °C; black sooty smoke. - Ceiling layer begins to descend due to thermal expansion. - Detection: Smoke + heat detectors both active. **Stage 4 — Heat (Fully Developed) Stage:** - Flashover achieved; entire compartment fully engulfed. - Temperature: $700$ °C – $1100$ °C+. - HRR is at peak; structural collapse risk (steel loses strength at 550 °C). - Tenability: ZERO; occupants must have evacuated earlier. **Temperature-Time Curve (Schematic ASCII Sketch):** ``` T(°C) 1100 | .──────────── | .────` 900 | .───` | .───` 700 | .───` ← FLASHOVER ZONE | .───` 500 | .───` ← Ceiling layer critical |.─` 300 |` ← Flame growth begins | 50 | ← Smoldering zone 20|← Incipient └─────────────────────────────────────→ t (min) 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the t-squared fire growth model. A medium-growth fire with $\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s² starts in a room. Calculate (i) the HRR at 120 s, and (ii) the total energy released in the first 120 s. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the standard fire temperature-time curve as per ASTM E119 / ISO 834. Compute the furnace temperature at $t = 45$ minutes. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **t-squared Fire Growth Model:** The **t²-fire** is the simplest semi-empirical model describing the Heat Release Rate (HRR) growth of a fire from effective ignition: $$Q(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$ where: - $Q(t)$ = Heat Release Rate in kW at time $t$ - $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient in kW/s² - $t$ = time in seconds after effective ignition **Physical Meaning:** Energy release is proportional to the square of time, reflecting the increasing pyrolysis rate as the fire spreads over the fuel surface. This model is widely used in performance-based fire safety design (e.g., FDS, NIST simulations). **Standard Growth Categories:** | Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Real-World Example | |----------|------------------|---------------------| | Slow | $0.00293$ | Smoldering upholstered chair | | Medium | $0.01172$ | Office trash basket | | Fast | $0.0469$ | Stacked wood pallets | | Ultra-fast | $0.1876$ | Flammable liquid pool | **(i) HRR at t = 120 s:** $$Q(120) = 0.01172 \times (120)^2$$ $$Q(120) = 0.01172 \times 14400$$ $$Q(120) = 168.77 \text{ kW}$$ **(ii) Total energy released in 120 s:** $$E_{total} = \int_0^{120} \alpha t^2 \, dt = \frac{\alpha t^3}{3} \Big|_0^{120}$$ $$E_{total} = \frac{0.01172 \times (120)^3}{3}$$ $$E_{total} = \frac{0.01172 \times 1{,}728{,}000}{3}$$ $$E_{total} = \frac{20{,}252.16}{3} = 6750.72 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{total} = 6.75 \text{ MJ}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Standard Fire Curve (ASTM E119 / ISO 834):** The standard fire curve is a **time-temperature relationship** used to test and rate the fire resistance of structural elements (beams, columns, slabs, walls). It represents a fully developed cellulosic fire in a compartment. **Mathematical Form:** $$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ where: - $T(t)$ = average furnace gas temperature (°C) - $T_0$ = ambient initial temperature ($20$ °C) - $t$ = elapsed time (minutes) - $345$ = empirical constant (°C) - $8$ = empirical time coefficient (per minute) **Diagrammatic Representation (Schematic):** ``` T(°C) 1200| .───────── | .────` 1000| .───` | .──` 800| .───` ← Approx. 800°C at 30 min | .─` 600| .─` ← Flashover threshold | .─` 400| .─` ← Auto-ignition of cellulosics | .─` 200| .` 0|.` ← ISO 834 starts at 20°C └───────────────────────────────────→ t (min) 0 30 60 90 120 150 180 ``` **Computation at t = 45 min:** $$T(45) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 45 + 1)$$ $$T(45) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(360 + 1)$$ $$T(45) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(361)$$ $$\log_{10}(361) = 2.5575$$ $$T(45) = 20 + 345 \times 2.5575$$ $$T(45) = 20 + 882.34$$ $$\boxed{T(45) = 902.34 \text{ °C}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS (Read before writing the exam!)** > > 1. **Wrong class identification:** Students often confuse **Class E (Electrical)** with **Class C (Flammable Gas)**. Recall: Class C is for *gases like propane and methane*; Class E (now merged with Class C in some NFPA revisions) is for *energised electrical equipment*. If the question says "live wire fire" → **Class E / Class C (energised)**, agent = **CO₂**. > > 2. **Using water on metal fires:** Writing "use water to extinguish magnesium fire" is an **automatic 0.5–1 mark deduction** and possibly full rejection depending on the examiner. **Always write: NaCl-based dry powder.** > > 3. **Forgetting $T_0$ in the standard fire curve:** Many students write $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ **without adding $T_0 = 20$ °C**. This gives $T(0) = 0$ °C instead of $20$ °C → **lose 1 mark**. > > 4. **Unit errors in $E_{total}$:** Computing total energy and forgetting to convert from kJ to MJ when comparing to fuel loads (which are in MJ/m²) → lose 1 mark. > > 5. **Mis-stating flashover temperature:** Flashover is **500 °C – 600 °C at the ceiling layer**, NOT 1000 °C. The 1000 °C value is the *furnace gas temperature*, not the flashover criterion. > > 6. **t²-fire $\alpha$ values:** Do NOT memorise only one value. Examiners *deliberately* test all four growth coefficients. **Memorize all four.** > > 7. **Skipping detector types:** In Stage descriptions, always mention *which detector type activates* — this is asked for **2–3 marks** every year. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1, Topic: Fire Classification & Enclosure Fire Stages** ### Fire Classification (Memorize All 5 Classes) - **Class A:** Wood, paper, cloth → **Water** (cooling). - **Class B:** Flammable liquids (petrol, oil) → **Foam / CO₂ / DCP** (smothering). - **Class C:** Flammable gases (LPG, methane) → **DCP** + shut-off source. - **Class D:** Combustible metals (Mg, Na) → **Special Class D powder** — NEVER water. - **Class E (Electrical) / Class K (Kitchen oils)** → **CO₂ / Wet chemical**. ### Enclosure Fire Stages - **Incipient** → $20$–$50$ °C, no flame, aerosols only. - **Smoldering** → $50$–$300$ °C, **HIGH CO**, smoke detectors trigger. - **Flame (Growth)** → $300$–$700$ °C, $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$, **$t^2$ model**. - **Heat (Fully Developed)** → $700$–$1100$ °C, **flashover** at $500$–$600$ °C ceiling. ### Must-Know Formulas - Standard Fire Curve: $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ — **always include $T_0 = 20$ °C**. - t²-Growth: $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$. - Total energy: $E_{total} = \frac{\alpha t^3}{3}$. - Time to flashover: $t_{fo} = \sqrt{Q_{fo}/\alpha}$. ### Must-Know Numerical Constants - $T_0 = 20$ °C (ambient). - Flashover ceiling temperature = $500$–$600$ °C. - Steel critical temperature = $550$ °C (loses 50% strength). - Slow growth $\alpha = 0.00293$; Medium $= 0.01172$; Fast $= 0.0469$; Ultra-fast $= 0.1876$ kW/s². ### Examiner Golden Rules - Always state **agent + mechanism + reason** (e.g., "Water cools below ignition temperature due to high latent heat 2260 kJ/kg"). - For t² problems, show **all 4 steps**: substitution → multiplication → unit conversion → comparison with table. - For fire curve problems, **never forget $T_0 = 20$ °C**. - Mention **flashover** explicitly when discussing the transition to the heat stage. > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — CLASSIFICATION OF FIRE & ENCLOSURE FIRE DEVELOPMENT**

topics.title: Factors Influencing Fire Development in an Enclosure. Diffusion Flames - Zones of Combustion, Characteristics of Diffusion Flames, Laminar and Turbulent Jet Flames, Flame from Natural Fires, Buoyant Plume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Fire Development in an Enclosure. Diffusion Flames - Zones of Combustion, Characteristics of Diffusion Flames, Laminar and Turbulent Jet Flames, Flame from Natural Fires, Buoyant Plume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Factors Influencing Fire Development in an Enclosure

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Fire development within an enclosed compartment is governed by a coupled interaction of **fuel characteristics**, **ventilation conditions**, and the **thermal/physical properties of the bounding enclosure** (walls, ceiling, floor). The process transitions from a fuel-controlled (pre-flashover) regime to a ventilation-controlled (post-flashover) regime, ultimately producing a **fully developed fire**.

#### Key Influencing Factors (Classified)

| Category | Parameter | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Fuel | Heat of combustion, mass loss rate, pyrolysis kinetics | Determines HRR magnitude |
| Ventilation | Opening area, opening geometry, location | Controls oxygen ingress |
| Enclosure | Thermal inertia $k\rho c$, wall thickness | Governs heat feedback |
| Geometry | Floor area, ceiling height, aspect ratio | Affects plume dynamics |
| Ignition | Location, source power, duration | Sets initial seed fire |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Inertia Concept:** Enclosure walls act as a *heat sink*. Materials with low thermal inertia (e.g., **gypsum board**, $k\rho c \approx 0.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{J}^2/(\text{K}^2 \cdot \text{m}^4 \cdot \text{s})$) allow rapid heat build-up, accelerating flashover. Materials with high thermal inertia (e.g., **concrete**) delay flashover but can sustain post-flashover fires longer.

#### Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine a closed kitchen with a gas stove left on. The fire is initially small (fuel-controlled). As walls absorb heat and reflect it back, the room becomes an "oven" (heat feedback). When the accumulated heat auto-ignites all combustible items simultaneously — that instantaneous transition is **flashover**, analogous to a pressure cooker whistle: a critical threshold beyond which the fire is no longer dependent on the original fuel.

---

### 1.2 Diffusion Flames — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Diffusion Flame:** A non-premixed flame in which the **fuel and oxidizer are initially separated** and mixing occurs primarily by **molecular and turbulent diffusion** prior to combustion. Combustion is therefore limited by the rate at which fuel vapor and oxygen intermingle at the reaction zone, rather than by the overall stoichiometry.

**Contrasting Premixed vs. Diffusion Flames:**

| Property | Premixed Flame | Diffusion Flame |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Mixing | Pre-mixed before flame | Mixing & reaction coupled |
| Color | Blue (lean) / uniform | Luminous yellow (sooty) |
| Speed | High (deflagration possible) | Lower |
| Typical Example | Bunsen burner air-adjusted | Candle, pool fire, jet flame |

#### Conceptual Analogy
> A diffusion flame is like two rivers — fuel and air — meeting at a junction. Combustion happens only at the **interface** where they mix. The flame's *height* is dictated by how far the fuel must travel (by diffusion) to find enough oxygen to burn completely.

---

### 1.3 Zones of Combustion in a Diffusion Flame

A buoyant diffusion flame (e.g., candle) consists of three distinct zones:

1. **Fuel-Rich Core (Inner Zone):** Unburnt fuel vapor + pyrolysis gases. No oxygen. Temperature is low.
2. **Reaction Zone (Annular Flame Sheet):** The luminous, sooty, high-temperature zone where stoichiometric combustion occurs. Peak temperature reaches **~1200 K to 1500 K** for hydrocarbon diffusion flames.
3. **Oxidizer-Rich Outer Zone (Entrainment Zone):** Excess air surrounds the flame; completes oxidation of CO and soot particles.

> [!TIP]
> The luminous yellow color of a candle is due to **incandescent soot particles** radiating in the fuel-rich core. In microgravity or highly-ventilated conditions, the flame turns blue (no soot formation).

---

### 1.4 Characteristics of Diffusion Flames

- **Stoichiometric Surface:** A virtual surface where fuel and oxygen are in exact stoichiometric ratio.
- **Flame Height ($L_f$):** Distance from burner rim to the tip where fuel is fully consumed.
- **Lift-off Height:** For jet flames, the distance between the nozzle exit and the visible flame base.
- **Soot Formation:** Strongly depends on the **C/O ratio** and residence time.
- **Radiative Fraction ($\chi_r$):** Fraction of total HRR released as thermal radiation. **Pool fires: $\chi_r \approx 0.2$ to 0.4**; **turbulent jet flames: $\chi_r \approx 0.1$ to 0.2**.

---

### 1.5 Laminar vs. Turbulent Jet Flames

> [!NOTE]
> **Jet Flame:** A diffusion flame established by a momentum-driven jet of fuel issuing from a nozzle into a quiescent ambient oxidizer.

**Reynolds Number Criterion:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho u D}{\mu} = \frac{uD}{\nu}$$

- **Laminar Jet Flame:** $Re < 2300$. Smooth, conical, steady, laminar. Flame length scales linearly with fuel flow rate.
- **Turbulent Jet Flame:** $Re > 2300$. Wrinkled, fluctuating flame sheet; flame length scales as $(Q^{0.4})$ approximately.

---

### 1.6 Flame from Natural Fires

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Fire Flame:** A buoyancy-dominated diffusion flame fueled by a combustible liquid or solid (e.g., pool fire, crib fire, forest fire). Velocity is governed by **buoyancy**, not by the issuing momentum.

Characteristic features:
- **Quasi-steady burning** under constant ventilation.
- **Time-dependent growth** during the growth phase (pre-flashover).
- **Mass burning rate $\dot{m}''$** for liquids follows: $\dot{m}'' \propto (k\rho c / \Delta H_v)$ (classical classical result).

---

### 1.7 Buoyant Plume

> [!NOTE]
> **Buoyant Plume:** A column of hot combustion gases rising from a fire source driven by **buoyancy forces** resulting from density differences between hot gases and the cooler ambient air. It entrains ambient air as it rises and grows in width and decreases in velocity.

The plume is the **conduit** that carries heat and smoke to upper compartment layers and ultimately to the ceiling, where it spreads radially as a **ceiling jet**.

#### Conceptual Analogy
> Picture boiling water in a pot. Steam rises as a column, drags surrounding air with it, and widens as it ascends — a buoyant plume behaves identically, but the "steam" is hot combustion products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant for Air at 20°C:**
> - Density: $\rho_\infty = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Specific heat: $c_p = 1.0 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
> - Gravitational constant (volumetric): $g/\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \approx 9.81 \; \text{K·m}^2/\text{J}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fire Development Stages in a Compartment

The timeline of an enclosure fire is conventionally divided into four stages:

1. **Incipient (Ignition) Stage:** Localized seed fire; growth controlled by fuel chemistry and ignition source.
2. **Growth Stage:** $Q \propto t^2$ (t-squared fire model). Fuel-controlled; limited by pyrolysis kinetics.
3. **Fully Developed (Post-Flashover) Stage:** All combustible surfaces participate; ventilation-controlled.
4. **Decay Stage:** Fuel consumption exceeds replenishment; HRR declines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flashover Criterion (Waterman):** Flashover occurs when the upper gas layer temperature reaches **500°C to 600°C**, or when the incident radiative heat flux at floor level reaches approximately **20 kW/m²**.

---

### 2.2 The t-Squared Fire Growth Model

$$Q(t) = \alpha t^2$$

where:
