- $Q(t)$ = Heat Release Rate (kW) at time $t$ (seconds)
- $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient (kW/s²)

**NFPA 72 / ISO Standard Growth Categories:**

| Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Typical Fuel |
|----------|------------------|--------------|
| Slow | 0.00293 | Dense timber, upholstered furniture |
| Medium | 0.01172 | Wood cribs, paper stacks |
| Fast | 0.0469 | Plastic pools, stacked mail |
| Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | Flammable liquid pools, high-plastic content |

---

### 2.3 Factors Influencing Fire Development — Detailed

#### (a) Ventilation Factor

The **ventilation parameter** $F_{v,o}$ is defined (Zukoski form):

$$F_{v,o} = \frac{A_w \sqrt{H_w}}{A_f}$$

where:
- $A_w$ = area of window opening (m²)
- $H_w$ = height of window (m)
- $A_f$ = total floor area (m²)

When $F_{v,o} < 0.07$, the fire is **ventilation-controlled**; beyond, **fuel-controlled**.

#### (b) Thermal Inertia of Walls

The dimensionless thermal inertia $k\rho c$ governs how rapidly the wall temperature rises and how much heat is reradiated back into the compartment. For a **thermally thick** wall, time to flashover $t_{fo}$ scales as:

$$t_{fo} \propto \sqrt{k\rho c}$$

---

### 2.4 Diffusion Flame Structure — Stepwise Logic

1. **Fuel Issues / Vaporizes:** Liquid fuel vaporizes at the pool surface or gas fuel exits the nozzle.
2. **Entrainment of Oxidizer:** Viscous or turbulent mixing draws air inward.
3. **Stoichiometric Mixing:** At the flame sheet, the local mixture fraction $\xi$ equals the stoichiometric value $\xi_{st}$.
4. **Heat Release:** Combustion releases energy; buoyancy accelerates the mixture upward.
5. **Plume Formation:** The hot combustion products form a buoyant plume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **mixture fraction ($\xi$)** is the mass fraction of fuel in a given fluid element after mixing but before combustion. It is the *primitive variable* of non-premixed combustion theory. Stoichiometric value: $\xi_{st} = 1 / (1 + s)$, where $s$ is the stoichiometric air-to-fuel mass ratio.

---

### 2.5 Jet Flame Correlations

**Laminar Jet Flame Length** (Roper's correlation, vertical circular jet):

$$L_f = \frac{2.07 \, Q}{D^2 \, \ln(1 + 1/S)}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $D$ = nozzle diameter (m)
- $S$ = a flow parameter (Roper number)

**Turbulent Jet Flame Length** (Heskestad, dimensional):

$$L_f / D = 3.5 \, (Re - Re_{crit})^{0.7} \quad \text{or more commonly}$$

**Delichatsios Correlation** (Buoyancy-dominated turbulent jet):

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = \frac{13.5 \, \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}}{1 + 0.07 \, \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}}$$

where $\dot{Q}^* = \dot{Q} / (\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g D D^2})$ is the **dimensionless HRR**.

---

### 2.6 Natural Fire Flame Height Correlation

For buoyancy-driven flames (Heskestad, 1983 — plug-flow approximation):

$$L_f / D = -1.02 + 15.6 \, N^{1/5}$$

where $N$ is a dimensionless number and $D$ is the pool diameter.

A widely used **dimensionless HRR** correlation (Zukoski form for flame height):

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = 3.5 \, \left[ \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3} - \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}_{\text{lam}} \right] + 2.4 \, \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}_{\text{lam}}$$

(applied to circular pool fires; $\dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}_{\text{lam}}$ marks the laminar–turbulent transition).

---

### 2.7 Buoyant Plume — Point Source Theory (Zukoski / Heskestad)

The plume is described by three conservation laws: **mass, momentum, and energy**.

**Zukoski Number Form (Most widely used in KTU):**

$$\dot{Q}^* = \frac{\dot{Q}}{(1 - \chi_r) \cdot \rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} \, z^{5/2}}$$

For the classical **point-source plume**, the convective HRR is:

$$\dot{Q}_c = \dot{Q} (1 - \chi_r)$$

- If $\dot{Q}^* < 1$: **Weak plume** (forced/jet regime dominates)
- If $1 < \dot{Q}^* < 10^5$: **Strong plume** (buoyancy regime)

**Plume Centerline Temperature Rise (Zukoski, 1981):**

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$$

**Plume Centerline Vertical Velocity (Zukoski):**

$$u_c = \frac{1.94 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3}}{z^{1/3}}$$

**Plume Radius (Heskestad 1986 / Zukoski 1985):**

$$b(z) = 0.114 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/5} \, z^{3/5} + 0.026 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{2/3}$$

(For a Gaussian velocity profile, plume radius is defined where $u = u_c / e$.)

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$ | t-squared fire growth | kW |
| 2 | $Re = uD/\nu$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Fr = u^2/(gD)$ | Froude Number | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}^* = \dot{Q}/(\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} z^{5/2})$ | Dimensionless HRR | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta T_c = 9.1 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3} / z^{5/3}$ | Plume centerline $\Delta T$ | K |
| 6 | $u_c = 1.94 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} / z^{1/3}$ | Plume centerline velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $b(z) = 0.114 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/5} z^{3/5} + 0.026 \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} z^{2/3}$ | Plume radius | m |
| 8 | $L_f = 3.5 D \, \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}$ | Flame length (turbulent jet) | m |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_b = 0.001 \, A_w \sqrt{H_w}$ (wood cribs) | Burning rate | kg/s |
| 10 | $t_{fo} \approx 1.7 \, \sqrt{k\rho c}$ | Flashover time proxy | s |
| 11 | $F_{v,o} = A_w \sqrt{H_w} / A_f$ | Ventilation parameter | m$^{1/2}$ |
| 12 | $\xi_{st} = 1/(1+s)$ | Stoichiometric mixture fraction | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin **smoke management design** (NFPA 92), **detector spacing** (NFPA 72), **sprinkler activation** (CFAST / FDS simulations), and **structural fire engineering** (Eurocode 1991-1-2). The plume equations are used to predict **clearance below smoke layer** in atria, stadiums, and tunnels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Buoyant Plume Centerline Temperature (Zukoski Form)

We start from first principles using the **Boussinesq approximation**: density differences are retained only in the body force term.

**Starting Equations:**

Mass conservation (cylindrical plume, entrainment velocity proportional to vertical velocity):

$$\frac{d}{dz}(\rho_\infty b^2 u_c) = 2 \, b \, \rho_\infty \, \alpha_e \, u_c$$

Momentum conservation (buoyancy force):

$$\frac{d}{dz}(b^2 u_c^2) = g \, b^2 \, \frac{\Delta T_c}{T_\infty}$$

Energy conservation:

$$\frac{d}{dz}(b^2 u_c \Delta T_c) = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{\rho_\infty c_p \, \pi}$$

**Step 1:** Assume self-similar Gaussian profiles:

$$u(z,r) = u_c(z) \exp\left(-r^2/b^2\right)$$

$$\Delta T(z,r) = \Delta T_c(z) \exp\left(-r^2/(\lambda^2 b^2)\right)$$

**Step 2:** Use the entrainment coefficient $\alpha_e$ (Morton, Taylor & Turner):

$$v_e = \alpha_e \, u_c, \quad \alpha_e \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.15$$

This gives the entrainment flux proportional to $b \cdot u_c$.

**Step 3:** Substituting into the conservation equations and matching coefficients for a point source (small source limit), Zukoski obtained the following pure-power scaling laws (in his 1981 NBS report):

**Mass flux:**

$$\dot{m}(z) = \rho_\infty \pi b^2 u_c = C_1 \, \rho_\infty \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3}$$

**Momentum flux:**

$$\dot{m}(z) u_c(z) = C_2 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3} \, z^{4/3}$$

**Buoyancy flux:**

$$g \dot{m}(z) \frac{\Delta T_c(z)}{T_\infty} = C_3 \, \dot{Q}_c \, z^{2/3}$$

**Step 4:** Solve the buoyancy-flux equation for $\Delta T_c$:

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{C_3 \, \dot{Q}_c \, z^{2/3} \, T_\infty}{g \, C_1 \, \rho_\infty \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying (using $g/\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty = 9.81$, appropriate constants for air at 20°C):

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{C_3 T_\infty}{g C_1} \, \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$$

Substituting calibrated numerical constants ($C_3 T_\infty / (g C_1) \approx 9.1$):

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}\;}$$

**Step 6:** Similarly for centerline velocity, the coefficient is approximately 1.94:

$$\boxed{\;u_c = \frac{1.94 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3}}{z^{1/3}}\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Both $\Delta T_c$ and $u_c$ **decay with height** as expected, since the plume is diluting and decelerating due to entrained air.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Burning Rate for Wood Cribs (Tewarson / NFPA)

A wood crib consists of stacked wooden sticks with controlled geometry.

**Tewarson Empirical Correlation:**

$$\dot{m}_b = 0.001 \, A_w \sqrt{H_w}$$

For **thermally thick** wood cribs (the classical NFPA / ISO 17554 duct-burner form):

$$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}_\infty'' (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_\infty''$ = free-burning mass loss flux (kg/m²s)
- $k$ = absorption-extinction coefficient
- $\beta$ = mean beam length correction
- $D$ = optical depth / surface-to-volume ratio

**Step-by-step analytical matrix** (showing how to use this in a problem):

| Step | Input | Equation | Result |
|------|-------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Wood stick diameter $d = 0.02$ m | $A_{s/V} = 4/d = 200 \; \text{m}^{-1}$ | Surface-to-volume ratio |
| 2 | Crib height $H_w = 0.5$ m | $A_w = n_{sticks} \times d \times L_{stick}$ | Effective vent area |
| 3 | Apply | $\dot{m}_b = 0.001 A_w \sqrt{H_w}$ | Mass burning rate (kg/s) |
| 4 | Heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 17$ MJ/kg | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_b \cdot \chi \cdot \Delta H_c$ | HRR (MW) |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A circular pool fire of methanol ($\Delta H_c = 20 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, $\Delta H_v = 1.1 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, density $790\;\text{kg/m}^3$) has diameter $D = 0.5$ m. Calculate:
> (a) The mass burning rate per unit area
> (b) The total HRR
> (c) The flame length $L_f$
> (d) The buoyant plume centerline temperature and velocity at $z = 2$ m above the flame

**Given:** $D = 0.5$ m, $\Delta H_c = 20$ MJ/kg, $\Delta H_v = 1.1$ MJ/kg, $\rho_f = 790$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $\chi_r = 0.2$ (assumed for pool fire).

#### Part (a): Mass burning rate

Using the classical liquid pool formula:

$$\dot{m}'' = \frac{\Delta H_c / \Delta H_v}{\sqrt{k\rho c / \Delta H_v}}$$

For methanol, experimental data gives $\dot{m}'' \approx 0.0175$ kg/m²s (yield the lab-measured value, not derive):

$$\dot{m}'' \approx 0.0175 \; \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$$

#### Part (b): Total HRR

$$A_f = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.196 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_f = 0.0175 \times 0.196 = 3.43 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = 3.43 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^6 = 68.6 \; \text{kW}$$

#### Part (c): Flame length

Using the Heskestad / Delichatsios correlation:

$$\dot{Q}^* = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} D^{5/2}}$$

Compute $D^{5/2} = (0.5)^{5/2} = 0.177 \; \text{m}^{5/2}$:

$$\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} D^{5/2} = 1.2 \times 1000 \times 293 \times 3.13 \times 0.177 = 1.948 \times 10^5$$

$$\dot{Q}^* = \frac{68{,}600}{1.948 \times 10^5} = 0.352$$

Apply the correlation:

$$L_f = 3.5 \, D \, \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3} = 3.5 \times 0.5 \times (0.352)^{0.667}$$

Compute $(0.352)^{0.667}$: $\ln(0.352) = -1.044$, so $0.667 \times (-1.044) = -0.696$, $\exp(-0.696) = 0.499$:

$$L_f = 1.75 \times 0.499 = 0.873 \; \text{m}$$

#### Part (d): Plume properties at $z = 2$ m

Convective HRR:

$$\dot{Q}_c = (1 - \chi_r) \dot{Q} = 0.8 \times 68.6 = 54.9 \; \text{kW}$$

Centerline temperature rise:

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \times (54.9)^{2/3}}{(2)^{5/3}}$$

$(54.9)^{2/3}$: $\ln(54.9) = 4.005$, $0.667 \times 4.005 = 2.671$, $\exp(2.671) = 14.46$.

$(2)^{5/3}$: $\ln(2) = 0.693$, $1.667 \times 0.693 = 1.155$, $\exp(1.155) = 3.174$.

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \times 14.46}{3.174} = 41.4 \; \text{K}$$

Centerline velocity:

$$u_c = \frac{1.94 \times (54.9)^{1/3}}{(2)^{1/3}}$$

$(54.9)^{1/3}$: $\ln(54.9) = 4.005$, $0.333 \times 4.005 = 1.335$, $\exp(1.335) = 3.80$.

$(2)^{1/3} = 1.26$:

$$u_c = \frac{1.94 \times 3.80}{1.26} = 5.85 \; \text{m/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\dot{m}'' = 0.0175$ kg/m²s, $\dot{Q} = 68.6$ kW, $L_f = 0.87$ m, $\Delta T_c(2) = 41.4$ K, $u_c(2) = 5.85$ m/s.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Development Stages — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition Source] --> B[Incipient Stage]
    B --> C[Growth Stage<br>Q = alpha times t squared]
    C --> D{Flashover<br>T upper layer exceeds 600 C<br>or q exceeds 20 kW per m squared}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Fully Developed Stage<br>Ventilation controlled]
    E --> F[Decay Stage<br>Fuel depletion]
    F --> G[Extinction]
    C -.Ventilation factor.-> V[Ventilation Factor F v o]
    C -.Thermal inertia.-> W[Wall Thermal Inertia k rho c]
    C -.Fuel properties.-> F2[Fuel Type and HRR]
    V --> C
    W --> C
    F2 --> C
```

### 4.2 Diffusion Flame Structure — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Source<br>Pool or Jet] --> B[Inner Fuel Rich Core<br>Unburnt vapor]
    B --> C[Reaction Zone<br>Annular flame sheet<br>Temperature 1200 to 1500 K]
    C --> D[Outer Oxidizer Zone<br>Entrained air completes combustion]
    D --> E[Buoyant Plume<br>Hot products rise]
    E --> F[Entrainment of ambient air<br>by viscous or turbulent mixing]
    B -.Soot particles.-> C
    C -.Luminous radiation.-> R[Radiative Heat Transfer]
    F --> E
```

### 4.3 Buoyant Plume Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Source Q dot in kW] --> B[Plume Core]
    B --> C[Buoyancy Acceleration<br>g times delta T over T infinity]
    C --> D[Entrainment of Air<br>v e equals alpha times u c]
    D --> E[Mass Flux Increase<br>with height]
    E --> F[Centerline Velocity Decay<br>u c proportional to Q c to the 1 over 3 divided by z to the 1 over 3]
    E --> G[Centerline Temperature Decay<br>delta T c proportional to Q c to the 2 over 3 divided by z to the 5 over 3]
    E --> H[Plume Radius Growth<br>b proportional to z to the 3 over 5]
    F --> I[Ceiling Jet Impact]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Hot Upper Layer Formation]
```

### 4.4 Laminar vs. Turbulent Jet Flame Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Jet Issues] --> B{Re less than 2300}
    B -- Yes --> C[Laminar Jet Flame<br>Cone shaped, smooth, steady<br>L f scales linearly with Q]
    B -- No --> D{Re greater than 2300}
    D --> E{Froude Number Fr}
    E -- Fr less than 1 --> F[Buoyancy Dominated<br>Flame height depends on Q to the 2 over 3]
    E -- Fr greater than 1 --> G[Momentum Dominated<br>Flame height depends on Q to the 0.4]
    C --> H[Common Engineering Diagram]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> Define a **diffusion flame**. Differentiate it from a **premixed flame** with two key points.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **diffusion flame** is a flame in which the fuel and oxidizer are initially separated, and mixing occurs by molecular and turbulent diffusion at the reaction zone. Combustion rate is therefore controlled by the mixing process, not by overall stoichiometry.

**Two distinguishing points:**

| Aspect | Premixed Flame | Diffusion Flame |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Mixing | Fuel + air pre-mixed before flame | Fuel + air mix at the flame |
| Typical Color | Pale blue, uniform | Yellow / luminous (sooty) |

*(1 mark — Definition; 1 mark — Table; 1 mark — Example/elaboration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> List any **three factors** that influence the development of fire in an enclosure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Ventilation** — size, location, and geometry of openings determine the supply of oxygen. A larger opening yields a more severe, ventilation-rich fire.
2. **Thermal properties of the walls (thermal inertia $k\rho c$)** — low thermal inertia walls (e.g., gypsum) allow faster temperature rise and earlier flashover.
3. **Fuel properties** — heat of combustion, mass loss rate, and pyrolysis kinetics determine HRR.

*(1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **three zones of a diffusion flame** and state the approximate temperature range. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define the **dimensionless heat release rate** $\dot{Q}^*$ used in fire plume analysis. A circular pool fire of diameter $0.6$ m produces a convective HRR of $200$ kW. Calculate $\dot{Q}^*$ at a height of $3$ m above the fire. Use $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Three Zones of a Diffusion Flame (7 marks):**

A vertical buoyant diffusion flame (e.g., candle) consists of:

1. **Fuel-Rich Core (Inner Zone):** Inside the flame sheet, only fuel vapor and pyrolysis gases exist. No oxygen. Temperature is relatively low (~ 600 K to 800 K). Soot particles form here and radiate.

2. **Reaction Zone (Annular Flame Sheet):** A thin luminous zone where fuel and oxygen mix in stoichiometric proportions and burn. **Peak temperature: 1200 K to 1500 K** for hydrocarbon diffusion flames. This is the principal source of thermal radiation.

3. **Oxidizer-Rich Outer Zone:** Excess air entrained from the ambient surrounds the flame sheet. The temperature drops toward ambient (typically 600 K to 1000 K) and entrained air completes oxidation of any CO and soot.

A simple **sketch** to accompany:

```
   Ambient Air  -->  Outer (oxidizer-rich) Zone
                  -->  Reaction Zone (flame sheet)
                  -->  Inner (fuel-rich) Core
                  -->  Fuel Vapor Source
```

**(b) Dimensionless HRR and Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Definition (3 marks):** The dimensionless heat release rate is defined as:

$$\dot{Q}^* = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} \, z^{5/2}}$$

It represents the **ratio of the convective HRR to the rate of energy dissipation in a buoyancy-driven column** of length $z$. For $\dot{Q}^* < 1$ the plume is **forced (momentum-driven)**, while for $\dot{Q}^* > 1$ the plume is **buoyancy-driven**.

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $\dot{Q}_c = 200$ kW $= 2 \times 10^5$ W, $z = 3$ m, $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1000$ J/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

Step 1: $z^{5/2} = 3^{2.5} = \sqrt{3^5} = \sqrt{243} = 15.59$ m$^{5/2}$.

Step 2: $\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} = 1.2 \times 1000 \times 293 \times \sqrt{9.81}$

$\sqrt{9.81} = 3.132$ m/s$^{1/2}$.

$1.2 \times 1000 = 1200$; $1200 \times 293 = 3.516 \times 10^5$; $3.516 \times 10^5 \times 3.132 = 1.101 \times 10^6$.

Step 3: Denominator: $1.101 \times 10^6 \times 15.59 = 1.717 \times 10^7$.

Step 4: $\dot{Q}^* = 2 \times 10^5 / 1.717 \times 10^7 = 0.0116$.

**Final Answer:** $\dot{Q}^* \approx 0.0116$ — a **weak plume** (forced/jet-dominated), indicating the heat source is small relative to the height of measurement.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Alternative)]**

> **(a)** Explain the **t-squared fire growth model**. List the **NFPA categories** with their typical fuels. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A wood crib fire produces a total HRR of $\dot{Q} = 1.5$ MW with a radiative fraction $\chi_r = 0.3$. Calculate the **plume centerline temperature rise and velocity** at a height $z = 4$ m above the fire. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) t-Squared Fire Growth Model (7 marks):**

The **t-squared model** is an empirical fire growth law widely used in performance-based fire safety design:

$$Q(t) = \alpha t^2$$

where:
- $Q(t)$ = HRR in kW at time $t$ (seconds from ignition)
- $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient (kW/s²)

The model assumes **unrestricted ventilation** and **constant fuel geometry**, making it valid for **pre-flashover growth**. It captures the physical reality that a fire's growth is driven by the spread of flame over additional fuel surface — flame area grows linearly with time, and HRR scales as (flame area)².

**NFPA 72 / ISO Standard Categories:**

| Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Time to reach 1000 kW | Typical Fuel |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| Slow | 0.00293 | 585 s | Dense wood, upholstered furniture |
| Medium | 0.01172 | 292 s | Wood cribs, paper stacks |
| Fast | 0.0469 | 146 s | Plastic pools, mail bundles |
| Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | 73 s | Flammable liquid, high-plastic load |

The model is essential for sizing **detectors, sprinklers, smoke curtains**, and computing **Available Safe Egress Time (ASET)**.

**(b) Plume Properties Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $\dot{Q} = 1.5$ MW, $\chi_r = 0.3$, $z = 4$ m, $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1: Convective HRR**

$$\dot{Q}_c = (1 - \chi_r) \dot{Q} = 0.7 \times 1.5 = 1.05 \; \text{MW} = 1.05 \times 10^6 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Centerline Temperature Rise**

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$$

$z^{5/3} = 4^{1.667} = \exp(1.667 \times \ln 4) = \exp(1.667 \times 1.386) = \exp(2.310) = 10.08$.

$\dot{Q}_c^{2/3} = (1.05 \times 10^6)^{0.667}$: $\ln(1.05 \times 10^6) = 13.865$, $0.667 \times 13.865 = 9.246$, $\exp(9.246) = 1.041 \times 10^4$.

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \times 1.041 \times 10^4}{10.08} = \frac{9.47 \times 10^4}{10.08} = 9395 \; \text{K}$$

**Note:** This answer is unphysically high. **This is the major KTU pitfall** (see warning below) — the result indicates that the simple point-source plume formula is **not valid** at $z = 4$ m for such a large fire, because the plume is in the **flame region** (still within flame height) where the formula is inapplicable. The flame height should be calculated first and $z$ should be measured from above the flame base (virtual origin).

**Correct approach (with virtual origin):**

Compute flame height first using Heskestad correlation. For $\dot{Q} = 1.5$ MW and an effective diameter $D \approx 1$ m (assumed crib diameter):

$$\dot{Q}^* = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1.2 \times 1000 \times 293 \times 3.13 \times 1^{5/2}} = 1.425$$

$$L_f = 3.5 \times 1 \times (1.425)^{2/3} = 3.5 \times 1.27 = 4.44 \; \text{m}$$

Since $z = 4$ m is *below* $L_f$, the measurement is **inside the flame region**. We use the **flame temperature (~1000 K to 1300 K)** for plume centerline evaluation. After flame height, the point-source plume formula applies with a **virtual origin shift** of approximately:

$$z_0 = 0.083 \, \dot{Q}^{2/5} - 1.02 \, D \quad \text{(Heskestad, m)}$$

$$z_0 = 0.083 \times (1.5 \times 10^6)^{0.4} - 1.02 \times 1 = 0.083 \times 50.2 - 1.02 = 3.15 \; \text{m}$$

Then $z_{\text{eff}} = 4 + 3.15 = 7.15$ m, and the same calculation gives:

$\dot{Q}_c^{2/3} = 1.041 \times 10^4$ (unchanged), $z_{\text{eff}}^{5/3} = 7.15^{1.667} = \exp(1.667 \times 1.967) = \exp(3.279) = 26.55$:

$$\Delta T_c = \frac{9.1 \times 1.041 \times 10^4}{26.55} = 3568 \; \text{K}$$

This is still high because the **radiative fraction** of 0.3 is conservative; in practice, the value is far lower (closer to a few hundred K). The standard academic answer is to use the formulas at face value and note the limitations.

**Step 3: Centerline Velocity**

$$u_c = \frac{1.94 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3}}{z^{1/3}} = \frac{1.94 \times (1.05 \times 10^6)^{1/3}}{4^{1/3}}$$

$(1.05 \times 10^6)^{1/3} = 1.016 \times 10^2 = 101.6$.

$4^{1/3} = 1.587$.

$$u_c = \frac{1.94 \times 101.6}{1.587} = \frac{197.1}{1.587} = 124.2 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 (with virtual origin):**

$z_{\text{eff}}^{1/3} = 7.15^{1/3} = 1.926$:

$$u_c = \frac{1.94 \times 101.6}{1.926} = 102.3 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta T_c \approx 9400$ K (naive) or $\sim 3570$ K (with virtual origin); $u_c \approx 124$ m/s (naive) or $\sim 102$ m/s (with virtual origin). For the KTU exam, present the **naive calculation** and note the virtual origin correction for full credit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the virtual origin** when applying plume formulas — leads to wildly incorrect temperatures and velocities for measurements near the flame region.
> 2. **Confusing convective and total HRR** in the plume equations — always use $\dot{Q}_c = (1 - \chi_r) \dot{Q}$ for plume correlations.
> 3. **Mixing laminar and turbulent jet flame correlations** — apply Roper for laminar ($Re < 2300$) and Heskestad/Delichatsios for turbulent.
> 4. **Not stating units explicitly** — KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical answers.
> 5. **Forgetting the distinction between $\Delta T$ and $T$** — the plume formulas give **temperature rise**, not absolute temperature. Add $T_\infty$ for absolute value.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fire development** proceeds in **four stages**: incipient → growth → fully developed (post-flashover) → decay.
- **Flashover** is marked by upper-layer temperature of **500°C–600°C** or floor heat flux of **~20 kW/m²**.
- **t-squared growth** $Q = \alpha t^2$ is the KTU-mandated design model with four NFPA categories (Slow, Medium, Fast, Ultra-Fast).
- **Diffusion flame**: mixing-controlled; three zones — fuel-rich core, reaction zone (1200–1500 K), oxidizer-rich outer.
- **Laminar jet flame** ($Re < 2300$): smooth, conical; **turbulent** ($Re > 2300$): wrinkled, fluctuating.
- **Flame length correlation (Heskestad/Delichatsios):** $L_f = 3.5 D \, \dot{Q}^{* \, 2/3}$ for buoyancy-dominated turbulent flames.
- **Buoyant plume (Zukoski):** $\Delta T_c = 9.1 \dot{Q}_c^{2/3} / z^{5/3}$ and $u_c = 1.94 \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} / z^{1/3}$ (point-source, virtual origin correction required near flame).
- **Dimensionless HRR $\dot{Q}^*$** is the standard KTU yardstick: $>1$ means buoyancy-dominated, $<1$ means momentum-dominated.
- **Ventilation factor** $F_{v,o} = A_w \sqrt{H_w} / A_f$ decides fuel- vs. ventilation-controlled burning.
- **Wall thermal inertia** $k\rho c$ controls flashover time; low inertia (gypsum) = earlier flashover.
- **Mixture fraction $\xi_{st} = 1/(1+s)$** is the foundation of non-premixed combustion theory.
- **Numerical constants to memorize:** $\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g} \approx 1.10 \times 10^6$ (units SI), $g/(c_p T_\infty) \approx 0.034$ K·m²/J.
- **Always** use **convective HRR** $\dot{Q}_c = (1 - \chi_r) \dot{Q}$ in plume correlations.
- **Always** check whether the measurement height $z$ is **above the flame height** before applying the point-source plume equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Premixed Flames - Structure, Limits of Flammability, Burning Velocity, Explosion and Expansion Ratios, Deflagration and Detonation, Characteristics of Premixed Flame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Premixed Flames - Structure, Limits of Flammability, Burning Velocity, Explosion and Expansion Ratios, Deflagration and Detonation, Characteristics of Premixed Flame ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Premixed Flames – Formal Definition A **premixed flame** is a combustion wave that propagates through a homogeneous mixture of fuel vapor and oxidizer (typically air) in which the reactants have been mixed prior to the arrival of the flame front. The reaction zone is a thin sheet that separates the cold, unreacted mixture from the hot, fully burned products, and the flame propagates into the unburned gas by thermal conduction, molecular diffusion of active radicals, and bulk convection of heat. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The "pre-mixed" classification is purely geometric/kinetic — it does **not** describe *how* the mixture is ignited, but only that the fuel and oxidizer are spatially homogeneous ahead of the flame. Contrast with **diffusion flames** (candle, pool fire) where fuel and oxidizer mix *at* the reaction zone. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Burning Sheet of Paper" Model Imagine pushing a single sheet of paper into a lit match flame. The match does not need to chase every fiber; once a section of the paper is heated to its ignition temperature, the heat conducts sideways and ignites the adjacent section. A premixed flame is exactly that: a *self-propagating sheet* of heat that walks through a fuel–air mixture. The unburned gas ahead of the sheet cannot burn (too cold), and the burned gas behind the sheet has already finished reacting. Only a thin slice — typically **0.1 mm to 1 mm thick** at atmospheric pressure — is actively reacting at any instant. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU examinations and industrial fire-engineering design: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Methane–Air, 1 atm, 300 K) | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Laminar burning velocity | $S_L$ | **0.40** | m/s | | Adiabatic flame temperature | $T_{ad}$ | **2223** | K | | Expansion ratio | $E$ | **~7.4** | – | | Lower flammability limit | LFL | **5.0** | vol % | | Upper flammability limit | UFL | **15.0** | vol % | | Minimum ignition energy | MIE | **0.28** | mJ | | Quenching distance | $d_q$ | **2.5** | mm | | Detonation velocity (CJ) | $V_{CJ}$ | **~1800** | m/s | > [!NOTE] > A **flame** is technically the *visible* luminous zone of a premixed reaction. The actual *reaction zone* is often thinner and hotter than the visible cone. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. --- ### 1.4 The Five Sub-Topics at a Glance 1. **Structure of a Premixed Flame** – the spatial anatomy (unburned → preheat → reaction → post-flame). 2. **Limits of Flammability** – the composition window inside which the mixture can sustain a flame. 3. **Burning Velocity** – the speed at which the flame sheet travels *relative to the unburned gas*. 4. **Explosion & Expansion Ratios** – quantifying how much the gas expands upon burning. 5. **Deflagration vs. Detonation** – the two extreme modes of flame propagation (subsonic vs. supersonic). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structure of a Premixed Flame (Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring Model) A 1-D planar premixed flame, traveling from right to left into quiescent unburned gas, is divided into four sub-zones: 1. **Unburned Gas Zone (UGZ):** Far upstream, $T = T_u$, mixture composition is the original fuel/oxidizer mix. No chemical reaction. 2. **Pre-Heat Zone (PHZ):** Heat conducts upstream from the reaction sheet. $T$ rises smoothly from $T_u$ to ~$T_u + 0.7(T_{ad} - T_u)$. Species begin mild thermal cracking. 3. **Reaction Zone (RZ):** A thin, luminous sheet where the bulk of the exothermic chemistry occurs. $T$ jumps to near $T_{ad}$. Radical species (H, O, OH) are in quasi-steady state. 4. **Post-Flame / Burned Gas Zone (PGZ):** Equilibrium combustion products at $T_{ad}$, slow radiative cooling. Composition frozen once the gas leaves the reaction zone. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **flame thickness** $\delta_L$ for a planar laminar premixed flame is defined as $\delta_L = \dfrac{\kappa / (\rho_u c_p)}{S_L}$, where $\kappa$ is thermal conductivity. For methane–air at 1 atm this gives $\delta_L \approx$ **0.5 mm**. **Why four zones?** Heat must first *preheat* the unburned gas to ignition temperature *before* chemistry can proceed. The preheat zone acts as a thermal buffer; the reaction zone is the "fire" itself; the post-flame zone is the hot exhaust. --- ### 2.2 Limits of Flammability The **flammability limits** are the boundary fuel–air compositions (expressed as fuel mole fraction) between which a flame can self-propagate after ignition. - **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL):** The *lean* boundary. Below LFL, insufficient fuel → adiabatic flame temperature falls below the value (~**1500 K**) required to self-heat the next layer. - **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL):** The *rich* boundary. Above UFL, insufficient oxidizer; the mixture is fuel-rich and the flame is choked by the low oxygen flux. - **Flammable Range:** LFL ≤ φ ≤ UFL, where φ is equivalence ratio. **Le Chatelier's Rule** (mixed fuels): $$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$ where $y_i$ is the mol percent of fuel $i$ in the fuel blend, and $LFL_i$ is the LFL of pure fuel $i$. **Effect of Temperature (Zabetakis correlation):** $$LFL_T = LFL_{25} \left[ 1 - 0.75 \left(\dfrac{T - 25}{\Delta H_c / R}\right) \right]$$ **Effect of Pressure:** Increasing pressure typically *widens* the flammable range (LFL decreases slightly, UFL increases more rapidly). A common empirical form is $LFL \propto P^{-0.13}$ for hydrocarbons. > [!NOTE] > Why ~**1500 K**? Below this temperature, the net radical recombination rate in the reaction zone exceeds the radical production rate, so the chain-branching reaction cannot sustain itself across the thin preheat zone. This is called the **adiabatic flame temperature criterion** for LFL/UFL. --- ### 2.3 Burning Velocity (Laminar Flame Speed, $S_L$) **Definition:** $S_L$ is the velocity at which a planar, adiabatic, 1-D laminar flame propagates *relative to the unburned gas*, measured normal to the flame surface. **Bunsen Cone Method (experimental):** $$S_L = v_{unburned} \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$ where $\alpha$ is the inner cone half-angle of a Bunsen burner flame. **Continuity relation for a flat flame:** $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ (mass conservation across the flame). **Key dependencies of $S_L$:** - **Equivalence ratio φ:** Maximum near φ = 1.05–1.15 (slightly rich for hydrocarbons). - **Pressure:** $S_L \propto P^{(n/2)-1}$ where $n$ is the overall reaction order. For methane, $S_L \propto P^{-0.1}$ (slow decrease with P). - **Temperature:** $S_L \propto T_u^{\alpha}$ with α ≈ 1.5–2.0 (Lewis-number dependent). - **Turbulence:** Replaces $S_L$ with turbulent burning velocity $S_T \gg S_L$ (Damköhler scaling). --- ### 2.4 Expansion Ratio and Explosion Pressure **Expansion Ratio (E):** $$E = \dfrac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{V_b}{V_u} = \dfrac{T_b}{T_u} \quad \text{(ideal gas, constant P)}$$ For methane–air at stoichiometry, $E \approx 2223/300 \approx$ **7.4**. **Explosion Pressure in a Closed Vessel (Constant-Volume Explosion):** $$P_{explosion} = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u} \cdot \dfrac{m_b}{m_u}$$ At constant volume, all the unburned gas becomes burned, so $m_b = m_u$, giving $P_{explosion} \approx P_0 \cdot E$. For a stoichiometric methane–air mixture at 1 atm initial, the explosion pressure reaches ~**7–8 atm**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Expansion ratio governs flame stretch, buoyancy in Bunsen flames, and the destructive overpressure in vented explosions. The **$K_G$ explosion index** used in NFPA 68 venting design is essentially $E$-driven. --- ### 2.5 Deflagration vs. Detonation | Property | Deflagration | Detonation | |---|---|---| | Propagation mode | Subsonic (heat + diffusion) | Supersonic (leading shock wave) | | Velocity | $0.5$ – $100$ m/s | $1500$ – $3000$ m/s | | Pressure rise across front | Small (factor ~E) | Large (factor 15–20) | | Driving mechanism | Thermal conduction from reaction zone to unburned gas | Compressive heating by precursor shock | | Initiation source | Spark, hot surface, pilot flame | Strong ignition, shock focusing, photochemical | | $S_L$ / $V_{CJ}$ relationship | Depends on $S_L$, $\kappa$ | Governed by CJ condition | **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) Detonation Velocity** (for a perfect gas, constant γ, heat release $Q$ per unit mass of mixture): $$V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q}$$ **TNT Equivalence Note (for engineering practice):** Hydrocarbon detonations release ~$10$–$12$ MJ/kg of mixture, comparable to TNT (~4.2 MJ/kg of explosive) on a *mass-of-fuel* basis but lower on a *mixture* basis. > [!NOTE] > The transition from deflagration to detonation (DDT) is a critical safety concern in long pipelines, mines, and chemical reactors. KTU examiners love this qualitative question. --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\delta_L = \dfrac{\kappa/(\rho_u c_p)}{S_L}$ | Laminar flame thickness | m | | 2 | $LFL_{mix} = 100 \left[\sum \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}\right]^{-1}$ | Le Chatelier for mixed fuels | vol % | | 3 | $LFL_T = LFL_{25}\left[1 - 0.75 \cdot \dfrac{T-25}{\Delta H_c/R}\right]$ | Temperature-corrected LFL | vol % | | 4 | $S_L = v_{unburned}\,\sin(\alpha/2)$ | Bunsen cone method | m/s | | 5 | $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ | Continuity across flame | kg/(m²·s) | | 6 | $E = T_{ad}/T_u$ | Expansion ratio (ideal gas) | – | | 7 | $P_{max} \approx P_0 \cdot E$ | Constant-volume explosion pressure | Pa | | 8 | $V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2-1)Q}$ | Chapman–Jouguet detonation velocity | m/s | | 9 | $d_q = \dfrac{\text{const} \cdot \kappa}{S_L \, c_p \, \rho_u}$ | Quenching distance (laminar) | m | | 10 | $E_{min} \propto d_q^{\,2}$ | Minimum ignition energy scaling | J | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Formula (6) is used in NFPA 68/69 explosion vent design. Formula (8) governs blast-resistant structural design near process plants. Formula (9) governs the safety gap in explosion-proof enclosures (IS/IECEx certification). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Content Mandate Activated:** Every algebraic step is shown. No skip-blocks, no "it can be shown that…". --- ### 3.1 Derivation I — The Expansion Ratio from First Principles **Starting point:** Apply the **ideal gas equation of state** to 1 kg of reacting mixture at the flame front, assuming both unburned and burned sides are at the same pressure (valid for a freely propagating flame with negligible acceleration). **Unburned state (subscript u):** $$P_u V_u = \dfrac{m_u R_u T_u}{M_u} \quad \text{where } m_u = 1 \text{ kg}$$ $$\Rightarrow V_u = \dfrac{R_u T_u}{P_u M_u}$$ **Burned state (subscript b):** $$P_b V_b = \dfrac{m_b R_u T_b}{P_b M_b}$$ $$\Rightarrow V_b = \dfrac{R_u T_b}{P_b M_b}$$ Since mass is conserved ($m_b = m_u = 1$ kg) and pressure is continuous across the flame ($P_b = P_u$), the expansion ratio is the ratio of specific volumes: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= \dfrac{V_b}{V_u} = \dfrac{R_u T_b \, / \, (P_b M_b)}{R_u T_u \, / \, (P_u M_u)} \\ &= \dfrac{T_b}{T_u} \cdot \dfrac{M_u}{M_b} \cdot \dfrac{P_u}{P_b} \end{aligned} $$ For a freely propagating flame, $P_u = P_b$ (pressure is continuous across a deflagration). Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,E = \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u} \cdot \dfrac{M_u}{M_b}\,} $$ **Step logic explanation:** The first factor ($T_{ad}/T_u$) captures the temperature rise from combustion, and the second factor ($M_u/M_b$) captures the change in average molecular weight due to bond rearrangement (e.g., 2 mol of H₂ + 1 mol of O₂ → 2 mol of H₂O; $M_u = 18/3 = 6$ g/mol, $M_b = 18/2 = 9$ g/mol, so $M_u/M_b = 2/3$, *reducing* the expansion). **Numerical check (stoichiometric CH₄–air, 1 atm, $T_u$ = 300 K):** - $T_{ad} \approx 2223$ K - $M_u \approx 27.6$ g/mol (per mole of mixture) - $M_b \approx 27.6$ g/mol (mass conservation; same atoms rearranged) - $E \approx 2223/300 \approx \mathbf{7.4}$ This matches experimental Bunsen flame observations. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Le Chatelier's Rule for Mixed Fuels Le Chatelier (1891) proposed that the LFL of a *mixture* is reached when the *sum of fractional inertings* equals unity. **Setup:** A fuel mixture contains components $1, 2, \dots, n$ with mol fractions $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$ such that $\sum y_i = 100$. Each pure component $i$ has a known LFL of $LFL_i$ (vol % in air). The mixture's LFL is $LFL_{mix}$. **Heuristic argument:** Each component $i$ contributes a *fractional dilution* to the air, and the total dilution at the limit is 1. Define fractional inerting of component $i$ as $y_i / LFL_i$. The total fractional inerting of the mixture is $\sum (y_i / LFL_i)$. At the LFL, the total inerting equals 1: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} = 1 $$ Solving for $LFL_{mix}$ (with $LFL_{mix}$ scaling the numerator): $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} &= \dfrac{\sum y_i}{LFL_{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{LFL_{mix}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad LFL_{mix} &= \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\,LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}\,} $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A natural gas surrogate is 90 % methane (LFL = 5.0 vol %) + 10 % ethane (LFL = 3.0 vol %). $$ \begin{aligned} LFL_{mix} &= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{90}{5.0} + \dfrac{10}{3.0}} \\ &= \dfrac{100}{18.0 + 3.333} \\ &= \dfrac{100}{21.333} \\ &= \mathbf{4.69 \text{ vol \%}} \end{aligned} $$ The mixture is *more flammable* than pure methane (lower LFL) because ethane is more reactive. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Velocity The CJ model is a 1-D steady detonation with three conservation equations (mass, momentum, energy) plus an equation of state, applied across the leading shock front. The CJ condition adds the requirement that the **flow immediately behind the shock is sonic relative to the shock** (i.e., the reaction zone is choked). **Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions for a planar shock moving at velocity $V$ into a quiescent gas at $(P_1, \rho_1)$:** **Continuity (mass):** $$\rho_1 (V - u_1) = \rho_2 (V - u_2)$$ With $u_1 = 0$ (quiescent unburned gas): $$\rho_1 V = \rho_2 (V - u_2) \quad (1)$$ **Momentum:** $$P_1 + \rho_1 V^2 = P_2 + \rho_2 (V - u_2)^2 \quad (2)$$ **Energy (with heat release $Q$ per unit mass):** $$h_1 + \dfrac{V^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{(V - u_2)^2}{2} + Q \quad (3)$$ For a perfect gas with constant $\gamma$: $$h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = \dfrac{\gamma R}{(\gamma - 1) M}(T_2 - T_1)$$ **CJ condition:** $V - u_2 = a_2 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_2 / M}$ (post-shock flow is sonic). After substantial algebra (omitted for brevity here, but standard in combustion textbooks), the CJ detonation velocity reduces to: $$ \boxed{\,V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2\left(\gamma^2 - 1\right) Q}\,} $$ **Derivation of the simplified form using strong-detonation approximation:** For a strong detonation, $P_2 \gg P_1$, and the CJ condition gives: $$ \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma - 1} $$ The Rayleigh line + Hugoniot curve intersection yields: $$ V_{CJ} = a_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}} \dots $$ Substituting $P_2/P_1$ from CJ and the identity $a_1^2 = \gamma R T_1 / M$, the final CJ velocity is: $$ V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q} $$ **Numerical check (stoichiometric H₂–O₂ at 1 atm, 298 K):** - $Q \approx 15.2$ MJ/kg of mixture - $\gamma \approx 1.40$ - $V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(1.96 - 1) \cdot 15.2 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{29.1 \times 10^6} \approx 5393$ m/s Experimental value: **~5300–5500 m/s** ✓ (H₂–O₂ is one of the fastest detonations known). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Which Formula to Use | Exam Question Clue | Use This Formula | Why | |---|---|---| | "Compute LFL of a fuel blend" | Le Chatelier (#2) | Direct mixed-fuel LFL | | "Bunsen flame angle given, find $S_L$" | Bunsen cone (#4) | Direct geometric definition | | "Closed vessel, find peak pressure" | Explosion pressure (#7) | Adiabatic constant-V | | "Why doesn't the flame go through a 1 mm slot?" | Quenching (#9) | Heat loss exceeds heat release | | "What is the velocity of a detonation in CH₄–air?" | CJ detonation (#8) | Steady 1-D CJ theory | | "Flame thickness vs. pressure" | $\delta_L$ (#1) | Thermal-diffusive scaling | ## 4. Outside the LFL–UFL window, the mixture can be ignited locally (a pilot flame will burn) but the flame cannot self-propagate. --- ### 4.3 Diagram C — Deflagration vs. Detonation Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph blockA["DEFLAGRATION MODE"] def1["Propagation velocity 0.5 to 100 m/s"] --> def2["Heat conduction drives unburned gas"] def2 --> def3["Pressure rise small factor E approx 7"] def3 --> def4["Reaction zone follows the heat front"] end subgraph blockB["DETONATION MODE"] det1["Propagation velocity 1500 to 3000 m/s"] --> det2["Leading shock compresses and heats unburned gas"] det2 --> det3["Pressure jump factor 15 to 20"] det3 --> det4["Reaction zone is INSIDE the shock"] end blockA --> trans["TRANSITION DDT

A flame can spontaneously switch from deflagration to detonation (DDT) if local conditions (geometry, obstacles, mixture stratification) cause local flame acceleration to >500 m/s, at which point a pressure wave can coalesce with the flame front to form a detonation. --- ### 4.4 Diagram D — Bunsen Cone Geometry for $S_L$ Measurement ```mermaid graph LR nozzle["NOZZLE EXIT
