The flame front is conical because the flame propagates *normal to itself* at velocity $S_L$. The vector triangle gives $S_L = v_u \sin(\alpha/2)$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define "Lower Flammability Limit" and "Upper Flammability Limit" of a fuel–air mixture. Why does a stoichiometric mixture have the highest adiabatic flame temperature but is not always the most easily ignitable?** **Model Answer:** The **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** is the *minimum* fuel concentration in air below which a flame cannot self-propagate. At concentrations below LFL, the energy released per unit mass of mixture is insufficient to heat the next layer to its ignition temperature; the adiabatic flame temperature falls below ~1500 K and chain-branching reactions cannot sustain themselves. The **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** is the *maximum* fuel concentration in air above which a flame cannot self-propagate. Above UFL, there is insufficient oxidizer to release the heat needed to drive the flame forward; the reaction is oxygen-starved. A stoichiometric mixture has the *highest* adiabatic flame temperature because every fuel molecule finds exactly one oxidizer molecule, maximizing the energy release per unit mass of mixture. However, "ease of ignition" depends on a different criterion: the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** is a *kinetic* quantity that depends on radical-pool formation rates, not just the equilibrium temperature. Slightly *rich* or slightly *lean* mixtures often have *lower* MIE because radical quenching by excess reactant is minimized at φ ≈ 1.0–1.1. Thus, the most reactive mixture (lowest MIE) is not always the stoichiometric mixture. *(3 marks: 1 for LFL definition, 1 for UFL definition, 1 for the T_ad vs MIE distinction)* --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a deflagration and a detonation. State two practical situations where the transition from deflagration to detonation (DDT) is a serious safety concern.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Deflagration | Detonation | |---|---|---| | Propagation velocity | Subsonic, 0.5–100 m/s | Supersonic, 1500–3000 m/s | | Driving mechanism | Thermal conduction + radical diffusion from reaction zone into unburned gas | Compressive heating by a leading shock wave | | Pressure jump across front | Small, factor ≈ $E$ (≈ 7 for CH₄–air) | Large, factor 15–20 | | Reaction zone location | Behind the heat front, ahead of any pressure wave | Inside the shock front, immediately behind the shock | **Two practical DDT hazards:** 1. **Long natural-gas pipelines:** A leak + ignition produces a deflagration that accelerates in the pipe; if it reaches ~500 m/s, a detonation can form, leading to pipe rupture at 15–20 bar overpressure. 2. **Coal mines and grain silos:** Methane or grain-dust explosions can transition to detonation in confined, obstacle-laden geometries, causing catastrophic structural failure. *(3 marks: 2 for the table, 1 for the two DDT examples)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice) --- #### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **structure of a planar premixed flame** in detail. Label all four zones and the direction of heat flux. Define **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$ and derive the relation between $S_L$ and the unburned-gas velocity for a **Bunsen cone flame** of half-angle $\alpha$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A stoichiometric methane–air mixture is contained in a closed, rigid, well-insulated vessel at 1 atm and 300 K. The mixture is ignited, and combustion proceeds to completion at constant volume. Calculate: - (i) the **expansion ratio** $E$ if the adiabatic flame temperature is 2223 K and the molecular weight does not change appreciably, - (ii) the **peak explosion pressure** in atm, - (iii) the **LFL of the mixture** if 10 % of the methane is replaced by ethane (LFL of pure ethane = 3.0 vol %; LFL of pure methane = 5.0 vol %). *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Part 1 — Structure of a planar premixed flame (4 marks):** A 1-D planar premixed flame, propagating from right to left into a quiescent unburned mixture, consists of four sub-zones: 1. **Unburned Gas Zone (UGZ):** Far upstream. $T = T_u$, original fuel–air composition. No reaction. 2. **Pre-Heat Zone (PHZ):** Heat is conducted from the reaction zone backward (i.e., into the fresh mixture). $T$ rises smoothly from $T_u$ to approximately $T_u + 0.7(T_{ad} - T_u)$. No appreciable reaction yet. 3. **Reaction Zone (RZ):** A thin, luminous sheet where the bulk of the exothermic chemistry occurs. Radical species (H, O, OH) are in quasi-steady state. $T$ jumps to ≈ $T_{ad}$. The thickness of this zone is typically $10^{-5}$–$10^{-4}$ m. 4. **Post-Flame Zone (PFZ):** Equilibrium products at $T_{ad}$, undergoing slow radiative cooling. **Sketch:** *(refer to Section 4.1 diagram)* — leftmost UGZ (blue), PHZ (orange), RZ (red, thinnest), PFZ (grey). Heat flux arrow points from RZ to PHZ (leftward). **Part 2 — Bunsen cone derivation (3 marks):** In a Bunsen burner, the unburned gas flows *axially* upward with velocity $v_u$. The flame front is conical, inclined at half-angle $\alpha$ to the burner axis. The flame propagates *normal to its own surface* at velocity $S_L$. Resolving the velocity vector normal to the flame surface: $$v_u \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \alpha\right) = v_u \sin \alpha = S_L$$ **Final:** $\boxed{S_L = v_u \sin(\alpha/2)}$ *(If grader uses half-angle definition, $S_L = v_u \sin(\alpha/2)$; if full cone angle, $S_L = v_u \sin(\alpha)$ — both accepted if consistent.)* --- **(b) Part 1 — Expansion Ratio (2 marks):** For an ideal gas with constant molecular weight: $$E = \dfrac{T_{ad}}{T_u} = \dfrac{2223}{300} = 7.41$$ **Part 2 — Peak Explosion Pressure (2 marks):** In a *closed* rigid vessel, all gas burns at constant volume. From the ideal gas law at constant $V$ and $n$: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = E = 7.41$$ $$\therefore P_{max} = 1.0 \text{ atm} \times 7.41 = \mathbf{7.41 \text{ atm}}$$ **Part 3 — LFL of Mixture (3 marks):** Using Le Chatelier's rule: $$ \begin{aligned} LFL_{mix} &= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{90}{5.0} + \dfrac{10}{3.0}} \\ &= \dfrac{100}{18.0 + 3.333} \\ &= \dfrac{100}{21.333} \\ &= \mathbf{4.69 \text{ vol \%}} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice** **(a)** Define **expansion ratio** for a premixed flame. Derive an expression for the **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) detonation velocity** for a perfect gas with constant specific-heat ratio γ and heat release $Q$ per unit mass of mixture. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A stoichiometric hydrogen–oxygen mixture initially at 1 atm and 298 K detonates. Given: - $\gamma = 1.40$ (constant) - Heat of reaction $Q = 15.2$ MJ/kg of mixture Calculate: - (i) the **CJ detonation velocity** in m/s, - (ii) the **peak detonation pressure** in atm, - (iii) compare your result with the experimentally observed detonation velocity of ~2800 m/s in a stoichiometric **hydrogen–air** mixture. Why is the H₂–O₂ velocity higher? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Part 1 — Expansion Ratio Definition (1 mark):** The expansion ratio $E$ is the ratio of the density of the unburned gas to the density of the burned gas at the same pressure: $$E = \dfrac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{V_b}{V_u} = \dfrac{T_b}{T_u} \cdot \dfrac{M_u}{M_b}$$ For an ideal gas with constant molecular weight ($M_u = M_b$), $E = T_{ad}/T_u$. **Part 2 — CJ Detonation Velocity Derivation (6 marks):** The CJ model is a 1-D steady detonation with a precursor shock moving at velocity $V$ into quiescent unburned gas. Three conservation laws across the shock: **Continuity:** $$\rho_1 V = \rho_2 (V - u_2) \quad (1)$$ **Momentum:** $$P_1 + \rho_1 V^2 = P_2 + \rho_2 (V - u_2)^2 \quad (2)$$ **Energy (with heat release $Q$ per unit mass):** $$h_1 + \dfrac{V^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{(V - u_2)^2}{2} + Q \quad (3)$$ **CJ condition:** The flow immediately behind the shock is *sonic* relative to the shock: $$V - u_2 = a_2 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_2 / M} \quad (4)$$ **Solution procedure (algebra summarized):** From (1): $u_2 = V(1 - \rho_1/\rho_2)$. Substitute into (2) and use $a^2 = \gamma P/\rho$: $$\rho_1 V^2 (1 - \rho_1/\rho_2) = P_2 - P_1$$ For a strong detonation ($P_2 \gg P_1$), the CJ condition gives $P_2/P_1 = (\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)$. Combining the Rankine–Hugoniot jump relations and the CJ condition: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{CJ}^2 &= 2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q \\ \Rightarrow V_{CJ} &= \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q} \end{aligned} $$ --- **(b) Part 1 — CJ velocity for H₂–O₂ (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{CJ} &= \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q} \\ &= \sqrt{2(1.40^2 - 1)(15.2 \times 10^6)} \\ &= \sqrt{2(1.96 - 1)(15.2 \times 10^6)} \\ &= \sqrt{2 \times 0.96 \times 15.2 \times 10^6} \\ &= \sqrt{29.18 \times 10^6} \\ &= \mathbf{5402 \text{ m/s}} \end{aligned} $$ **Part 2 — Peak Detonation Pressure (2 marks):** For CJ detonation: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{1.40 + 1}{1.40 - 1} = \dfrac{2.40}{0.40} = 6.0$$ Wait — this is the *Rayleigh-line* value. Including heat release, the full CJ pressure ratio is: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{(\gamma + 1)(\gamma + 1)}{2\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{Q}{c_p T_1 / (\gamma - 1)} \dots$$ For H₂–O₂ at 298 K with $Q = 15.2$ MJ/kg: $$P_2 \approx 19.3 \text{ atm}$$ *(Students may use $P_2/P_1 \approx 1 + \gamma(\gamma-1)Q/(a_1^2)$ for a first estimate, giving $\approx$ 18 atm.)* **Part 3 — Comparison with H₂–air (2 marks):** - Experimental H₂–air: $V_{CJ} \approx 2800$ m/s. - H₂–air is *diluted* with N₂ (which is an inert diatomic in this context). The N₂ acts as a heat sink, lowering the *effective* $Q$ per unit mass of mixture. Using $Q_{air} \approx 3.4$ MJ/kg of mixture (vs. 15.2 for pure O₂), the CJ velocity for H₂–air is: $$V_{CJ, air} = \sqrt{2(0.96)(3.4 \times 10^6)} \approx 2555 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{close to } 2800 \text{ m/s observed})$$ The H₂–O₂ velocity is **higher** because pure O₂ releases more heat per unit mass of mixture (no N₂ ballast) and produces lighter product molecules (H₂O, ~18 g/mol vs. N₂'s 28 g/mol), so the sound speed in the products is higher. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark-Deduction Zones):** > 1. **Confusing $S_L$ with $S_T$:** $S_L$ is the *laminar* burning velocity; $S_T$ is the *turbulent* one. Mixing them in a Bunsen-cone derivation is a **−2 mark** penalty. > 2. **Forgetting $M_u/M_b$ in $E$:** If molecular weight changes (e.g., for H₂ combustion, $M$ goes from 12 to 9 g/mol, $M_u/M_b = 4/3$), students often write $E = T_{ad}/T_u$ only, missing the multiplicative $M$ factor. **−1 mark**. > 3. **Using $\Delta H$ in the CJ formula:** The CJ formula uses *heat of reaction per unit mass of unburned mixture*, not the standard enthalpy of formation. Energy per mole of fuel must be divided by total mass of reactants. **−1.5 marks**. > 4. **Mixing deflagration & detonation pressure ratios:** Deflagration: $P_2/P_1 \approx E \approx 7$–8. Detonation: $P_2/P_1 \approx 15$–20. Do not interchange. > 5. **Confusing the cone angle:** Bunsen $S_L$ formula uses the *half-angle* of the inner cone in most textbooks; some use the *full* cone angle. State your convention. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (rapid-fire before exam):** - A **premixed flame** has the fuel + oxidizer mixed *before* the flame; the reaction zone is a thin sheet of thickness $\delta_L = \kappa/(\rho_u c_p S_L)$. - The four zones of a premixed flame: **Unburned → Pre-Heat → Reaction → Post-Flame**. - **LFL** is the lean limit, **UFL** is the rich limit; outside this range, flame self-propagation is impossible. - The **adiabatic flame temperature** at LFL/UFL is approximately **1500 K** for most hydrocarbons (the "rule of 1500"). - **Le Chatelier's rule** for mixed-fuel LFL: $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \sum(y_i / LFL_i)$. - **Laminar burning velocity $S_L$** is the speed of the flame *relative to the unburned gas*, normal to the flame surface. - **Bunsen method:** $S_L = v_u \sin(\alpha/2)$. - **Continuity at the flame:** $\rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$ (mass conservation). - **Expansion ratio $E = T_{ad}/T_u$** (ideal gas, $M$ unchanged). For CH₄–air stoichiometric: $E \approx$ **7.4**. - **Closed-vessel peak pressure:** $P_{max} \approx P_0 \cdot E$ (constant volume, complete combustion). - **Deflagration** is subsonic (heat-conduction-driven, 0.5–100 m/s); **Detonation** is supersonic (shock-driven, 1500–3000 m/s). - **CJ detonation velocity:** $V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$. - **Quenching distance** $d_q$ is the minimum gap below which a flame cannot propagate (heat loss > heat release). - **Minimum ignition energy (MIE)** scales as $d_q^{\,2}$. - The **DDT (deflagration-to-detonation transition)** is a major safety concern in long pipes, mines, and silos. - Slightly *rich* mixtures (φ ≈ 1.05–1.15) often have the **highest $S_L$** for hydrocarbons. - **Pressure effect** on $S_L$: weak (typically $S_L \propto P^{-0.1}$ for CH₄). - **Temperature effect** on $S_L$: $S_L \propto T_u^{\alpha}$ with α ≈ 1.5–2.0. - **Expansion ratio** controls flame buoyancy, acoustic emissions, and explosion-vent sizing in NFPA 68. - **Detonation pressure ratio** is ~15–20 (much higher than deflagration's ~7). - For **H₂–O₂** mixtures, $V_{CJ} \approx 5400$ m/s; for **H₂–air**, $V_{CJ} \approx 2800$ m/s (N₂ ballast reduces $Q$). - For **CH₄–air**, $V_{CJ} \approx 1800$ m/s; experimental range 1500–2000 m/s. - **Diluents** (CO₂, N₂, H₂O vapor) shift flammability limits by absorbing heat and quenching radicals.

topics.title: Spread of Flames over Liquid and Solid Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Spread of Flames over Liquid and Solid Surfaces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Spread of Flames — The Formal Definition **Flame Spread** is the propagation of a combustion reaction zone across the surface or through the interior of a combustible material, sustained by the combined processes of **heat transfer** from the flame to the unburnt fuel, **pyrolysis/vaporization** of the fuel, **mixing** of vapors with oxidizer, and **ignition** of the resulting mixture. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the process is classified by: - **Substrate State** → Liquid Pool vs. Solid Surface - **Relative Flow Direction** → Opposed-Flow (against oxidizer flow) vs. Concurrent-Flow (with oxidizer flow) - **Thermal Regime** → Thermally Thin (heat fully penetrates the fuel) vs. Thermally Thick (heat stays near the surface) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The governing physics of flame spread is fundamentally a **heat-transfer-controlled** phenomenon. The flame front advances at a rate determined by how fast the unburnt fuel can be raised to its **ignition temperature** $(T_{ig})$ by heat conducted or radiated from the established flame. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Burning Fuse" Imagine lighting the wick of a candle. The flame does not simply leap ahead — it slowly walks forward. Why? Because the flame must first **melt the wax** (energy demand), the molten wax must **wick up and vaporize** (mass transport), and the vapors must **mix and ignite** (chemistry). The slowest of these "three legs of the race" controls the rate. - **Liquid pool fires** = like wax: the spread is driven by **re-radiative preheating** of the unburnt liquid surface, creating a hot layer that vaporizes progressively. - **Solid surface fires** = like a piece of wood: preheating of the solid ahead of the flame through **conduction and radiation** decomposes (pyrolyzes) the material to release combustible gases. The "walking speed" of the flame front — the **flame spread rate** $(V_s)$ — is what fire engineers must quantify for hazard analysis, because it determines how fast a fire will engulf a building, a forest, or a chemical spill. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\rho_\infty$ | Ambient air density | **1.2 kg/m³** | | $T_{ig}$ | Ignition temperature | **300 °C – 600 °C** (typical solids) | | $T_\infty$ | Ambient temperature | **293 K (20 °C)** | | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** | | $c_p$ | Specific heat of air (constant p) | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Condition** used in KTU derivations: ISO 1 atm, 20 °C, still air unless otherwise stated. Any deviation in problem statements must be carried as additional variables $P$, $T_\infty$, $u_\infty$ in the final expression. --- ## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (At a Glance) | Mode | Subtype | Controlling Resistance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Liquid Pool** | Shallow (film) pool | Burnout / preheating length | | **Liquid Pool** | Deep pool | Heat feedback to surface | | **Solid Surface** | Thermally Thin, Opposed-flow | Solid-phase conduction | | **Solid Surface** | Thermally Thick, Opposed-flow | Gas-phase conduction (De Ris) | | **Solid Surface** | Concurrent-flow (wind-aided) | Radiation + combined convection | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Flame Spread Over Liquid Pool Surfaces ### 2.1.1 The Physical Mechanism A pool fire begins as a localized ignition. As the central region burns, **re-radiative and convective heat feedback** from the flame and hot combustion products warms the **unburnt liquid surface** ahead of the flame front. The preheated liquid: 1. Vaporizes at a faster rate (Clausius–Clapeyron driven). 2. Produces a vapor-rich boundary layer that mixes with air. 3. Ignites when the vapor–air mixture reaches the **lower flammability limit (LFL)** and the local temperature exceeds the **flash point**. The **flame front** therefore propagates radially outward as a propagating circular front whose instantaneous radius is $r(t)$. ### 2.1.2 Critical Liquid Pool Depth $(h^*)$ A central KTU concept is the **critical thickness** below which the pool cannot sustain burning because conductive heat losses to the substrate (or the cold liquid beneath) dominate. $$h^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_f \cdot (T_{ig} - T_s)}{(\dot{m}'' c_{p,f} \Delta T_{vap} / \tau_b)}}$$ For steady-state burning, the **regression (burn-down) rate** of a deep pool follows: $$\dot{r} = \dfrac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_f}$$ The **burnout time** for a deep pool (Thomas / Babrauskas correlation): $$\tau_b = \dfrac{h}{\dot{r}} = \dfrac{\rho_f h}{\dot{m}''}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Babrauskas (1983) Mass Loss Rate Correlation for Pool Fires:** > $$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k \beta D}\right)$$ > where $\dot{m}''_\infty$ = **3.5 × 10⁻³ kg/(m²·s)** (steady-state for large diameter), $\beta$ = **1 / m** (extinction coefficient of the flame), and $D$ = pool diameter. ### 2.1.3 Radial Spread Velocity on a Pool The **instantaneous flame radius** on a circular pool often follows a power-law: $$r(t) = C \cdot t^n$$ For **thermally deep, unconfined pools** the spread is **linear in time** ($n = 1$); for **shallow films** the exponent approaches $n = 0.5$ as the spread slows. The **radial flame spread velocity** is then: $$V_s = \dfrac{dr}{dt} = n C t^{n-1}$$ --- ## 2.2 Flame Spread Over Solid Surfaces ### 2.2.1 The Two Limiting Thermal Regimes The dominant parameter is the **thermal penetration depth**: $$\delta_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s t_{ig}}{\rho_s c_s}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification Rule (KTU Board Favourite):** > - **Thermally Thin:** $\delta_{th} > \tau_s$ (fuel thickness) → solid is heated through its full depth > - **Thermally Thick:** $\delta_{th} < \tau_s$ → heat remains in a thin surface layer ### 2.2.2 Opposed-Flow Spread (Flame moving against oxidizer flow) This is the classical **De Ris (1973)** configuration. The flame angle tilts away from the unburnt fuel, and gas-phase conduction from the flame to the unburnt surface dominates. **Thermally Thick Fuel — De Ris Formula:** $$V_s = \dfrac{\lambda_g \cdot \left( \dfrac{T_f - T_{ig}}{T_{ig} - T_s} \right)^2}{\rho_s c_s \delta}$$ For a more complete form including forced opposed flow velocity $u_\infty$: $$V_s = \dfrac{\lambda_g / (\rho_s c_s \delta)}{1 + (u_\infty / V_s^*)^{2/3}}$$ where $V_s^*$ is the **quench velocity** (minimum velocity above which flame cannot propagate). **Thermally Thin Fuel — Simplified De Ris:** $$V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \lambda_g \cdot \left( T_f - T_{ig} \right)^2 \cdot k_s}{\rho_s c_s \tau_s \cdot (T_{ig} - T_s)^2}}$$ ### 2.2.3 Concurrent-Flow Spread (Flame moving with oxidizer flow) Wind or forced draft pushes the flame **over** the unburnt surface, increasing the heat transfer and dramatically accelerating spread. Surface **radiation** often becomes dominant in the limit of large scale. **Quintiere / Ohlemiller correlation for concurrent spread:** $$V_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}''_{flame}}{\rho_s c_s \tau_s (T_{ig} - T_s) + \Delta H_{py} \rho_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why concurrent is faster:** In opposed flow, the incoming oxidizer literally **cools** the reaction zone and reduces gas-phase conduction. In concurrent flow, the oxidizer is **pre-mixed and pre-heated** as it passes through the flame footprint, and the flame lies flat on the surface, radiating efficiently forward. ### 2.2.4 Effect of Orientation and Slope - **Lateral spread (horizontal surface):** $V_s$ lowest. - **Upward vertical spread:** $V_s$ dramatically higher (chimney effect + radiation feedback). - **Downward (descending) spread:** Often **quenched** or self-extinguishing on thermally thick fuels. The classic **Lattimer (2008)** expression for upward spread velocity on a wall: $$V_{s,up} = \dfrac{\dot{q}''_{f} \cdot Y_{O_2,\infty}}{\rho_s c_s (T_{ig} - T_s) + \rho_s \Delta H_{py}}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Cheat Sheet — Memorize for 14-mark derivations.** | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\delta_{th} = \sqrt{k_s t_{ig} / (\rho_s c_s)}$ | Thermal penetration depth | m | | 2 | $V_s = \lambda_g \cdot \Delta T^2 / [\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_s)]$ | De Ris (opposed, thick) | m/s | | 3 | $V_s = \sqrt{2 \lambda_g k_s \Delta T^2 / [\rho_s c_s \tau_s (T_{ig} - T_s)^2]}$ | De Ris (opposed, thin) | m/s | | 4 | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ | Pool burning rate (Babrauskas) | kg/(m²·s) | | 5 | $\tau_b = \rho_f h / \dot{m}''$ | Pool burnout time | s | | 6 | $r(t) = C t^n$ | Radial flame radius | m | | 7 | $V_s = \dot{q}''_{f} / [\rho_s c_s \tau_s (T_{ig} - T_s)]$ | Concurrent (thin solid) | m/s | | 8 | $T_{ig} - T_s = (k_s / V_s \rho_s c_s) (dT/dx)$ | Energy balance at flame front | K | | 9 | $\text{Pe} = V_s \delta / \alpha_s$ | Spread Péclet number | — | | 10 | $u_\infty^{quench} \approx \sqrt{\alpha_g / t_{ig}}$ | Quench velocity | m/s | > **Notation:** $\lambda_g$ = gas thermal conductivity, $\rho_s c_s$ = solid volumetric heat capacity, $k_s$ = solid conductivity, $\tau_s$ = solid thickness, $t_{ig}$ = time to reach ignition, $\dot{q}''_f$ = flame heat flux, $D$ = pool diameter, $k\beta$ = flame optical depth, $\alpha_g$ = gas thermal diffusivity. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Forest Fire Modeling:** Wind-aided (concurrent) spread rates on tree canopies → Rothermel & Andrews fire spread equations are engineering descendants of the De Ris theory. - **Building Code Compliance (NBC India / IS 14435):** Wall and ceiling lining materials are tested for **Flame Spread Index (FSI)** per ASTM E84, a direct application of the opposed-flow concept. - **Chemical Process Safety:** Predicting how fast a spill of LNG or a hydrocarbon will spread over bunding/dykes is essential for designing **dike capacity** and **foam application time**. - **Aircraft Cabin Safety:** FAA regulations use upward concurrent spread velocity to certify interior materials. - **Fire Forensics:** Burn patterns (V-patterns) on walls are reverse-engineered using $V_{s,up}$ to determine the **origin and time** of the fire. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: De Ris Spread Velocity (Thermally Thick, Opposed Flow) ### Step 0 — Setup and Assumptions Consider a semi-infinite solid fuel with surface temperature $T_s$ far from the flame, gas-phase flame temperature $T_f$, and ignition temperature $T_{ig}$. The flame front stands off from the surface and heat reaches the unburnt region by **gas-phase conduction through the flame wedge** of characteristic length $\delta$ (boundary-layer thickness). > **Assumption Set:** > 1. Steady propagation at velocity $V_s$. > 2. Solid is semi-infinite (thermally thick). > 3. Heat transfer is dominated by gas-phase conduction. > 4. Lewis number Le = 1 (heat and mass diffuse equally). --- ### Step 1 — Energy Balance at the Flame Foot The flame receives chemical energy per unit area of order $\rho_\infty c_p (T_f - T_{ig}) V_s$. The flame in turn delivers energy to the unburnt solid via gas-phase conduction. Equating conduction from the flame to the conduction required to heat the solid: $$\lambda_g \dfrac{T_f - T_{ig}}{\delta} = \rho_s c_s (T_{ig} - T_s) V_s$$ **Conversion logic:** Left side = conductive flux from gas (Fourier's law with temperature drop across boundary layer of thickness $\delta$). Right side = energy needed per unit time per unit area to heat fresh solid entering the flame zone at velocity $V_s$. --- ### Step 2 — Solve for the Boundary-Layer Thickness $\delta$ We use the classical **boundary-layer scaling**: $$\delta \sim \sqrt{\dfrac{\nu x}{u_\infty}}$$ In the absence of forced flow, $u_\infty$ is replaced by a flame-induced velocity scale. For natural-convection-induced flow: $$u^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \beta (T_f - T_s) \delta}{\text{const}}}$$ For the standard De Ris derivation, $\delta$ scales with the characteristic flame standoff distance and we can eliminate it by combining the energy balance with the integral boundary-layer profile, which yields: $$\dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{\alpha_g t_{ig}}} = f(\text{geometry})$$ After substituting and simplifying (De Ris 1973, Arch. Comb.): $$V_s = \dfrac{\lambda_g}{\rho_s c_s \delta} \cdot \left( \dfrac{T_f - T_{ig}}{T_{ig} - T_s} \right)^2$$ > **Final Form (Classical De Ris, Thermally Thick):** > $$\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{\lambda_g \, \Delta T_{f\text{-}ig}^2}{\rho_s c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_s)^2}}$$ --- ### Step 3 — Order-of-Magnitude Estimate (Numerical Sanity Check) Take: - $\lambda_g = 0.025$ W/(m·K) - $\rho_s c_s = 2.0 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K) (wood-like) - $T_f - T_{ig} = 800$ K - $T_{ig} - T_s = 400$ K - $\delta = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m Then: $$ \begin{aligned} V_s &= \dfrac{0.025 \times 800^2}{2.0 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 400^2} \\ &= \dfrac{0.025 \times 6.4 \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6 \times 10^5} \\ &= \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^4}{1.6 \times 10^9} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = 0.036 \text{ m/hr} \end{aligned} $$ **Sanity check:** Order-of-magnitude is correct for slow opposed-flow spread on a thermally thick material. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flame Spread on a Thermally Thin Fuel ### Step 0 — Physical Picture When the solid is **thin** (e.g., a sheet of paper, a thin curtain), the entire thickness is heated by the flame. The energy required to ignite a unit area is **finite and stored in the solid**, rather than being supplied continuously from a semi-infinite reservoir. --- ### Step 1 — Total Energy to Ignite Unit Area $$Q_{ig} = \rho_s c_s \tau_s (T_{ig} - T_s) + \rho_s \tau_s \Delta H_{py}$$ **Conversion logic:** First term = sensible heat to raise solid to $T_{ig}$. Second term = enthalpy for pyrolysis of the volatile content (often dominant for cellulose-type fuels). --- ### Step 2 — Heat Flux Delivered by Flame For thin fuels the gas-phase conductive flux reaches a steady value: $$\dot{q}''_c = \lambda_g \dfrac{T_f - T_{ig}}{\delta_g}$$ --- ### Step 3 — Equate and Solve for $V_s$ In the limit of high conductivity, the spread is limited by the time to charge the solid to ignition: $$V_s \tau_s \rho_s (c_s \Delta T + \Delta H_{py}) = \dot{q}''_c \cdot \delta_g$$ After rearrangement (using $\delta_g \sim \sqrt{\alpha_g \tau_g}$ where $\tau_g$ is gas residence time): $$V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \lambda_g (T_f - T_{ig})^2 k_s}{\rho_s c_s \tau_s (T_{ig} - T_s)^2}}$$ > **Final Form (Thermally Thin):** > $$\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{1}{(T_{ig} - T_s)}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \lambda_g k_s (T_f - T_{ig})^2}{\rho_s c_s \tau_s}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Burnout Time for a Deep Liquid Pool ### Step 1 — Mass Conservation Mass of fuel in pool = $\rho_f A h$. Mass burned per unit time = $\dot{m}'' A$. Hence: $$\dfrac{dh}{dt} = -\dfrac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_f}$$ ### Step 2 — Integrate from $h_0$ to 0 For a **deep pool** with $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty$ (steady-state value): $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{h_0}^{0} dh &= -\dfrac{\dot{m}''_\infty}{\rho_f}\int_{0}^{\tau_b} dt \\ -h_0 &= -\dfrac{\dot{m}''_\infty}{\rho_f}\tau_b \\ \tau_b &= \dfrac{\rho_f h_0}{\dot{m}''_\infty} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** > $$\boxed{\tau_b = \dfrac{\rho_f h_0}{\dot{m}''_\infty}}$$ ### Step 3 — Including Transient (Shallow Pool) Correction For finite-diameter pools the burning rate is not constant. Using Babrauskas: $$\dot{m}''(D) = \dot{m}''_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$ A more accurate burnout time integral is: $$\tau_b = \int_0^{h_0} \dfrac{\rho_f \, dh}{\dot{m}''(D)} = \dfrac{\rho_f h_0}{\dot{m}''_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)}$$ For **large $D$** ($k\beta D \gg 1$): $e^{-k\beta D} \to 0$, reducing to the deep-pool result. ✓ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Liquid Pool | Solid Surface | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Phase change mechanism | Vaporization (latent heat) | Pyrolysis (chemical decomposition) | | Dominant heat-transfer mode | Re-radiation from flame + conduction in liquid | Gas-phase conduction (opposed) / radiation (concurrent) | | Spread controlling step | Surface preheat to flash point | Surface preheat to $T_{ig}$ | | Effect of wind | Tilts flame, changes $V_s$ modestly | Order-of-magnitude increase (concurrent) | | Effect of substrate | Insulated sublayer → longer burn | Substrate mass and conductivity matter | | Classic correlation | Babrauskas (1983) | De Ris (1973) / Quintiere (2002) | | Key dimensionless group | $(k\beta D)$, $\rho_f h / \dot{m}'' \tau_b$ | Péclet Pe, Biot Bi, $\delta_{th}/\tau_s$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Flame Spread Phenomena ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Ignition Source
Origin Point] --> B1[Heat Transfer to Fuel] B1 --> C1{Substrate State} C1 -->|Liquid Pool| D1[Liquid Pool Fire Module] C1 -->|Solid Surface| D2[Solid Surface Fire Module] D1 --> E1A[Preheating of Liquid Surface
to Flash Point] D1 --> E1B[Vaporization
Latent Heat Demand] D1 --> E1C[Re-radiative Feedback
from Hot Soot Layer] E1A --> F1[Mixing of Vapor with Air
above LFL] E1B --> F1 E1C --> F1 F1 --> G1[Flame Anchors at Front
Vapor-Air Mixture Ignites] G1 --> H1[Radial Spread Velocity Vr] H1 --> I1[Babrauskas Correlation
m dot vs Diameter D] H1 --> I2[Pool Burnout Time Tb] D2 --> E2A{Fuel Thickness} E2A -->|Thin| F2A[Thermally Thin Module] E2A -->|Thick| F2B[Thermally Thick Module] F2A --> G2A{Opposed or Concurrent} F2B --> G2B{Opposed or Concurrent} G2A -->|Opposed| H2A[De Ris Thin
Vs proportional to k rho c] G2A -->|Concurrent| H2B[Radiation Driven
Very High Vs] G2B -->|Opposed| H2C[De Ris Thick
Vs inversely proportional to delta] G2B -->|Concurrent| H2D[Lattimer Wall Spread
Chimney Effect Boost] H2A --> I2A[Slow Spread
Order cm per min] H2B --> I2B[Fast Spread
Order m per s] H2C --> I2C[Moderate Spread
Order cm per min] H2D --> I2D[Very Fast Spread
Order m per s] ``` ## 4.2 Flame Spread Control Loops and Feedback Pathways ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Flame Front Position] -->|Advances at Vs| P2[Newly Exposed Fuel] P2 -->|Heat Absorbed| P3[Rise in Surface Temperature] P3 -->|Reaches Tig| P4[Pyrolysis or Vaporization] P4 -->|Releases Fuel Vapors| P5[Mixing with Oxidizer] P5 -->|Ignition when LFL and Tig| P6[New Flame Element] P6 -->|Heat Feedback| P3 P6 -->|Advances| P1 H1[External Wind u infinity] -->|Tilts Flame| P6 H2[Slope of Substrate] -->|Chimney Effect| P6 H3[Radiation from Flame] -->|Preheats Ahead| P3 H4[Substrate Conductivity] -->|Drains Heat| P3 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Logic for Thermal Regime Classification (KTU Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Start: Fuel Sample] --> Q1[Compute Thermal Penetration Depth
delta th = sqrt ks tig over rho s cs] Q1 --> Q2[Compare delta th with Solid Thickness tau s] Q2 -->|delta th greater than tau s| R1[THIN FUEL] Q2 -->|delta th less than tau s| R2[THICK FUEL] R1 --> R3[Apply Thin De Ris Formula] R2 --> R4[Apply Thick De Ris Formula] R3 --> R5[Estimate Vs and Compare with Test Data] R4 --> R5 R5 --> R6{Spread Rate Plausible} R6 -->|Yes| R7[Final Design Spread Velocity] R6 -->|No| R8[Re-examine Assumptions
Check for Charring, Ablation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The first flowchart (Section 4.1) is the **canonical taxonomy** you should reproduce in a KTU 14-mark answer to demonstrate structured thinking. Section 4.2 emphasizes the **feedback loop** nature of flame spread — a key conceptual point examiners reward. Section 4.3 is the **decision algorithm** that determines which De Ris branch to use. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the term "flame spread" and distinguish between opposed-flow and concurrent-flow spread with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Flame spread is the propagation of a combustion reaction front across a combustible surface, governed by the heat-transfer rate from the flame to the unburnt fuel. In **opposed-flow spread**, the oxidizer flow (natural or forced) is directed **opposite** to the direction of flame propagation, e.g., wind blowing against a wall fire from above. In **concurrent-flow spread**, the oxidizer flow is **aligned** with the direction of propagation, e.g., a forest fire spreading in the same direction as the wind. Concurrent-flow spread is significantly faster because the flame lies flat on the surface, maximising radiative and convective pre-heating of unburnt fuel. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is meant by "thermally thick" and "thermally thin" fuels? Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A fuel is **thermally thin** when the heat from the flame penetrates through its entire thickness during the preheating period — the thermal penetration depth $\delta_{th} = \sqrt{k_s t_{ig} / (\rho_s c_s)}$ exceeds the physical thickness $\tau_s$. Examples: a sheet of paper, a thin curtain, a tissue. A fuel is **thermally thick** when the heat from the flame is confined to a thin near-surface layer because $\delta_{th} \ll \tau_s$. Examples: a thick wooden plank, a PMMA slab, a concrete wall lined with insulation. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice A]` **Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** Derive the De Ris expression for the spread velocity of a flame over a **thermally thick solid fuel in opposed flow**. State clearly the assumptions used. **Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** A wooden wall has the following properties: $\rho_s = 700$ kg/m³, $c_s = 1.6$ kJ/(kg·K), $k_s = 0.15$ W/(m·K), $T_s = 25$ °C, $T_{ig} = 350$ °C, $T_f = 900$ °C, $\delta = 5$ mm, and the gas thermal conductivity is $\lambda_g = 0.030$ W/(m·K). Calculate the spread velocity. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A.(a) 1. **State the governing physics:** Heat from the flame reaches the unburnt solid through gas-phase conduction across the flame wedge of characteristic thickness $\delta$. 2. **Write the energy balance at the flame front:** $$\lambda_g \dfrac{T_f - T_{ig}}{\delta} = \rho_s c_s V_s (T_{ig} - T_s)$$ 3. **Apply boundary-layer scaling** to relate $\delta$ to the thermal field and rearrange, leading to: $$V_s = \dfrac{\lambda_g (T_f - T_{ig})^2}{\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_s)^2}$$ 4. **List assumptions:** steady propagation, semi-infinite solid (thermally thick), Le = 1, flame wedge geometry, gas-phase conduction dominant, constant properties. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A.(b) Given: - $\lambda_g = 0.030$ W/(m·K) - $T_f - T_{ig} = 900 - 350 = 550$ K - $T_{ig} - T_s = 350 - 25 = 325$ K - $\delta = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $\rho_s c_s = 700 \times 1600 = 1.12 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K) **Substitute into the De Ris formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_s &= \dfrac{0.030 \times 550^2}{1.12 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 325^2} \\ &= \dfrac{0.030 \times 3.025 \times 10^5}{1.12 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.05625 \times 10^5} \\ &= \dfrac{9075}{5.915 \times 10^8} \\ &= 1.534 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Final answer:** $V_s \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s = **5.52 cm/hr**. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice B]` **Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** Explain the mechanism of **flame spread over a liquid pool fire**. Define burnout time and derive an expression for the burnout time of a deep liquid pool. **Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** A circular pool of diesel (spill in a chemical yard) has a diameter of 8 m. Using the Babrauskas correlation with $k\beta = 0.7$ /m and $\dot{m}''_\infty = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m²·s), and taking $\rho_f = 850$ kg/m³ and $h_0 = 25$ mm, calculate the **steady-state burning rate**, the **regression rate**, and the **burnout time**. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B.(a) 1. **Mechanism:** Localized ignition → flame establishes → re-radiative and convective heat feedback from flame to liquid surface → preheating of unburnt liquid surface → vaporization increases → vapor-air mixture above LFL → ignition at the advancing front → radial spread velocity $V_s$. 2. **Burnout time** is the time required for a fuel layer of initial thickness $h_0$ to be completely consumed at a given mass burning rate $\dot{m}''$. It is the design parameter for **fire-fighting resource allocation** and **safe re-entry time** in process plants. 3. **Derivation:** Mass balance $\rho_f \cdot dh/dt = -\dot{m}''$ → integrate over burn duration → for deep pool ($\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty = $ const) → $\tau_b = \rho_f h_0 / \dot{m}''_\infty$. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B.(b) **Step 1: Compute the steady burning rate for $D = 8$ m.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}''(D) &= \dot{m}''_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right) \\ &= 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - e^{-0.7 \times 8}\right) \\ &= 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - e^{-5.6}\right) \\ &= 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - 0.00370\right) \\ &= 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9963 \\ &= 3.487 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the regression rate.** $$ \dot{r} = \dfrac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{3.487 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 4.10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s} \approx 1.48 \text{ cm/hr} $$ **Step 3: Compute the burnout time for $h_0 = 25$ mm = 0.025 m.** $$ \tau_b = \dfrac{\rho_f h_0}{\dot{m}''} = \dfrac{850 \times 0.025}{3.487 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{21.25}{3.487 \times 10^{-3}} = 6093 \text{ s} \approx 1.69 \text{ hr} $$ **Final Answers:** - Burning rate: $\dot{m}'' \approx 3.487 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m²·s) - Regression rate: $\dot{r} \approx 4.10 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s - Burnout time: $\tau_b \approx 1.69$ hr (or 101.5 min) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up thermally thin and thick formulas** — Always state the classification criterion $\delta_{th}$ vs $\tau_s$ first. > 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical substitution — KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for dimensional inconsistency, even if the final number is right. > 3. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — A derivation without explicit assumptions gets only 50% credit on the derivation part. > 4. **Confusing $T_f$, $T_{ig}$, and $T_s$** — Always use $T_f$ for flame temperature, $T_{ig}$ for ignition, $T_s$ for ambient solid. Many students write the wrong $\Delta T$. > 5. **Treating $\delta$ as constant** — Remind that $\delta$ depends on flow conditions. In some numerical problems, examiners give a fixed $\delta$ as input; use that value directly. > 6. **Pool vs solid confusion** — Liquid pool problems should use **Babrauskas / Thomas** correlations; solid surface problems should use **De Ris / Quintiere** formulas. Marks deducted for using the wrong family of equations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Flame spread = heat-transfer-controlled** propagation of a combustion front. - [x] **Two main substrates:** liquid pool & solid surface; each has its own dominant physics. - [x] **Liquid pool fires:** governed by **Babrauskas correlation** $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$; burnout time $\tau_b = \rho_f h_0 / \dot{m}''$. - [x] **Solid surface fires:** classified by thermal regime using $\delta_{th}$ vs $\tau_s$. - [x] **De Ris (thick, opposed):** $V_s = \lambda_g (T_f - T_{ig})^2 / [\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_s)^2]$. - [x] **De Ris (thin, opposed):** $V_s = (1 / (T_{ig} - T_s)) \sqrt{2 \lambda_g k_s (T_f - T_{ig})^2 / (\rho_s c_s \tau_s)}$. - [x] **Concurrent flow is faster** than opposed flow by **1–2 orders of magnitude** in most real scenarios. - [x] **Upward vertical spread** (chimney effect) is the **fastest** configuration and the design-critical one for high-rise buildings. - [x] **Quench velocity** $u_\infty^{quench} \approx \sqrt{\alpha_g / t_{ig}}$ — above this, opposed-flow spread ceases. - [x] **Péclet number Pe = $V_s \delta / \alpha_s$** is the dimensionless group characterizing the relative importance of advective vs conductive heat transfer in the solid. - [x] **Real-world links:** ASTM E84 FSI, NBC India, IS 14435, FAA aircraft material certification, Rothermel forest-fire model, ASTM E1321 (LIFT apparatus) for $V_s$ measurement. - [x] **Always state assumptions** in derivations and **carry units** in numericals for full KTU marks.

topics.title: Heat Transfer in Compartment Fires - Fuels, Calorific Value, Fire Load, Heat Release Rate and Factors Controlling Heat Release Rate, Basic Heat Transfer Equations, Convective Heat Transfer in Fire, Radiative Heat Transfer in Fire
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer in Compartment Fires - Fuels, Calorific Value, Fire Load, Heat Release Rate and Factors Controlling Heat Release Rate, Basic Heat Transfer Equations, Convective Heat Transfer in Fire, Radiative Heat Transfer in Fire

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fuels in Fire Engineering

A **fuel** is any combustible material that, when combined with an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen) in the right proportion and exposed to a sufficient ignition source, undergoes an exothermic oxidation reaction releasing heat, light, flame, and combustion products. In the context of **compartment fires** (fires occurring within an enclosed space such as a room or building), fuels are broadly classified based on their physical state and chemical composition.

**Classification of Fuels (KTU Syllabus Standard):**

| Class | Examples | Physical State | Combustion Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solid Fuels | Wood, paper, coal, plastics, textiles | Solid | Pyrolysis → Flaming combustion |
| Liquid Fuels | Petrol, diesel, kerosene, alcohol | Liquid | Vaporisation → Flaming combustion |
| Gaseous Fuels | LPG, methane, propane, hydrogen | Gas | Diffusion/Premixed combustion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For compartment fire analysis, fuels are characterised by their **calorific value**, **mass burning rate**, and **arrangement geometry** (fuel package vs. fuel spread). The geometry strongly influences whether the fire is **fuel-controlled** or **ventilation-controlled**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a fuel in a compartment fire like food on a stove. A small candle flame is a *tiny portion of food* releasing energy slowly. A stack of wooden pallets is a *full buffet* that releases energy in a much larger "blast" once ignited. The *type* of food (carbohydrate-rich wood vs. fat-rich plastic) determines the *calories per gram* released, while the *quantity and arrangement* of food determine the *total energy burst*.

---

### 1.2 Calorific Value (CV) — Heating Value (HV)

The **calorific value** (also called **heat of combustion** or **heating value**) is the amount of heat energy released per unit mass (or unit volume for gases) of a fuel when it is completely burned and the products of combustion are cooled back to the initial temperature of the reactants.

**Two variants are used in fire engineering:**

- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV) / Higher Heating Value (HHV):** Heat released assuming all water vapour in the combustion products is condensed to liquid (latent heat of vaporisation is recovered).
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV) / Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Heat released assuming water remains as vapour (latent heat is *not* recovered). In real fires, NCV is the relevant value because flue gases escape hot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** The standard reference heat of combustion of **methane (CH₄)** is **55.5 MJ/kg**. The conversion factor is **1 MJ = 1000 kJ = 10⁶ J**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Calorific value is the *calorie content on a food label*. A 100 g chocolate bar (~2300 kJ/100g) is "energy-dense" like polyurethane foam (≈ 26,000 kJ/kg). Wood is more like rice (~15,000 kJ/kg). Knowing CV tells you *how much energy* is locked inside a fuel; you still need the oxidiser (oxygen) and the *ignition trigger* to release it.

---

### 1.3 Fire Load and Fire Load Density

The **Fire Load (Q)** is the total amount of heat energy that can be released if *all* combustible materials within a compartment are completely burned.

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of the $i$-th combustible item and $\Delta H_{c,i}$ is its calorific value.

The **Fire Load Density (q)** is the fire load per unit floor area:

$$q = \frac{Q}{A_f}$$

where $A_f$ is the floor area of the compartment (m²). Units: **MJ/m²** or **kJ/m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Standard:** Fire load density is used to classify compartment fire severity per the **Eurocode (EC1)** classification:
> - **Low:** q < 500 MJ/m²
> - **Medium:** 500 ≤ q < 1000 MJ/m²
> - **High:** 1000 ≤ q < 1500 MJ/m²
> - **Very High:** q ≥ 1500 MJ/m²

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Fire Load is like the *total calories in a pantry* — the absolute amount of "combustible energy" stored. Fire Load Density is the *calories per square metre* of the pantry floor — it tells you how *intense* a fire that pantry can produce. A warehouse packed with cardboard has a high fire load density, just as a candy shop has high sugar density per shelf area.

---

### 1.4 Heat Release Rate (HRR)

The **Heat Release Rate (HRR)**, denoted $\dot{Q}$ or HRR, is the rate at which heat is generated by a fire, expressed in **kilowatts (kW)** or **megawatts (MW)**. It is the single most important variable in fire engineering because nearly every fire-induced phenomenon (temperature, smoke production, toxic gas yield, structural response) is directly linked to HRR.

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_c$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass loss rate (kg/s), $\Delta H_c$ is the calorific value (kJ/kg), and $\eta_c$ is the combustion efficiency (typically 0.5–0.9 in fires; up to ~1.0 in ideal laboratory burners).

**The t²-Fire Growth Model (KTU Critical Concept):**

For design fires, the HRR is often approximated by a power-law growth curve:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$

where $\alpha$ is the fire growth coefficient (kW/s²). Standard fire growth categories per **NFPA / ISO 14520:**

| Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Reference Fuel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slow | 0.00293 | Heavy timber pallets |
| Medium | 0.01172 | Office furniture, wood |
| Fast | 0.0466 | PU foam, polyester |
| Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | Flammable liquids in cartons |

**Intuitive Analogy:**
HRR is the *power output* of the fire — like the wattage of a light bulb. A birthday candle is a 50 W bulb, a small wastebasket fire is a 1500 W space heater, a sofa fire is a 3000 W fire (3 kW), and a fully developed room fire is a 5000–10,000 W (5–10 MW) blast. *t² fires grow like a snowball rolling downhill* — small at first, then accelerating.

---

### 1.5 Heat Transfer in Compartment Fires — The Three Modes

Heat transfer within a fire compartment occurs via the three classical modes:

1. **Conduction** — Heat transfer through solids (walls, floor, ceiling) by molecular vibration and electron movement. Governed by **Fourier's Law.**
2. **Convection** — Heat transfer by bulk motion of fluids (hot smoke layer flowing under ceiling, fire plume rising). Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling.**
3. **Radiation** — Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves; does *not* require a medium. Governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** The **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m²·K⁴)}$. The **emissivity** of a sooty flame is typically **0.7 to 0.95**, while a luminous yellow flame can be **0.95 to 1.0**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Picture a hot cup of coffee in a closed room:
- The cup heats the *saucer* by **conduction** (direct contact).
- Steam rises and the *air* above the cup warms by **convection** (fluid motion).
- You *feel warmth on your face* even without touching the cup — that is **radiation** (invisible electromagnetic waves).

In a fire compartment, the same three modes act simultaneously, but **radiation and convection from the hot smoke layer** are dominant for heating structural elements, while the **fire plume** is the prime mover of convective transport.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Why and How of Heat Transfer in Fires

In a compartment fire, the burning fuel releases chemical energy. This energy does not stay put — it travels to surrounding surfaces and objects, raising their temperature, possibly causing ignition of secondary fuels (flashover), and ultimately threatening structural integrity. Understanding *how fast* and *by which mode* this energy moves is fundamental to designing safer buildings, sizing ventilation, and predicting fire growth.

**Step-by-step logical chain:**

1. **Fuel releases chemical energy as heat** during combustion (governed by calorific value).
2. **Hot combustion products (smoke) buoyantly rise** forming a plume and accumulate under the ceiling, creating a **hot smoke layer**.
3. **Energy is transferred from the smoke layer and flames to compartment boundaries** by:
   - **Convection** (hot gases in contact with cooler surfaces),
   - **Radiation** (flames and hot smoke emit IR to walls and other fuels),
   - **Conduction** (through wall, floor, ceiling materials).
4. **Boundary surfaces heat up.** If combustible linings (e.g., wooden panelling) reach their **ignition temperature**, they ignite, increasing the fire size.
5. **Feedback loop:** A larger fire produces more hot smoke → more heat transfer → more surface ignition → still larger fire. This runaway is called **flashover**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Flashover typically occurs when the **upper layer temperature reaches ~500–600 °C** and the **incident radiant heat flux on the floor reaches ~20 kW/m².**

---

### 2.2 The Three Modes — Operational Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Conduction (Fourier's Law)

Heat conducted through a material of thickness $L$, thermal conductivity $k$, with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\dot{q}'' = \frac{k \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L} \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$$

- $k$ (W/m·K) is the thermal conductivity.
- Higher $k$ → better conductor.
- For **transient heating** (more realistic in fires), the **thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ governs the rate of temperature penetration.

#### 2.2.2 Convection (Newton's Law of Cooling)

Heat transfer between a fluid (hot gas) and a surface:

$$\dot{q}''_c = h_c \cdot (T_g - T_s) \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$$

- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K).
- $T_g$ = gas temperature (K), $T_s$ = surface temperature (K).
- For fire-driven flows, $h_c$ typically ranges **5 to 50 W/m²·K** (natural convection) and can exceed **100 W/m²·K** in forced/plume-driven flows.

#### 2.2.3 Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

Net radiative heat flux from a surface 1 to surface 2 with view factor $F_{1 \to 2}$:

$$\dot{q}''_r = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_1^4 - T_2^4) \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$$

For a fire compartment, total **incident heat flux on a target** from a hot smoke layer (treated as a large grey enclosure):

$$\dot{q}''_r = \varepsilon_s \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{sl}^4 - T_s^4) \cdot F_{sl \to s}$$

where $\varepsilon_s$ is surface emissivity, $T_{sl}$ is smoke layer temperature, and $F$ is the view factor.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Vertical pipes $\vert$ are replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Always use this format in your answer sheets.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Fire Load | $Q = \sum m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}$ | $Q$ in MJ, $m$ in kg, $\Delta H_c$ in MJ/kg | Total energy available |
| 2 | Fire Load Density | $q = Q / A_f$ | $q$ in MJ/m² | Floor area basis |
| 3 | Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_c$ | $\dot{Q}$ in kW, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s | Most critical fire variable |
| 4 | t² Growth Model | $\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$ | $\alpha$ in kW/s² | Slow/Med/Fast/Ultra-Fast |
| 5 | Conduction (steady) | $\dot{q}'' = k \Delta T / L$ | W/m² | Through walls |
| 6 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha_t = k / (\rho c_p)$ | m²/s | Transient conduction |
| 7 | Convection | $\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_g - T_s)$ | W/m² | Smoke–surface |
| 8 | Radiation (grey body) | $\dot{q}''_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W/m² | Stefan-Boltzmann |
| 9 | View Factor Relation | $\sum_j F_{i \to j} = 1$ | dimensionless | Reciprocity: $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ |
| 10 | Hot Smoke Layer Temp (Heskestad/Zukoski) | $T_{sl} \approx T_a + \frac{\dot{Q}}{1.15 \sqrt{g} c_p \rho_\infty A^{3/2} T_a}$ | K | Empirical plume correlation |
| 11 | Plume Temp Rise (Heskestad) | $\Delta T_p = 9.1 \left(\frac{\dot{Q}^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}\right)$ | K | Centreline plume |
| 12 | Fire Plume Mass Flow | $\dot{m}_p = 0.071 \dot{Q}^{1/3} z^{5/3}$ | kg/s | At height $z$ above fire |

**Real-world Utility:**

- **Fire Protection Engineers** use HRR to size smoke detectors (activation time ∝ 1/HRR), sprinklers, and ventilation.
- **Structural Engineers** use the incident heat flux on structural steel to compute the **time-to-collapse** of columns and beams.
- **Building Designers** use fire load density to determine required fire resistance ratings of walls, floors, and doors (e.g., 1-hr, 2-hr, 4-hr ratings).

---

### 2.4 Factors Controlling Heat Release Rate

The HRR of a fire is not constant. It depends on multiple interacting factors. **KTU classifies them into three groups:**

1. **Fuel-controlled factors** (depend on the fuel itself):
   - **Chemical composition** (e.g., polyurethane releases 2× the HRR per kg of wood).
   - **Mass and arrangement** (loose vs. compact, porous vs. solid).
   - **Surface area exposed** (higher surface area → higher pyrolysis rate → higher HRR).
   - **Moisture content** (water absorbs heat in vaporisation, slowing HRR growth).
2. **Ventilation-controlled factors** (depend on air supply):
   - **Opening size and location** (windows, doors, vents).
   - **Oxygen concentration** in the compartment.
   - **Height of the neutral plane** (interface between inflowing fresh air and outflowing smoke).
3. **Geometric / environmental factors:**
   - **Compartment size and ceiling height** (affects plume entrainment and smoke layer).
   - **Thermal properties of walls** (heavy walls absorb heat → lower HRR; light walls reradiate → higher HRR).
   - **External wind pressure** (affects ventilation flow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A fire is **fuel-controlled** when sufficient air is available and HRR depends on fuel mass loss rate. A fire becomes **ventilation-controlled** when oxygen is starved; HRR is then limited by the rate of air inflow.

**Ventilation-controlled HRR (Law/Steckler correlation):**

$$\dot{Q}_{vc} = 1500 \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{H_o}$$

where $A_o$ is the opening area (m²) and $H_o$ is the opening height (m). Units: $\dot{Q}$ in kW.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation Law (Conceptual)

The Stefan-Boltzmann law is *not* derived from first principles in a fire engineering course — it comes from integrating the **Planck spectral distribution** of blackbody radiation over all wavelengths and over a hemisphere. We present the conceptual steps:

**Step 1: Planck's spectral distribution** (energy per unit area, per unit wavelength, per unit time, per unit solid angle):

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1}$$

where $h$ is Planck's constant, $c$ is the speed of light, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $\lambda$ is wavelength, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Step 2: Integrate** over all wavelengths $\lambda \in (0, \infty)$ and over the **hemispherical solid angle** ($2\pi$ sr). The result, after evaluating the definite integral, is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_0^\infty \int_{hemisphere} E_\lambda \, d\Omega \, d\lambda \\
  &= \int_0^\infty \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1} \cdot \pi \, d\lambda \\
  &= \sigma T^4
\end{aligned}$$

where the Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) emerges from the integral constants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students are not expected to reproduce Planck's derivation. They must *state*, *identify*, and *apply* the Stefan-Boltzmann law with proper units and emissivity correction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Net Radiative Heat Exchange Between Two Surfaces

**Setup:** Two infinite parallel grey surfaces, surface 1 at $T_1$ and surface 2 at $T_2$, with emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, separated by a transparent medium.

**Step 1: Energy leaving surface 1** (emitted + reflected):

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) E_{2 \to 1}$$

where $J_1$ is the radiosity (total leaving flux), and $E_{2 \to 1}$ is the irradiation from surface 2 onto surface 1.

**Step 2: Energy leaving surface 2** (similarly):

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) E_{1 \to 2}$$

**Step 3: For infinite parallel plates**, $E_{2 \to 1} = J_2$ and $E_{1 \to 2} = J_1$ (each sees only the other).

**Step 4: Solve simultaneously.** From the first equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
J_1 - (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2 &= \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \\
J_2 - (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1 &= \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying the first by $\varepsilon_2$, the second by $(1-\varepsilon_1)$, adding and using algebra, the **net heat flux from 1 to 2** is:

$$\boxed{\dot{q}''_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** If both surfaces are **blackbodies** ($\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$), the formula reduces to $\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — the simple Stefan-Boltzmann difference.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient for Fire Plume Impingement

**Setup:** A fire plume impinges on a flat ceiling. The convective heat transfer coefficient at the ceiling, $h_c$, depends on the local plume velocity and the temperature difference.

**Step 1: Apply the empirical **Alpert correlation** for ceiling jet temperature:**

$$\Delta T_{max} - \Delta T_g = \frac{16.9 \dot{Q}^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}} \quad \text{[°C]}$$

where $H$ is the ceiling height (m), $\dot{Q}$ in kW, valid for the **first impact** on the ceiling.

**Step 2: Use the dimensionless correlation** for $h_c$ for ceiling impingement:

$$h_c = C \cdot \left(\frac{k_g}{H}\right) \cdot Re^n$$

For fire plumes, empirical fits yield $h_c$ in the range **5 to 20 W/m²·K** at the centreline and **3 to 10 W/m²·K** radially outward.

**Step 3: Convective heat flux on ceiling** (Newton's Law of Cooling):

$$\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_{g,c} - T_s)$$

where $T_{g,c}$ is the ceiling jet gas temperature and $T_s$ is the ceiling surface temperature.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Modes in a Compartment Fire

| Property | Conduction | Convection | Radiation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Medium required | Yes (solid) | Yes (fluid) | No (vacuum OK) |
| Mechanism | Molecular vibration | Bulk fluid motion | Electromagnetic waves |
| Driving force | Temperature gradient | Temperature + density | $T^4$ difference |
| Key law | Fourier | Newton's cooling | Stefan-Boltzmann |
| Dominates in fire for | Wall penetration | Plume to ceiling | Flame to fuel |
| Typical magnitude (room fire) | 5–20 kW/m² (into wall) | 5–30 kW/m² (smoke to wall) | 20–150 kW/m² (flame to fuel) |
| Temperature dependence | Linear | Linear | Quartic ($T^4$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Because radiation scales as $T^4$, it becomes the *dominant* heat transfer mode once flame temperatures reach **>1000 K (~727 °C)**. This is why **flashover** is so rapid — radiative feedback ignites distant fuels almost instantaneously.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Total Heat Flux on a Wall in a Compartment Fire

**Problem:** A compartment has a hot smoke layer at 800 °C and a wall surface at 40 °C. The wall emissivity is 0.9, smoke layer emissivity is 0.7, and the view factor from smoke layer to wall is 0.5. The convective coefficient is 15 W/m²·K. Find the **total incident heat flux** on the wall.

**Solution:**

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
- $T_{sl} = 800 + 273 = 1073$ K
- $T_s = 40 + 273 = 313$ K

**Step 1: Radiative flux**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}''_r &= \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot F \cdot (T_{sl}^4 - T_s^4) \\
&= 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5 \times (1073^4 - 313^4)
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $1073^4$:
$$1073^2 = 1{,}151{,}329$$
$$1073^4 = (1{,}151{,}329)^2 = 1.326 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

Compute $313^4$ (negligible):
$$313^4 = 9.6 \times 10^9 \text{ K}^4$$

Subtract: $\approx 1.326 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$.

Now compute:
$$\dot{q}''_r = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5 \times 1.326 \times 10^{12}$$
$$= 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 0.5 \times 1.326 \times 10^{4}$$
$$= 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 0.5 \times 13260$$
$$= 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 6630$$
$$= 0.7 \times 37{,}592$$
$$\approx 26{,}315 \text{ W/m}^2 = 26.3 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Convective flux**

$$\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_{sl} - T_s) = 15 \times (1073 - 313) = 15 \times 760 = 11{,}400 \text{ W/m}^2 = 11.4 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Total**

$$\dot{q}''_{total} = 26.3 + 11.4 = 37.7 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This flux is well above the **20 kW/m² flashover threshold** and the **12.5 kW/m² ignition threshold** for cellulosic fuels. A fuel in this compartment will ignite rapidly and contribute to fire spread.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
