## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

Define **Calorific Value** and **Fire Load Density**. Mention the standard units used for each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Calorific Value (CV):** The amount of heat energy released per unit mass (or unit volume for gases) of a fuel when it undergoes complete combustion, with products returning to the initial temperature.
  - **GCV (HHV):** Latent heat of water condensation recovered.
  - **NCV (LHV):** Latent heat *not* recovered (relevant to fires).
  - **Units:** kJ/kg (solids/liquids) or kJ/m³ (gases).

- **Fire Load Density (q):** The total fire load of a compartment per unit floor area.
  - **Formula:** $q = Q / A_f$
  - **Units:** MJ/m².

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

List the **three modes of heat transfer** and state the fundamental law governing each.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Governing Law | Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Conduction | Fourier's Law | $\dot{q}'' = -k \, dT/dx$ |
| Convection | Newton's Law of Cooling | $\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_g - T_s)$ |
| Radiation | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $\dot{q}''_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T^4 - T_\infty^4)$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **t² fire growth model** in detail. List the four standard fire growth categories with their $\alpha$ values and reference fuels. A medium-growth fire is ignited in a compartment. Compute the HRR at $t = 60$ s, $t = 120$ s, and $t = 300$ s.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **Fire Load** and **Fire Load Density**. A small office has 50 kg of wood furniture (CV = 17 MJ/kg) and 5 kg of PVC cable insulation (CV = 25 MJ/kg). The floor area is 20 m². Calculate the fire load density and classify the compartment per Eurocode.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) t² Fire Growth Model:**

The t² model assumes HRR grows quadratically with time:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$$

**Categories (NFPA 72 / ISO 14520):**

| Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Reference Fuel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slow | 0.00293 | Heavy timber |
| Medium | 0.01172 | Office wood furniture |
| Fast | 0.0466 | PU foam |
| Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | Flammable liquids |

**For medium growth** ($\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}(60) &= 0.01172 \times 60^2 = 0.01172 \times 3600 = 42.2 \text{ kW} \\
\dot{Q}(120) &= 0.01172 \times 120^2 = 0.01172 \times 14400 = 168.8 \text{ kW} \\
\dot{Q}(300) &= 0.01172 \times 300^2 = 0.01172 \times 90000 = 1054.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** At 5 minutes, the fire is releasing over 1 MW — well into the dangerous growth zone.

**(b) Fire Load Calculation:**

**Step 1: Total Fire Load**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{wood} &= m_1 \times \Delta H_{c1} = 50 \times 17 = 850 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{PVC} &= m_2 \times \Delta H_{c2} = 5 \times 25 = 125 \text{ MJ} \\
Q_{total} &= 850 + 125 = 975 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Fire Load Density**

$$q = \frac{Q_{total}}{A_f} = \frac{975}{20} = 48.75 \text{ MJ/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Eurocode Classification**

- Low: < 500 MJ/m² ✓
- This office falls in the **Low fire load category**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **net radiative heat flux** between two infinite parallel grey surfaces, stating all assumptions. Apply the result to two surfaces at 1000 K and 300 K with $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.6$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A compartment fire has a hot smoke layer at 700 °C and a wall at 25 °C. The convective heat transfer coefficient is 20 W/m²·K. The smoke layer emissivity is 0.85, the wall emissivity is 0.9, and the view factor is 0.6. Calculate (i) the radiative heat flux, (ii) the convective heat flux, and (iii) the total heat flux on the wall. Comment on whether flashover is likely.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** (as in Section 3.2 above):

The two-surface energy balance gives:

$$\boxed{\dot{q}''_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}}$$

**Numerical application:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 &= \frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.6} - 1 \\
&= 1.25 + 1.667 - 1 \\
&= 1.917
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1^4 - T_2^4 &= 1000^4 - 300^4 \\
&= 10^{12} - 8.1 \times 10^9 \\
&\approx 9.92 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}''_{1 \to 2} &= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.92 \times 10^{11}}{1.917} \\
&= \frac{56{,}237}{1.917} \\
&\approx 29{,}335 \text{ W/m}^2 \\
&\approx 29.3 \text{ kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Heat Flux Calculation:**

Convert temperatures: $T_{sl} = 700 + 273 = 973$ K; $T_s = 25 + 273 = 298$ K.

**(i) Radiative flux:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}''_r &= \varepsilon_{sl} \cdot \varepsilon_s \cdot F \cdot \sigma (T_{sl}^4 - T_s^4) / [\text{appropriate form}] \\
\end{aligned}$$

Using the parallel-plate grey-surface formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}''_r &= \frac{\sigma (T_{sl}^4 - T_s^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{sl}} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1} \cdot F \\
&= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (973^4 - 298^4)}{\frac{1}{0.85} + \frac{1}{0.9} - 1} \times 0.6
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $973^4 \approx 8.96 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴, $298^4 \approx 7.88 \times 10^9$ K⁴. Difference $\approx 8.88 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴.

Denominator: $1/0.85 + 1/0.9 - 1 = 1.176 + 1.111 - 1 = 1.287$.

$$\dot{q}''_r = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.88 \times 10^{11}}{1.287} \times 0.6 = \frac{50{,}350}{1.287} \times 0.6 = 39{,}121 \times 0.6 \approx 23{,}473 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 23.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**(ii) Convective flux:**

$$\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_{sl} - T_s) = 20 \times (973 - 298) = 20 \times 675 = 13{,}500 \text{ W/m}^2 = 13.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**(iii) Total flux:**

$$\dot{q}''_{total} = 23.5 + 13.5 = 37.0 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Comment:** The total flux of 37 kW/m² **exceeds the flashover threshold (~20 kW/m²)**. **Flashover is highly likely.** The compartment's contents will ignite almost simultaneously, leading to full-room involvement and a peak HRR possibly exceeding several MW.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Forgetting to convert °C to **Kelvin** before applying $T^4$. Marks lost: 2.
> 2. **Emissivity misuse:** Treating the smoke layer and wall as blackbodies ($\varepsilon = 1$). Marks lost: 1–2.
> 3. **View factor omission:** Failing to include the view factor $F$ in radiation problems. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Forgetting to add convection and radiation** when asked for "total" heat flux. Marks lost: 2.
> 5. **t² model misuse:** Using the wrong $\alpha$ value for the growth category. Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **No interpretation/conclusion:** Numerical answers without a final comment lose the last 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Calorific Value (CV)** = heat per unit mass of fuel; use **NCV** for fire calculations.
- ✅ **Fire Load (Q)** = total heat energy of all combustibles in compartment (MJ).
- ✅ **Fire Load Density (q)** = $Q / A_f$ in **MJ/m²**; Eurocode categories: Low < 500, Med < 1000, High < 1500, Very High ≥ 1500.
- ✅ **HRR (Q-dot)** = mass loss rate × CV × combustion efficiency; the **most important** fire variable.
- ✅ **t² fire growth:** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$; remember $\alpha$ values: 0.00293 (Slow), 0.01172 (Med), 0.0466 (Fast), 0.1876 (Ultra-Fast).
- ✅ **Fourier's Law** for conduction: $\dot{q}'' = k \Delta T / L$ — works for **steady** state; for **transient** use thermal diffusivity $\alpha_t = k/(\rho c_p)$.
- ✅ **Newton's Law of Cooling** for convection: $\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_g - T_s)$; $h_c$ ≈ 5–50 W/m²·K in fires.
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** for radiation: $\dot{q}''_r = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$; remember $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) and **always use Kelvin**.
- ✅ **Two-surface grey-body formula:** $\dot{q}'' = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) / (1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1)$.
- ✅ **View factor identity:** $\sum_j F_{i \to j} = 1$.
- ✅ **HRR controlling factors:** Fuel-controlled (mass, surface, composition, moisture) vs. Ventilation-controlled (openings, O₂).
- ✅ **Ventilation-limited HRR:** $\dot{Q}_{vc} = 1500 A_o \sqrt{H_o}$.
- ✅ **Flashover** at ~500–600 °C upper layer temperature OR ~20 kW/m² incident heat flux on floor.
- ✅ **Radiation dominates** when T > 1000 K because of $T^4$ dependence — *the* reason flashover is sudden.
- ✅ **Plume centreline temperature (Heskestad):** $\Delta T_p = 9.1 \dot{Q}^{2/3} / z^{5/3}$.
- ✅ **Smoke layer temperature** depends on compartment size, HRR, and ceiling height.
- ✅ **Combustion efficiency $\eta_c$** is typically 0.5–0.9 in real fires, ~1.0 in ideal burners.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always **restate the law** before plugging in numbers.
> 2. Always **convert units** (°C → K, mm → m, g → kg).
> 3. Always **state assumptions** (grey body, infinite parallel surfaces, steady state).
> 4. Always **comment on results** in context (above/below threshold, fire severity).
> 5. Draw **neat diagrams** wherever possible — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation from Hot Gases and Non-luminous Flames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT302 - FIRE ENGINEERING FUNDAMENTALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation from Hot Gases and Non-luminous Flames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Radiation from Hot Gases** is the process by which gaseous combustion products (primarily carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water vapor (H₂O)) emit thermal electromagnetic radiation during fire scenarios. Unlike solid surfaces, gases emit and absorb radiation only within **discrete spectral bands** (wavelength intervals) rather than over a continuous spectrum. This phenomenon governs the radiative heat transfer in **non-luminous flames**, where soot particle concentration is negligible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Locked Terminology):** A **non-luminous flame** is a combustion flame whose yellow/sooty component is absent — i.e., it emits thermal radiation *only* through its gaseous combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, and minor species like SO₂). The radiative energy is concentrated in the **infrared (IR) region** between 1 µm and 20 µm.

The mathematical descriptor of gas radiation capability is **total emissivity (ε_g)**, which depends on the **gas temperature (T_g)**, the **partial pressure of the radiating species (p)**, and the **mean beam length (L_m)** of the gas volume.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Prism vs. Flashlight" Analogy**
> Imagine two light sources side by side in a dark room:
> - **A glowing-hot metal bar (solid radiator)** = emits light in *every* color uniformly, like a **flashlight beam** — a continuous spectrum.
> - **A hot gas cloud (gas radiator)** = emits light in *only a few* specific colors, like a **prism splitting a flashlight into isolated bands** — a banded, selective spectrum.
>
> This is why CO₂ has bright emission bands near **2.7 µm, 4.3 µm, and 15 µm**, and H₂O emits near **2.7 µm, 6.3 µm, and beyond 12 µm**, while emitting *nothing* in between. In fire engineering, this means the radiative danger from a non-luminous flame is **wavelength-dependent** and can be partially blocked by IR-reflective surfaces or attenuated by even small amounts of water mist.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Board-Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | **σ** | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** | Mandatory |
| Speed of light | **c** | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | Reference |
| Planck's constant | **h** | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Reference |
| Boltzmann constant | **k_B** | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Reference |
| Universal gas constant | **R** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Combustion calcs |
| Mean beam length (sphere) | **L_m** | **0.65 D** (D = diameter) | Geometry |
| Mean beam length (infinite cyl.) | **L_m** | **0.95 D** (D = diameter) | Geometry |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Fire Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In compartment fires, **60–80% of total heat flux to surrounding surfaces** can come from gas radiation during the fully developed phase. KTU examiners routinely test whether students can:
> 1. Distinguish **non-luminous (gaseous)** from **luminous (sooty)** radiation.
> 2. Apply **Hottel's mean beam length** concept to irregular geometries.
> 3. Use **partial emissivity charts** for CO₂ and H₂O (Hottel charts).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Gases Behave Differently from Solids

### 2.1.1 Atomic-Level Origin of Selective Emission
- **Solids** have closely packed molecules, forming quasi-continuous energy bands → emission is **continuous in wavelength**.
- **Gases** have widely spaced molecules; transitions between rotational-vibrational quantum levels occur at **discrete wavelengths** → emission is **banded (selective)**.

### 2.1.2 Logical Breakdown of the Radiative Process in a Fire Plume
1. **Combustion** produces CO₂, H₂O, N₂, and trace gases at high temperature (T_g = 800–1500 K).
2. Molecules in excited rotational-vibrational states **spontaneously emit photons** as they return to lower energy levels.
3. Emission occurs **only in characteristic spectral bands** (e.g., CO₂ 4.3 µm band).
4. A cooler surface intersecting the beam path **absorbs** these photons (gray-surface approximation often used).
5. Net heat flux = emitted flux from gas − absorbed flux from surroundings.

## 2.2 The Beer-Lambert Law (Foundation of All Gas Radiation Math)

For a monochromatic beam of intensity $I_{\lambda,0}$ entering a gas layer of thickness $L$:

$$I_{\lambda}(L) = I_{\lambda,0} \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$$

where:
- $\kappa_{\lambda}$ = spectral absorption coefficient (m⁻¹·atm⁻¹)
- $p$ = partial pressure of radiating gas (atm)
- $L$ = geometric path length (m)

The **spectral transmissivity** of the gas:

$$\tau_{\lambda} = \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$$

The **spectral absorptivity** of the gas:

$$\alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \tau_{\lambda} = 1 - \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$$

## 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law Applied to Gases

For a gas in **thermal equilibrium** at temperature $T_g$:

$$\alpha_{\lambda}(T_g) = \varepsilon_{\lambda}(T_g)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Kirchhoff's law *strictly* applies only at the **same temperature**. In a fire, the gas (T_g) and surface (T_s) are at different temperatures, so a correction factor is needed:

$$\alpha(T_s, T_g) = \left(\frac{T_s}{T_g}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \varepsilon(T_g, pL)$$

## 2.4 Mean Beam Length — The Engineer's Equivalent Path

The **mean beam length $L_m$** is the *effective* path length a typical emitted photon travels before striking the enclosure wall. For a gas volume $V$ surrounded by a surface of area $A_s$:

$$L_m = 3.6 \cdot \frac{V}{A_s}$$

For simple geometries (Hottel):

| Geometry | $L_m$ |
|---|---|
| Sphere (diameter D) | $0.65 \, D$ |
| Infinite cylinder (diameter D) | $0.95 \, D$ |
| Infinite parallel plates (spacing D) | $1.8 \, D$ |
| Hemisphere radiating to center of base | $0.65 \, D$ |
| Cube (side L) | $0.6 \, L$ |
| Rectangular enclosure (L × W × H) | $\approx 2 \, \dfrac{L \, W \, H}{L \, W + L \, H + W \, H}$ |

## 2.5 Hottel's Empirical Correlations (KTU Board Favourite)

### 2.5.1 Edwards' Correlation for CO₂ Emissivity

For CO₂ at total pressure P (atm) and gas temperature T_g (K):

$$\varepsilon_{CO_2} = a \, (pL_m)^{b} \cdot \left(\frac{T_g}{1000}\right)^{c}$$

where $a$, $b$, $c$ are tabulated constants.

### 2.5.2 Hottel's Charts (Graphical Form)
- Plot of $\varepsilon_{CO_2}$ vs. $(T_g, p L_m)$
- Plot of $\varepsilon_{H_2O}$ vs. $(T_g, p L_m)$ with pressure correction factor $C_{H_2O}$ applied

### 2.5.3 Pressure & Temperature Correction Factor for H₂O

$$\varepsilon_{H_2O,\text{actual}} = C_{H_2O}(T_g, p L_m) \cdot \varepsilon_{H_2O,\text{chart}}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{\lambda} = \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$ | Spectral transmissivity (Beer-Lambert) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$ | Spectral absorptivity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L_m = 3.6 \dfrac{V}{A_s}$ | Mean beam length (general) | m |
| 4 | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon_g \sigma T_g^{4}$ | Emitted flux from gas | W/m² |
| 5 | $q_{net} = \sigma \left(T_g^{4} - T_s^{4}\right) \cdot F$ | Net heat transfer | W/m² |
| 6 | $\alpha(T_s) = \left(\dfrac{T_s}{T_g}\right)^{0.5} \varepsilon(T_g)$ | Cold-gas absorptivity correction | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{mix} = \sum_i a_{\varepsilon,i} \, \varepsilon_i$ | Weighted mixture emissivity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Blackbody emissive power (Stefan-Boltzmann) | W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) in any KTU exam answer table** — use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values if needed.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire compartment design:** Calculating ceiling jet radiative heating.
- **Sprinkler activation:** Gas radiation can pre-heat smoke detectors and sprinkler links.
- **Furnace & boiler design:** Heat transfer efficiency in industrial fired heaters.
- **Smoke logging:** Evaluating radiative cooling of smoke layers in atria.
- **Wildland-urban interface (WUI):** Non-luminous vs. luminous flame contribution to facade ignition.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Beer-Lambert Law from First Principles (Exhaustive)

### Step 1: Define the differential attenuation
Consider a thin gas slab of thickness $dx$. The reduction in monochromatic intensity is proportional to the local intensity, the gas concentration (proportional to partial pressure $p$), and the slab thickness:

$$dI_{\lambda} = -\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, I_{\lambda} \, dx$$

> *Logic:* The negative sign indicates attenuation. The constant of proportionality is the spectral absorption coefficient $\kappa_{\lambda}$ — a property of the gas molecule and wavelength.

### Step 2: Separate variables and integrate

$$\frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, dx$$

Integrating from the entry face (where $x = 0$, $I_{\lambda} = I_{\lambda,0}$) to the exit face (where $x = L$, $I_{\lambda} = I_{\lambda}(L)$):

$$\int_{I_{\lambda,0}}^{I_{\lambda}(L)} \frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integrals

$$\ln \left( \frac{I_{\lambda}(L)}{I_{\lambda,0}} \right) = -\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, L$$

### Step 4: Exponentiate both sides

$$\frac{I_{\lambda}(L)}{I_{\lambda,0}} = \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, L)$$

### Step 5: Final form of Beer-Lambert Law

$$I_{\lambda}(L) = I_{\lambda,0} \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, L) \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 6: Derive spectral transmissivity and absorptivity

$$\tau_{\lambda} = \frac{I_{\lambda}(L)}{I_{\lambda,0}} = \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, L)$$

For a non-reflecting gas (which is the standard assumption):

$$\alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \tau_{\lambda} = 1 - \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} \, p \, L) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Beam Length from a Radiation Network Argument

### Step 1: Define the problem
Consider an isothermal gas volume $V$ bounded by a surface of area $A_s$ at temperature $T_s$. The gas is at $T_g$.

### Step 2: Total emitted power from gas

$$Q_{emitted} = \varepsilon_g \, \sigma \, T_g^{4} \, A_s$$

> *Logic:* By definition, an emissivity-weighted blackbody emission over the bounding surface area gives the gas emission rate.

### Step 3: Total absorbed power at surface

For a surface of absorptivity $\alpha_s$ (≈ ε_s for diffuse-gray):

$$Q_{absorbed} = \alpha_s \int_{V} \int_{A_s} \frac{I_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T_g)}{r^2} \, dA \, dV$$

### Step 4: Introduce the volume integral equivalent
For an isothermal, uniform gas, the double integral reduces to:

$$Q_{absorbed} = 4 \, \kappa \, \sigma \, T_g^{4} \, V \cdot F_{avg}$$

where $F_{avg}$ is the mean view factor from a gas element to the wall.

### Step 5: Equate absorbed to emitted (radiative equilibrium assumption for definition)

$$\varepsilon_g \sigma T_g^{4} A_s = 4 \kappa \sigma T_g^{4} V \cdot F_{avg}$$

### Step 6: Solve for the effective path length $L_m$

Defining $L_m \equiv \dfrac{1}{\kappa F_{avg}}$ and combining with geometry:

$$L_m = \frac{4 \, V}{A_s \cdot \beta}$$

where $\beta$ is a geometry-dependent factor (≈ 1.1 for most shapes). The empirical **Hottel constant 3.6** is the best-fit multiplier for engineering practice:

$$L_m = 3.6 \, \frac{V}{A_s} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gas-to-Surface Net Radiation Heat Flux (Radiation Network)

### Step 1: Define a two-node radiation network
Node 1: Gas at $T_g$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_g$
Node 2: Surface at $T_s$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_s$

### Step 2: Apply series resistance analogy (enclosure network)

$$q_{net} = \frac{\sigma (T_g^{4} - T_s^{4})}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_g}{\varepsilon_g} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g \cdot F_{gs}} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s}}$$

For a gas fully enclosing the surface ($F_{gs} = 1$):

$$q_{net} = \frac{\sigma (T_g^{4} - T_s^{4})}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1}$$

### Step 3: For a small object inside a large gas volume
If $A_s \ll A_g$ (a small target in a vast fire plume):

$$q_{net} \approx \varepsilon_s \, \sigma \, T_g^{4} \quad \text{(surface-controlled)}$$

### Step 4: Substitute numerical example
For T_g = 1000 K, T_s = 400 K, ε_g = 0.2, ε_s = 0.9:

$$q_{net} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000^4 - 400^4)}{1/0.2 + 1/0.9 - 1}$$

$$= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10})}{5 + 1.111 - 1}$$

$$= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11}}{5.111}$$

$$= \frac{5.524 \times 10^{4}}{5.111} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

> *Engineering interpretation:* A non-luminous flame at 1000 K delivers **~10.8 kW/m²** to a 0.9-emissivity surface — well above the **10 kW/m² critical flux for sustained piloted ignition** of wood.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison — Luminous vs. Non-luminous Flames

| Parameter | Non-luminous Flame | Luminous Flame |
|---|---|---|
| Primary radiator | CO₂ + H₂O gas molecules | Soot particles (carbon clusters) |
| Spectral character | **Banded** (IR only) | **Near-continuous** (visible + IR) |
| Peak emission band | 4.3 µm (CO₂), 2.7/6.3 µm (H₂O) | Broadband, peak ~ 0.6–1.0 µm |
| Typical ε_g | 0.1 – 0.4 | 0.5 – 0.95 (with soot) |
| Fire colour | Blue (methanol, H₂, CO) | Yellow/orange (wood, heptane) |
| Soot volume fraction $f_v$ | $f_v \to 0$ | $f_v \approx 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| Luminous efficiency | $\approx 0$ | $0.1$ – $0.3$ |
| Heat transfer to surfaces | Moderate (IR-dominated) | High (visible + IR) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Mean Beam Length for a Compartment Fire

**Problem:** A rectangular room of dimensions $5 \, \text{m} \times 4 \, \text{m} \times 3 \, \text{m}$ (L × W × H) contains a hot gas layer. Calculate the mean beam length.

**Solution:**

$$V = 5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$A_s = 2(LW + LH + WH) = 2(20 + 15 + 12) = 94 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$L_m = 3.6 \times \frac{60}{94} = 3.6 \times 0.6383 \approx 2.30 \text{ m}$$

> *Alternative check using the parallel-plates limit:*
> For an L/W/H = 5/4/3 box, the empirical correlation:

$$L_m \approx 2 \frac{LWH}{LW + LH + WH} = 2 \times \frac{60}{47} \approx 2.55 \text{ m}$$

> *Conclusion:* Both methods give values in the range **2.3 – 2.6 m** — consistent with engineering expectations.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Governing Equations of Gas Radiation (Summary)

| # | Equation | Origin / Source | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{\lambda} = \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$ | Beer-Lambert | Monochromatic transmission |
| 2 | $\alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \tau_{\lambda}$ | Energy balance | Monochromatic absorption |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}(T_g)$ | Kirchhoff | Isothermal gas |
| 4 | $L_m = 3.6 V/A_s$ | Hottel empirical | Mean beam length |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{CO_2} = f(T_g, pL_m)$ | Hottel chart | CO₂ contribution |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{H_2O} = f(T_g, pL_m) \cdot C_{H_2O}$ | Hottel chart + correction | H₂O contribution |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{mix} = \sum a_{\varepsilon,i} \varepsilon_i$ | Weighted sum | Mixed gas emissivity |
| 8 | $q = \sigma \left( T_g^4 - T_s^4 \right) F$ | Stefan-Boltzmann | Net flux |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Mean Beam Length Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Q:** Define *mean beam length* ($L_m$). Why is it necessary to use a "mean" value rather than a single physical path length in gas radiation calculations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Mean beam length $L_m$ is the *effective average path length* a photon travels through an absorbing-emitting gas volume before striking the bounding surface. Mathematically: $L_m = 3.6 \, V/A_s$.
- **Why a mean (1 mark):** In a 3-D gas volume, individual photons emitted from different points travel path lengths ranging from near zero (surface gas) to the diagonal (corner gas). The *mean* beam length converts this complex distribution to a single equivalent 1-D slab thickness for use with Beer-Lambert and Hottel charts.

---

### Question A2: Selective Emission of Gases
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Q:** State the **Beer-Lambert law** for the absorption of monochromatic radiation by a gas. Why does gas radiation occur in *bands* rather than continuously?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Law (2 marks):** $I_{\lambda}(L) = I_{\lambda,0} \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$ where $I_{\lambda,0}$ is the incident intensity, $\kappa_{\lambda}$ is the spectral absorption coefficient, $p$ is partial pressure, and $L$ is path length.
- **Why bands (1 mark):** Gas molecules are widely spaced; radiative transitions occur only between discrete rotational-vibrational quantum energy levels, producing photons at *specific wavelengths* corresponding to the energy gaps (e.g., CO₂ → 2.7 µm, 4.3 µm, 15 µm).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — Total Gas Radiation Analysis

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Theoretical Framework

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]**
> **Q(a):** Derive the expression for the **spectral absorptivity of a gas** using the Beer-Lambert law. Hence, state the **Kirchhoff relation** for a gas in thermal equilibrium, and explain the *cold-gas correction factor* used when gas and surface temperatures differ.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Beer-Lambert derivation.
A beam of intensity $I_{\lambda,0}$ traverses a gas slab of thickness $dx$. The infinitesimal attenuation is:

$$dI_{\lambda} = -\kappa_{\lambda} p I_{\lambda} \, dx$$

Rearranging and integrating from $0$ to $L$:

$$\int_{I_{\lambda,0}}^{I_{\lambda}(L)} \frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -\kappa_{\lambda} p \int_0^L dx$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{I_{\lambda}(L)}{I_{\lambda,0}} \right) = -\kappa_{\lambda} p L$$

$$I_{\lambda}(L) = I_{\lambda,0} \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Spectral transmissivity and absorptivity.

$$\tau_{\lambda} = \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L) \quad ; \quad \alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \tau_{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Kirchhoff's law for gases.
At thermal equilibrium ($T_g = T_s$): $\alpha_{\lambda}(T_g) = \varepsilon_{\lambda}(T_g)$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Cold-gas correction.
When $T_s < T_g$, the absorbing gas column is at $T_s$ while emitting at $T_g$:

$$\alpha(T_s) = \left( \frac{T_s}{T_g} \right)^{0.5} \varepsilon(T_g, pL)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Significance.
The exponent 0.5 accounts for the temperature dependence of the line-broadening mechanisms (Doppler and collision) in the gas absorption coefficient.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Problem

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper]**
> **Q(b):** A non-luminous methane flame produces a flue gas at $T_g = 1100$ K. The gas column has a mean beam length of 2.5 m. The partial pressures are: $p_{CO_2} = 0.12$ atm, $p_{H_2O} = 0.18$ atm. Given the chart values $\varepsilon_{CO_2}(T_g, pL) = 0.18$ and $\varepsilon_{H_2O}(T_g, pL) = 0.22$ with pressure correction $C_{H_2O} = 0.92$, calculate:
> (i) The total gas emissivity.
> (ii) The emitted radiative heat flux (W/m²).
> (iii) The net flux incident on a small detector of emissivity 0.85 located at $T_s = 450$ K.
> (iv) The corrected gas absorptivity (cold-gas effect).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Total gas emissivity (weighted sum).

$$\varepsilon_{mix} = \varepsilon_{CO_2} + C_{H_2O} \cdot \varepsilon_{H_2O} - \Delta \varepsilon$$

Neglecting spectral overlap correction $\Delta \varepsilon$ (≈ 0.02):

$$\varepsilon_{mix} = 0.18 + (0.92 \times 0.22) - 0.02 = 0.18 + 0.2024 - 0.02 \approx 0.36$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Emitted radiative heat flux.

$$q_{emit} = \varepsilon_{mix} \cdot \sigma T_g^{4} = 0.36 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1100)^{4}$$

$$(1100)^{4} = 1.4641 \times 10^{12}$$

$$q_{emit} = 0.36 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.4641 \times 10^{12}$$

$$= 0.36 \times 8.3014 \times 10^{4} \approx 2.988 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 29.9 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Net flux to small detector (small body approximation, $A_{detector} \ll A_{gas}$):

$$q_{net} = \varepsilon_{detector} \cdot \sigma T_g^{4} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1100)^4$$

$$= 0.85 \times 8.30 \times 10^{4} \approx 7.06 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 70.6 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

> *Note:* Since the detector is small, the gas sees an effective cold black background; the detector's own emission $\varepsilon_{det} \sigma T_s^4$ is negligible compared to incident flux.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Cold-gas correction.

$$\alpha_{corr}(T_s) = \left( \frac{T_s}{T_g} \right)^{0.5} \varepsilon_{mix} = \left( \frac{450}{1100} \right)^{0.5} \times 0.36$$

$$= (0.4091)^{0.5} \times 0.36 = 0.6396 \times 0.36 \approx 0.230$$

> *Engineering comment:* A cold detector at 450 K absorbs only ~23% of the gas's radiative capacity — most energy is re-emitted or transmitted through cooler gas.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Hottel Charts & Flame Characterisation

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Theory of Non-luminous Flames

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
> **Q(a):** Explain the spectral characteristics of radiation from CO₂ and H₂O in a non-luminous flame. With the aid of a sketch (or table), describe the **Hottel emissivity charts** and the procedure for computing the total gas emissivity of a combustion gas mixture.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** CO₂ spectral bands.
- **2.7 µm** band (weak) — combination of symmetric and bending modes.
- **4.3 µm** band (strong) — asymmetric stretching mode; principal radiator.
- **15 µm** band (weak) — bending mode.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** H₂O spectral bands.
- **2.7 µm** band (strong) — combination band.
- **6.3 µm** band (strong) — bending mode.
- **12–25 µm** band (broad continuum) — pure rotational transitions.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Hottel chart axes.
- X-axis: partial-pressure × mean-beam-length product $(pL_m)$ in atm·m.
- Y-axis: gas emissivity $\varepsilon_g$ (dimensionless).
- Family of curves: one per gas temperature $T_g$ (range 300–2000 K).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Procedure for mixture emissivity.
1. Compute $L_m = 3.6 V/A_s$.
2. For each species i, compute $(p_i \cdot L_m)$.
3. Read $\varepsilon_i(T_g, p_i L_m)$ from the chart.
4. Apply pressure correction $C_{H_2O}$ for water vapor.
5. Sum contributions (with overlap correction if precision needed):
   $\varepsilon_{mix} = \varepsilon_{CO_2} + C_{H_2O} \varepsilon_{H_2O} - \Delta \varepsilon$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical on Compartment Fire Geometry

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **Q(b):** A fire compartment has dimensions $6 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$. The hot gas layer (non-luminous) is at $T_g = 1200$ K with $p_{CO_2} = 0.10$ atm and $p_{H_2O} = 0.15$ atm. From Hottel charts: $\varepsilon_{CO_2} = 0.20$, $\varepsilon_{H_2O} = 0.25$, $C_{H_2O} = 0.95$, $\Delta\varepsilon = 0.03$. Calculate the total gas emissivity and the **net radiation** to a 1 m² steel surface of emissivity 0.7 at $T_s = 500$ K that fully encloses the gas.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Mean beam length.
$V = 6 \times 5 \times 4 = 120$ m³
$A_s = 2(30 + 24 + 20) = 148$ m²
$L_m = 3.6 \times 120 / 148 = 2.92$ m

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Verify the product $pL_m$.
$p_{CO_2} L_m = 0.10 \times 2.92 = 0.292$ atm·m
$p_{H_2O} L_m = 0.15 \times 2.92 = 0.438$ atm·m

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Mixture emissivity.
$\varepsilon_{mix} = 0.20 + (0.95 \times 0.25) - 0.03 = 0.20 + 0.2375 - 0.03 = 0.4075 \approx 0.41$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Net heat transfer (enclosure network).
For gas fully enclosing the surface ($F_{gs} = 1$):

$$q_{net} = \frac{\sigma (T_g^{4} - T_s^{4})}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1}$$

Compute $T_g^{4} - T_s^{4}$:
$T_g^{4} = (1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}$ K⁴
$T_s^{4} = (500)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}$ K⁴
$\Delta T^4 = 2.0111 \times 10^{12}$ K⁴

Numerator:
$\sigma \Delta T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0111 \times 10^{12} = 1.140 \times 10^{5}$ W/m²

Denominator:
$1/0.41 + 1/0.7 - 1 = 2.439 + 1.429 - 1 = 2.868$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final answer.
$q_{net} = 1.140 \times 10^{5} / 2.868 = 3.975 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 39.8 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Engineering interpretation.
This flux exceeds the **20 kW/m²** threshold for steel failure under sustained exposure — indicating a flashover-prone configuration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Gas Radiation Problems (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Pressure Correction Factor for H₂O** (-2 marks typical)
>    Students often use $\varepsilon_{H_2O}$ directly from the chart without applying $C_{H_2O}$. Always state: *"Apply Hottel's pressure correction, $C_{H_2O} = 0.92$ for this condition."*
>
> 2. **Using $L_m = V/A_s$ instead of $3.6 V/A_s$** (-1 to -2 marks)
>    The factor 3.6 is the Hottel empirical constant for *hemispherical* mean beam length. Use $L_m = 3.6 V/A_s$ unless the geometry is explicitly given.
>
> 3. **Conflating Luminous and Non-luminous Flame Emissivities** (-3 marks)
>    A non-luminous flame has $\varepsilon_g < 0.4$ typically. Values above 0.6 imply soot (luminous mode) and require the soot-volume-fraction approach, not the Hottel chart.
>
> 4. **Wrong Units in $pL_m$** (-1 mark)
>    Pressure must be in **atm** (not Pa, not bar) for Hottel charts. Path length in **m**.
>
> 5. **Neglecting the Overlap Correction $\Delta\varepsilon$** (-0.5 to -1 mark)
>    Always include $\Delta\varepsilon \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ for the spectral band overlap of CO₂ and H₂O around 2.7 µm.
>
> 6. **Incorrect Series Resistance for Enclosure Radiation** (-2 marks)
>    The denominator $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1$ applies *only* for $F_{gs} = 1$ (gas completely surrounds the surface). For other configurations, include the view factor $F_{gs}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam**

- **Gas radiation is *banded*, not continuous** — confined to specific IR wavelengths (CO₂: 2.7, 4.3, 15 µm; H₂O: 2.7, 6.3, 12–25 µm).
- **Non-luminous flames** = no soot = gaseous (CO₂ + H₂O) radiation only.
- **Luminous flames** = soot + gas = continuous + banded radiation; typically much higher emissivity.
- **Beer-Lambert law** is the foundation: $I_{\lambda}(L) = I_{\lambda,0} \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$.
- **Spectral absorptivity** = $1 - \exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$; **transmissivity** = $\exp(-\kappa_{\lambda} p L)$.
- **Kirchhoff's law** for gases: $\alpha_{\lambda}(T_g) = \varepsilon_{\lambda}(T_g)$ (at the *same* temperature).
- **Cold-gas correction**: $\alpha(T_s) = (T_s/T_g)^{0.5} \cdot \varepsilon(T_g)$.
- **Mean beam length** $L_m = 3.6 V/A_s$ (general), with $0.65 D$ for spheres, $0.95 D$ for infinite cylinders.
- **Hottel chart inputs**: $T_g$ (K) and $pL_m$ (atm·m).
- **Mixture emissivity**: $\varepsilon_{mix} = \varepsilon_{CO_2} + C_{H_2O} \cdot \varepsilon_{H_2O} - \Delta\varepsilon$.
- **Total emitted flux**: $q = \varepsilon_g \cdot \sigma T_g^{4}$ (use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)).
- **Enclosure network**: $q_{net} = \sigma (T_g^4 - T_s^4) / \left(1/\varepsilon_g + 1/\varepsilon_s - 1\right)$ when $F_{gs} = 1$.
- **Always apply pressure correction** $C_{H_2O}$ to water-vapor emissivity from Hottel charts.
- **Critical ignition flux** for piloted wood ignition ≈ 10–12 kW/m²; non-luminous flames can exceed this near 1000 K.
- **For small targets in large gas volumes**: $q_{net} \approx \varepsilon_s \sigma T_g^4$ (surface-controlled regime).
- **Three SI units to remember**: atm (for pressure in Hottel charts), m (for path length), K (for temperature).
- **KTU mark-distribution tip**: Always show the *units* in your final answer; 0.5 mark deduction per missing unit is common.
- **Be explicit about geometry assumption**: "Assuming a hemispherical radiation pattern" before writing $L_m = 3.6 V/A_s$.
- **Distinguish carefully** between *spectral* (per wavelength) and *total* (integrated over all wavelengths) properties.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
